The art teacher in our family took this document camera into her art class. No problems with set-up -- but, she says, "I should note that I had to have our tech person put the software on the laptop, since it's a work Mac. It is totally user-friendly. I could have plugged it straight into the overhead projector, but it's mounted pretty high on the wall." The only negative aspect would be that she found the camera quality to be less than hoped for. But she made some adjustments in the software, such as color balance and brightness, and it helped the overall look of the pictures. For something like a math lesson, it wouldn't be problematic. But for an art class, she had hoped for higher resolution. The camera also functions as an overhead projector, only without the need for the old clear sheets of plastic. You can put a book, a 3D object or do live demos on it. The camera orientation can swivel, which is handy because you don't have to flip a book around. It has a couple of features that the teacher found useful. The arm telescopes for a 10" x 12" viewing surface, it zooms in quite a bit, and there are some basic editing features such as adjusting the brightness and contrast. Another thing she really liked is that it lets you add text or draw on the image. Also, it records voice and video, so it can be used for posting demos to youtube. Overall, it's a good gadget that her students are using to blow up their small drawings onto a canvas. She says it's way easier than how they used to do it, by scanning and projecting.

I once saw them at K-Mart for a buck. Even at that price, it's too much. For starters, there's no external information screen when the phone is closed, just a small light to show if something's going on (received call or text). But that's not nearly the worst thing. The internal design of the SIM card slot is so bad, it's next to impossible to either insert or remove it. You have to use the small version of the SIM card, and the insertion point is right next to the edge of the battery compartment. What this means is that the card has to go in at a steep angle, and this causes the front edge of the card to actually go underneath one (or more) of four contacts and bend them back, which destroys the phone. If you can actually get the SIM card in without tearing up a contact and get the phone to work, well, there are zero features. It's a cellphone, and you can make/receive calls and texts, or browse the internet (why anyone would want to do that on a tiny screen is beyond me), but none of that stuff is user-friendly. In short, this is the absolute bottom feeder for a new cellphone. It's, quite literally, no different from a phone from twenty years ago, only much lower quality. It's the kind of phone they give away free to the homeless and, frankly, I'm not sure it's even up to the standards of the indigent. I must say, I am completely baffled by the nearly four-star rating of this phone and can only surmise that those high ratings are from people who have never owned a cellphone before. For them, maybe this thing is a revelation. But for anyone else who's had 'any' other cellphone, this thing just flat-out sucks.

This IQ treat ball is the third mind game I have purchased for my six month old puppy. For reference, she is a 6.5 pound Chorkie and I purchased the 3" ball and fill it with small breed puppy dry food. About this ball first,......it is the least expensive and most engaging of all three games that I have purchased. I will list the other two below. This ball takes her twenty minutes to eat the same amount of food that previously took her two minutes. Now, anyone with an active puppy knows the value of anything that will keep your puppy occupied for twenty minutes. The only thing previous to this ball to keep her occupied for this long was a brand new pool cue!!! Yes, I did say pool cue and brand new, .....and six pound puppy, lol. My husband says that's okay, the other dog owners in the park with their frisbee will have nothing on him when he takes that pool cue out and flings it and yells fetch and our little Chorkie picks it up and comes running. When I first got this ball, I took it apart to fill it,......big mistake!!! It took me forever to get it back together with food inside. Instead, I find it easier to just drop the pieces into the hole from the top and shake them down. Keep it screwed tightly, as it can pop open with all that action. The only reason I will take it apart now is for cleaning. Now, the other two games I bought for her were the Trixie treat tower (think doggy Kerplunk) and the Trixie level 2 flip board. Both are cute and fun for her, but she figured them out in five minutes and now can do them in less than two minutes. Nevertheless, they are fun for her and make her feel smart. If I could only afford one game, it would be this ball, as it keeps her busy for twenty minutes, 20 full minutes,......imagine what you can do with twenty minutes of freedom!!!!!

I was very excited, when Nintendo finally came out with another brand new Shadowgate game. This is completely different from the NES version, whereas the NES copied the PC version. This game takes a lot longer to finish, which was a big plus. The NES Shadowgate game was very fun, but not long enough for me. This game is even better then the NES version, as you get 360 degree play, and movement in any direction play, whereas the NES version was just 2-Dimension. Also, the game does have a lot of the NES ideas of paying attention to all your surroundings, as you very well find something you will need to use later. There are lots of puzzles in this game, and it makes you use your critical thinking skills, or you will die. The N-64 game takes the awesome Shadowgate game much farther, then the NES version, in that use don't mainly just stay inside a Castle, but you also go underground, outside the castle, etc, just wonderful. This is easily one of the best RPG's that i have ever played, and a must for Shadowgate die hard fans, or any RPG fan. Two thumbs way up, highly reccomend.

It's tax time, and as a small business owner, I am prepping paperwork for employers, my accountant, and myself. I have piles of receipts, invoices, tax forms, and miscellany to clip together. I used to use rubber bands and clips, but I have opted for staples since I dropped a giant box of paperwork in a parking lot and the clips and rubber bands didn't work. What a mess! This stapler is hand-held and holds together a ferocious number of papers. It's ergonomic enough so even with my very small hands, I don't have any trouble stapling big bunches of papers. It takes the regular size staples and is easy to fill--just pop open the mouth and insert the staples in a slide. Snap it together and it's ready to go. Great stapler. Of course! It's a Swingline.

I repeat, this is bristol board not copy paper!! It's thick and ever so slightly textured (like a business card but not quite as hard). If you're an artist on a budget I would highly suggest going for this rather than trying to make do with copy paper. It's only a little bit more expensive but the added thickness makes a world of difference. It's not quite as nice as easy to carry around as a dedicated sketchbook, it's a little stubborn about letting go of thicker pencil marks, and you will miss the sturdy cardboard back that comes with said sketchbooks. But, considering it's around half the cost I'm willing to take that trade. Student artists this should be your go to for practice! Don't waste money on those horrible toilet tissue thin "Sketch" pads. Get what the professor tells you for projects, but use this to hone your skills. Upgrade when you're ready to go pro.

Okay, so I am one of those people who has to have ALL THE PENS, correction tape, a Sharpie, and a highlighter on my desk at all times. My desk is roomy, but not THAT roomy - it gets cluttered. I was having trouble falling asleep a few nights ago, so I went shopping on Amazon for a small desk organizer, and I found this. It looks kind of on the small side online, but it's actually got quite a bit of space. The drawer in the front can fit quite a few of those paper clips with the metal thingies that fold down - what are those called? Anyway, I have a handful of them in there, in several different sizes. Since there are four cubbies to hold pens, etc, I am able to have one for Sharpies, one for my highlighter, one for the pens I use a lot, and one for my mechanical pencil and the pens I don't use quite so often. In the compartments on the top, I have one for my correction tape, one for my staple remover, one for paperclips (the regular kind) and one for Post-It notes. Everything is easily accessible and just as easy to put right back when I'm done. The best part about this is that at the end of the day, I can just pick the whole thing up and put it in a drawer or cabinet so no one steals my pens after I leave the office. :) My company would reimburse me for this expense if I turned in the receipt, but I don't want to - if anything should ever happen and I no longer work here, I want to be able to take this thing with me. I really do love it *that* much!

This is for the 2800 PSI 2.1 GPM version. I've never had a gas power washer, only electric, so I can't compare this to any other I've had experience with, this is coming from someone who only ever used the electric ones. My first impression was positive - this is FAR more solid than any of the electric ones I had, in just about every respect. Obviously the frame has to be tough enough to deal with the gas engine, but the wheels were far stronger as well. Every little thing on here is just "tough" compared to what I'm used to, including the metal spraying wand. Assembly was easy, but this is where they lost a star for me. First of all, the last couple gas powered items I purchased came with packages of oil that contained EXACTLY as much oil as it required. This comes with a really nice flexible pouch with an easy pour opening on it, but they warn not to overfill - and so you have to spend time pouring some, checking the level, pouring more, checking the level, etc... This is frustrating to me since they know EXACTLY how much oil will be required to fill it, how much was put in to run them during testing, how much was removed for shipping, etc... I shouldn't have to mess around like this until it hits the level. And yes, the pouch has too much oil in it so you can't just pour it all. My second complaint - there's a plastic hose out the bottom - think of something like the plastic hose for your refrigerator's ice maker or water dispenser. I can't figure out where it should store. The instructions specify that this is the siphon for soap. Fine, they also state "don't let it hit the muffler" for obvious reasons. But there's no where to PUT IT. It just drags on the ground. I don't know where it should "stow" and looking in the instructions, I didn't find anything showing where it should store away. Looking at the photos here on Amazon, I don't see it referenced at ALL or shown at all... Seriously, how hard would it be to put some clips somewhere to keep it stowed? Other than that, so far so good. I'm in Wisconsin so there's not much I can do as most days are below freezing, but we had a warm weekend and I was able to use this to clean my garage. It's considerably more noise than the electric ones, so I used my ear protection from my gun range bag while cleaning in there, but other than the noise, everything else was easy and worked better than I was used to. No electric cord to limit me, just the length of the hose - this is a major freedom for me. LOVE this side of things. Moving around is so much easier. Connections are more solid than what I'm used to with the electric one as well. Most of the electric ones I've had used small plastic couplers that screwed onto the end of the hose, then just quick disconnect / connect to the washer unit. I prefer this, a solid connector for the hose to screw in here. And screw connectors for the power washer as well. Plenty of power, I had to be careful because I have some poor spots in my garage that will split some concrete out and leave a little divot if I'm not paying attention, but everywhere else I pointed and sprayed, it worked great. The different nozzles are convenient, I'll have to play more to see how much, if at all, I use them, and one is specifically for detergent. All told, this is exceeding my expectations for use compared to the electric ones I've had.

My sweet girl was thrilled when I gave it to her and immediately wanted to play with it. That's when I noticed how much the toy shed. There was yellow fuzz in her mouth (no she's not a chewer) just from carrying it around. I inspected the toy and found the slightest tug on the fur made it come out. That's not safe for dogs to ingest should they swallow the fuzz or have it stuck in their throat. The Duckworth Duck is not good quality, poorly made. Please be careful! I felt bad taking the duck away from her as she loved it and is looking around for it. This duck will be going in the garbage and replaced with something different.

Bought these for my Abba Patio Rectangular Offset Cantilever Umbrella, and I'm glad I did - they do the job of keeping the umbrella stable. In my package, I received four empty plates along with a little plastic funnel. (Thanks for the funnel - I truly needed it for this!) The idea is to fill these things with wet sand so they're nice and heavy. I went to the big box hardware store and bought four ~11 lb. bags of washed play sand; one bag of sand fits nicely into each base plate. Helpful hint - put some water in the base plate BEFORE you start adding sand - it'll help spread the sand inside more evenly - and alternate adding sand and water until your sand is gone. Filled them, capped them, and slid them onto the umbrella base... into which they fit perfectly. I did not weigh them after filling them, but I'd guess each of the plates to weigh at least 25 lbs, if not more. Here is where my only real complaint is: the caps leak a little. I ended up sealing them shut, so no big deal, but I hope the plates hold up over time; I did a good job sealing on those caps, and I don't know that I'll ever be able to get them off again! :) I saw some people say in their reviews that they used flagstone to weigh their umbrella base as an alternative... which I guess looks okay, but I'm not sure that flagstone would be heavy enough. This particular umbrella is rather heavy and, in a decent breeze, has the potential to become a very efficient sail. I think these plates are the way to go.

Ive always wanted to play this game, but for a long time I had a DSL/3DS and couldn't justify the price of getting a PSP in addition to it. Of course, PSPs are old and cheap now that the Vita has come out, so I snagged a cheap system and a copy of Crisis Core to go with it (its just a pity that it was never released as a digital download for the PS3then I wouldve played it sooner!). Well, lets see how it stacks up after all this time! Graphics (score 9/10): I cant really compare this to other PSP games since this one is the first I have yet to play on the system, but these graphics look nice enough to me. Other than the Advent Children movie, this was the best chance of getting to see some of these iconic characters in higher resolution than the original FF7; and honestly, for a handheld, I have no complaints with the character models. I do wish some of the environments were more detailed, though. Music (score 8/10): Some of these tunes are reworked/based on original FF7 musical tracks, but many of them just aren't as memorable. However, I really like the normal battle/boss battle music, as it creates a nice feeling of excitement. The rest of the normal background music is pretty forgettable. Characters (score 9/10): Holy crud! Sephiroth!!! Sorry, let me start over. You play as Zack (who had a relatively minor but important role in FF7) before the events of FF7 - and you get to interact with Sephiroth! You also see Clouds early beginnings, hang out with Tseng, and meet a couple new characters. The new characters are interesting, too, but the highlight here (in my opinion) is getting to see what Sephy was like before he went completely crazy (spoilers?). Getting to know him here makes the events of FF7 feelreally sad. Story (score 8/10): Well, as mentioned previously, the story follows Zack in the events before FF7. Its quite interesting to see into the other side of the conflict in FF7 since you get to experience things from Shinras perspective. I always like game sequels/spinoffs where you get to see things from the side of the bad guys because you always end up learning that things aren't as cut and dry as youd imagine (unless the story/characters are really poorly written then they are just pure evil for no reason). I actually came to root for the people working for Shinra, many of whom honestly believe that they are doing good things In only wish things were a bit more fleshed out...and to be honest, the stuff with Genesis doesnt make a huge deal of sense. Battle System (score 7/10): I actually prefer turn based battle systems (more relaxing for me), but I had a lot of fun playing this game, too. The battle system here is a fascinating hybrid of an action game and a traditional RPG. Honestly, it reminded me most of Kingdom Hearts. Battles are random (sort of, they always seem to happen in larger areas and not in corridors). You run around freely (as Zack) on the battle field and smack things in the face with your sword, but you can use the trigger buttons to switch to various materia or items and use them in real time. You can dodge roll and block as well it really does remind me of Kingdom Hearts. There is one extra little twist, though the DMW system. This is reminiscent of a slot machine, and its constantly giving you stat buffs, limit breaks, and summons (or sometimes you get a bad combo and get nothing). Problem is, its completely random you don't even control where the spinning icons stop! Im not too keen on the random aspect of this, as a lot of limit breaks are utterly wasted, but honestly, I decided not to sweat it and just enjoy the game. Dont get me wrong, though, the DMW system is definitely a misstep in terms of game design, but its not a deal breaker. Gameplay (score 7/10): This is a bit of a catch-all category for me. The main focus of gameplay is combat, but there are a fair number of items to wrangle, NPCs to talk to, a materia crafting system (which I didn't use much), and a number of little side quests and extra missions (accessed via a save point) to do. The missions are important because they help you get leveled up and get better items, so don't skimp on them. In terms of exploration, though, the games not very interesting as most areas are small and bland (the missions are exceptionally bland, but thankfully short). It was honestly the story that kept me most engaged, and although the missions are not very exciting, they are short and I had fun obsessively marking them off my to-do list. I do have one complaint, though the leveling system. Apparently, you do accrue experience points and level up, but the experience point counter is INVISIBLE to the player, which means you cant really keep track of your progress towards the next level. Thats a shame, in my opinion. Overall (score 8/10): NOTE: this score is not an average; it's my subjective overall score. Hmmwell, if youve never heard of FF7 in your life, I cant imagine this game would hold much appeal for you, because honestly, there are better action games out there, and I don't think the gameplay alone is strong enough to keep you engaged. However, if you are a fan of FF7, then this game is a treasure trove of story tidbits, character backgrounds, fun cameos, and other lore-based interests. I suspect how much you enjoy Crisis Core will depend on which of those two camps (FF7 fan vs. action game fan) you fall into. Either way, I liked it - I just wished they had digitally released it to the PS3but hey, you cant have everything!

I bought this tin whistle as part of my experiment with wide bore and narrow bore instruments. This happens to be a narrow bore whistle as its tone is rather bright and sharp. I thought my Clarke Celtic D whistle was a narrow bore because of the tapered shape, but apparently that doesn't define the bore category. The Clarke whistle has a wider bore at the mouthpiece and it tapers toward the bottom, so the tone is rather rich and smooth and I read that the tapered shape helps reduce raspy sounds. This whistle isn't bad, but it does have some flaws. I found that the low D, low Cb, and high G tend to sound raspy; the high G being the most noticeable out of the three. Not sure if it differs from whistle to whistle on the production line, but this is my experience.

I got this because the typical drum feeders seem to not be able to dispense a small enough amount of food for a single betta without over feeding. It seemed to be straightforward and functional when I got it, with a ticking rotating dial that has pegs you can plug in at 3-hr intervals to push the food slot wheel over the opening. But the first Problem was that the food wheel does not move any faster for each of the slots to line up with the opening, and so it takes two hours to have a single bin line up with the food release opening completely. This is a problem for me, as all of the food pellets would lineup along the back edge and would not get released until the end of the two hours. OK... so I simply set the pegs Ahead three hours, so that food was dispensed by the time that I wanted it to be. But then, the pellets started not getting pushed by the food slot wheel at all, and stayed in one place underneath the plastic wells, which slid right over them. Well, this is a problem. The food was not getting dispensed at all. It seems that the small betta pellets were too small to be pushed forward by the wheel. I would think that this would be even more of a problem with food flakes. This purchase is going in the trash for me.

This unit is not aerodynamically made to properly perform in the wind, for optimum display. That is, it's heavier on one side, the side with the other two wheels. What happens, because of that, is that even a light wind will turn it around, with the two wheels facing away from the breeze. Then, all you see is the largest wheel, without the others being visible. If you go to the other side, those wheels won't be turning because the larger wheel is taking the full breeze. Very frustrating. So, HERE IS THE FIX: Take some gorilla tape and wrap it around the bottom part of the top pole section and the silver cylinder it slides into, going to the bottom section. That is where it rotates. You will freeze the pole rotation. Do this so all three spinners are most visible from the direction you most often view them from. The bad thing is that you will not see the other two wheels spinning from other directions BUT the good thing is you WILL see all spinners spinning from the direction you most favor. I like watching mine from my kitchen window. Recommended, with the fix.

Brown Rice grown in LA, or TX is usually grown on what had been cotton fields. Cotton was treated with arsenic to stop boll weivels. Unfortunately, arsenic is persistent and cumulative. Rice normally won't grow where arsenic is present; however, a variety was developed which can and is being grown on arsenic-contaminated soils. The arsenic accumulates most in the bran, and so BROWN rice has more than white rice (from which bran has been removed). Brown Rice syrup from rice grown on these soils will contain arsenic. Since India and Eqypt were not known to grow cotton with arsenic, their rice is considered safe.

Today I assembled this new cage for my three ferrets. They have been living in a cage meant for two for quite a while. Of course the cage was only meant for my girl, then i got her a sister a year later and got them both a brother a year after that. I knew for a while that they would need a bigger home but the problem is I couldn't seem to find one at a fair price or that didn't have overall bad reviews until I ran upon this item on Amazon. After reading both the positive and negative reviews I decieded to take the risk and purchase this item. The box came intact and all the items were wrapped and free from damage. I assembled this in about an hour but I had to backtrack once because the instructions are not very clear. Once I assembled the first level I realized I did so using the 2nd floor items so of course I had to backtrack. Luckily I have a power driver/drill with the allen wrench socket so it didn't take much time to correct my mistake. Good thing about assembling the 1st floor is that once you have completed that, the 2nd floor is the same process. Once assembled I added the ferrets three new beds (one hammock comes with the house). I made their 2nd floor their "living room/bedroom" with a litter-box and their 1st floor is their "kitchen" with food and water and of course another litter-box. My boy was able to figure out the stairs easily and quickly understood how to move about. He's the youngest of the three and the two others had to watch him over and over until they understood they could walk further up by using the stairs. Their home is now about triple the size of their old worn out house and I can definitely tell that they love it! I zip-tied all the stairs and plastic walkways so they could not be dislodged. ALSO it is nice to NOT have to purchase that expensive shredded bedding since the wire floor allows things to fall into the catch tray which slides out for easy cleaning! I love it! I gave four stars because the instructions while assembling the 1st floor and stand are not very clear but luckily the 2nd floor is the same process.

I'm posting this review after only using it for only a couple days because I didn't find the other reviews to be adequately informative. Here's what I think needs attention and clarification. The product description gives the impression that the screen is protected by a protruding edge while reviewers say that this is false, and whenever you lay the phone face-down, you are putting the screen directly on whatever surface you are using. What's actually happening is that there is a protective edge at the bottom that just barely just out past the screen, while their attempt at a protecting lip at the top is badly designed. The phone screen itself tapers and the case doesn't compensate for that. When you put the phone face-down, only the top edge of the screen touches the table surface. That means that the rest of the screen is very close to the surface, so any grit or sand on the table might scratch the screen. The ZeroLemon logo is a complete eyesore. This is really not designed to be a case. If you drop the phone, I expect you would be more likely to break the screen with this on versus without it. This is because the phone itself is very light, and a drop that causes an impact on the screen might not generate enough force to break the screen. However, this battery adds a significant amount of weight without adding screen protection, so any impact on the screen will be much more severe. Also, there doesn't seem to have been much thought put into the design of this to improve case performance. You just have an outer thin shell around the edge. This is better than nothing, but it's not very reassuring. On the plus side, the top half of the back of the case is just empty plastic, so it probably protects well for certain lucky drops, and the surface is grippy, so you're less likely to drop the phone. I also prefer having the phone with extra weight at the bottom. It adds to the grippy feeling. Because the battery is only in the bottom of the case, there is a lot of space left for the antennas to work without obstruction. This case doesn't seem to hurt signal strength. The cutout is also large, which allows you to appreciate the color of your phone if you got one with a nice color. The case is actually nicely designed as far as aesthetics go. There is some nice contouring at the bottom. It is probably as slim as possible given its capacity. Yes, this adds bulk and weight to your phone, but I was surprised by how light it is. The phone doesn't recognize it as a fast charger. As a battery, it works as expected. Overall, it's easy to recommend this as a battery but not as a case. Amazon sells similar battery cases which must be manufactured at the same plant in China since they appear identical. If I had it to do over again, I would buy one with less obnoxious branding. I wish I could remove that logo!

Had it out of the box and running in less than 10 minutes. Where the handle attaches to the main assembly was tricky to get aligned, and the small screws (while just to hold the bars in place) were inadequately small and 'whimpy' IMO. Assembling the handle sections would have been easier if the bolts were carriage to match the cutouts in the sections, instead I needed pliers to grip the heads. I'll probably replace these so I can fold down the handles more easily for storage. The extension cord keeper is a bit small for the gauge of cords they recommend, but is a nice touch. Also given the draw and gauges called for I'd prefer to see these Sun/Snow Joe products with a 3-prong plug b/c it's imperative to get them plugged in the right way. The height adjustment is easy and smooth to operate. Switching between the blade/tools is a breeze, they fit in positively so there's no question about alignment. The bag is a joke. It fills so quickly you're stopping every ~10 feet, and when you go to remove it most of the bagged materials fall out. Instead I recommend using it bagless and using a mower to pick up (added benefit of some mulching so the bagged material is denser). If it's your first time expect to need to take 2-3 passes between dethatching and picking up, but the results are worth it. The scarifier is effective as well, but if you're going through areas with patchy/thick grass it hangs up.

My husband has been wanting a paper shredder for forever, and when we got the opportunity to use this one, he was seriously like a kid in a toy store. He found everything in the house we needed to shred and he shredded it. But let me go back. The shredder came in a box that was inside a giant box. It was a bit awkward to carry up the stairs so make sure you have two people to lift it. It's basically assembled out of the box and even comes with an oil "cleaner" that you're supposed to use whenever the garbage area gets full and has to be dumped. The oil is supposed to lengthen the life of the shredder and you can purchase more online. We put all the junk mail through it, including the "credit card" offers that had just come through the mail. First of all, please be aware that reactions can happen to any animal and allergies can develop where previously there was none and some cats despise collars (seeing the bad review/stars by the person whose cat hated wearing a collar made me write this) . That being said I was hesitant to buy this for my dog and cats (obviously bought the dog the one intended for dogs). I was worried about the collar causing irritations and I've never known a flea collar that actually worked. Plus my cats have never worn collars, so I wasn't sure how they would respond. I live in southwest Florida, we have fleas year round because of the tropical weather and most fleas are resistant to the flea medications on the market. My cats are indoor cats (two) and my dog does not spend a lot of time outside, just out for walks. So for the past three years I have only treated the dog for fleas and all was fine, until last month when I found all three animals being bitten by fleas. I've had the dog on program, obviously this is no longer working. So I decided to try this collar for all of them. I was very surprised that within about 4 hours the itching was noticeably less. Two days later and I can say the animals seem to be flea free for the most part. Now I'm sure it will take a full cycle or two for all the fleas in the house to die off, but I am VERY happy with the results. I was nervous enough that the first day I put the collars on them I stayed home to watch for any bad reactions, and I check their necks daily to make sure they are ok, so far so good!

My one absolute requirement for a phone case is that it has to be less slippery than the bare metal of a phone with no case. That's the only reason I use a case. I don't drop phones, I don't sit on them, I don't do things that would damage them. Because I make sure I have something with better grip than the phone alone would have. After that fact, then every other benefit can be weighed and appreciated. But regardless of how good the case is, if it doesn't help make things less slippery, then it's a fail for me. This one was a fail. Sides are smooth and slippery, negating for me the entire point of having a case. It seems solid enough to survive a drop, but so do the other various iPhone X cases I have that have better grip and I can actually use... If they would've at least done some texturing or something on the sides, this may have been my go to case since I liked it otherwise. But so slippery, no point in using it...

I have been feeding my catfish these pellets. I never see them eat them, ever. I assume they are. My catfish have been in my tank for about 7 years now and have outlasted all of my fish and placostomus. I have to believe they are eating them and getting the nutrients that they need. The pellets sink quickly to the bottom where the fish are. I know the instructions say feed them twice a day, but I only do it once. I did find the description for the pellets interesting, as it provided information I did not know. ***Overfeeding, low palatability and low quality lead to unconsumed or poorly digested food which causes waste and raises ammonia levels. High levels of ammonia are toxic to fish and can cause gill damage, stress, illness and death. API Fish Food produces up to 30% less ammonia than leading competitor foods because they are made with highly palatable shrimp and nutritionally enhanced protein for maximum digestion. The more nutrients your fish digests, the less waste and ammonia. It is a complete and balanced diet for all bottom-feeding fish including Catfish, Plecostomus and Loaches.

I don't know how I missed it, but I didn't mean to order a toy with a squeaker in it. I'm working on pulling the mechanism out, but if I can't, it'll go in the trash. For those who don't know: the high pitched squeak in dog toys is actually there to mimic the sound of a small animal being killed. The sound excites dogs, and they then "kill" it over and over. This can stimulate the prey drive in every dog. I'm not saying every dog is going to begin hunting/killing small animals, but it can increase the excitement, and in dogs who already have a pretty strong drive, it could cause long term behavioral problems.

So far these inexpensive ink cartridges are giving me good print quality and seem to be lasting longer than HP branded cartridges. Even if they last less time than the branded ink it will still be worth it due to the price point difference. It is obvious that HP is making the majority of their money on their ink. Their printers are reasonably priced and work fairly well, but the ink is extremely expensive! I was constantly running out of black ink. I have an HP 7520 printer and frequently would run out of the photo black, despite the fact I never print photos. It was a chore to try and figure out which cartridge to buy (this printer has two black cartridges, one is regular sized and the other is much bigger, the issue is they aren't interchangeable and they would run out at different times and I would need to figure out which cartridge ran out). I'm happy I've tried OSIR and will continue to buy this ink as my printer had no difficulty recognizing the cartridges, they fit into the slots without difficulty, you can see how much ink is left in each cartridge (a huge difference from the branded cartridges), and the price can't be beat. In the meantime, I plan on also investigating refillable cartridges although with these inexpensive OSIR cartridges I'm not sure if it's worth it. I do recycle the cartridges when they're empty. I originally found these cartridges to be well worth money with good print quality. Unfortunately that was only until about 5 weeks of use. I mostly print in black and noticed that the print quality had degraded and was smearing and smudged, every time I printed. In fact, it's hard to read some of the printing. I cleaned the printer heads which didn't help at all. The quality of the color is also not very bright or true. I don't print that much but the printer is already notifying me I'm low on ink-this after purchasing the ink in early March. I'm changing my original 5 star rating to 3 stars (and could go down to 2 except they were inexpensive). What a disappointment to have this happen after thinking I had finally found ink that worked and was much less expensive than HP cartridges. I won't be buying this brand again.

They are edible, but not delicious to me, and I am unlikely to buy them again.

I want to like this mouse so badly. After 15 years they finally got the button locations right, moving "forward" back far enough that you can easily reach it (unlike the MX518/G400). And it's a beautiful, comfortable mouse, too. However, the sensor is a real problem. It's overly sensitive, to the point where if there are *any* irregularities on your mousing surface it will skip, or will read fast/slower depending on the texture. So you could move the mouse across the pad at an even pace, but it might speed up or slow down depending on slight variations on the pad. It was unusable on my older, worn mousepad and it's already unreliable on my new Razer mousepad that I got a few months ago. That shows no real signs of wear, but get a little dust or dirt on it and the mouse starts acting weird. My old Logitech mice would work reliably on almost ANY surface, even carpet if I was hooked up to a TV. So this is hardly something there is an excuse for. It also has a lot of little crevices on the bottom and I find myself having to clean lint and crap out of them frequently - again, something I never had to do with my old Logitech mice. I think for my next mouse I'll be leaving Logitech behind.

I am not a lover of heat but my husband is. Even he found it a bit hotter than he was comfortable with when he dipped the tip of a knife into the LITTLE jar to get a taste. The chili completely overpowers the taste of the honey which, primarily, provides texture and only a bit of taste initially. What we did was mix it some raw honey that I had on hand to increase the presence of honey in the mix and dilute the heat somewhat. I spread it over brie cheese and warmed it until the cheese was softened a little, and served it with crackers. Since dairy is the only thing that softens the effect of hot spices on the tongue, this was a good vehicle for using the hot honey. My husband and our heat-loving guests liked it. I have to confess that I was too chicken to try it. There is also the added benefit that the LITTLE jar will last longer if it's used as an ingredient rather than straight and by itself. Considering the price, that is a definite consideration.

Years ago when I got my first Kindle Fire, I bought a zipper case for it that had a built-in, wide and soft elastic strap for holding it while you read. It went from side to side, all the way across the tablet case. I still use this for reading my books, and it's never gone bad, saggy, loose, etc. How I wish they made a case like that for my iPad Pro 10.5. If they did I could read my books on it, instead of hauling both devices around when I go places. This grip is along that premise, but falls short in execution for something to hold a larger iPad Pro 10.5 - or to cover a wider hand. It does the job as far as sticking and supporting the device (so far), but the strap is too narrow, too stiff, and too tight for me to use for more than maybe 10-15 minutes. It's not wide enough to fit over the widest part of my hand, which is where it should fit, not across the fingers and middle knuckles. I don't have giant hands - men's medium gloves are loose on me - but this is seriously small and narrow. However it has given me the idea to make one of my own, and the materials I ordered should arrive tomorrow. An iPad Pro 10.5 is not a heavy tablet, but with a good case it has a bit of heft. I wouldn't glue a piece of cheap plastic directly onto a beautiful $700 tablet, so I attached it to my case. It seems to stick very well there, for those wondering about leather cases. They should come out with an appropriately sized one for the larger tablets.

After having this since day one I've come to the following conclusions: * Game is too big...meaning you can spend all day in the massive environment and not bump into any other players. And you spend WAY TOO MUCH MIND NUMBING TIME just trying to get from one place to the next...with no action in between other than constantly running between the map and the helm. * The quest for treasure is way more frustrating then it should be. The maps aren't detailed enough and you can spend, literally, hours searching. * If you do happen to run across other players you'll most likely be attacked because the other players have been running on sheer boredom (game brings the worst out of players). * Quests take hours (collect various kinds of chickens...when you go to "Chicken Isle" the chicken you need isn't there)...huh? *Customization is irrelevant. Spend $2500 on a new weapon...but it's no more powerful than the one you start with. *Customize your boat for $70,000...but the quest you just spent an hour on netted you $600??? Better use of time and money than this overhyped game.

Product is NOT easy to assemble. It says to "slide on" the chute onto the chute housing. There is a large lip on the chute housing, it is not possible to slide on the chute over a lip, the lip stops it. I tried loosening the bolts as much as possible to create a large gap, still did not work. I had to put the chute on then try to align the bolts and then tighten it down. Very labor intensive. In addition, the chute handle the turns the chute did NOT have the handle screw attached, it was laying in the box. We had to take the twist handle apart, and you can tell you are not supposed to because the screw holding down the handle was on so tight it was like a robot tightened it down. We finally got it off and put on the handle bolt (the picture in the manual showed it was already attached, which it wasn't). Some parts could have come pre-assembled, it looked like they were really trying to shrink the footprint for the container it is shipped in. Unit vibrates a LOT when using. In addtion, every time you engage the blades there is a very loud squel of metal on metal. When using the unit there is a lot of scrapping sounds. If you adjust the height of the unit off the pavement you can eliminate some scrapping noise but then it leaves behind a lot of snow. Pros: it throws snow VERY far and the unit is very powerful. Delivery company was AWESOME.

This is definitely the biggest power washer machine I have ever tried for home use. It weighs about 75 pounds, and I had to have help from the postman just getting it into my garage. Once I finally got it out of the box, I noticed that it had oil under the motor and on the frame. After doing some checking online I saw that other people were complaining of their units leaking, so I was hoping that was not the case with mine. So far, I have had the opportunity to use it once to wash down my driveway. I looked to see if there was an oil leak, but I did not notice anything. I think the oil from inside the box must have been the way it was shippedat least in my case. With a PSI range of 300 to 3300, I can do almost any type of washing I need to around my yard or house. It comes with a 30-foot hose, five different spray tips, and has different connections for you to control the PSI. It is very easy to move around because of the big rubber wheels, as is surprisingly easy to put together and setup with the oil and fuel. I was very pleased with how my driveway looked after washing it down, and I look forward to using this unit again. Recommended.

The Habitrail Mini Mushroom Oasis is designed for mice and dwarf hamsters, we have Robo Hamsters so I thought I would try this unique looking drinker out. This drinker is easy to fill and does not leak the way drip drinkers can and often do. This drinker has three small open surface access points for your mouse or hamster to drink by lapping or sipping the water without drips falling into the cage, or on the pet. It also looks very interesting and adds character to your mouse or dwarf hamster cage. When I made the review video, the hamsters had not accessed the water and sipped any yet, but they have since, so that was a relief to see them finally use it. Why four stars? Well, I took a star as I think the base could be wider for more stability and taller to raise the drinking openings higher for deeper litter. The sides of the base could be rougher textured so pets can park their front paws on it while drinking without constantly slipping off. Also, this drinker gets a tiny mark-down for cleaning access to the mushroom reservoir, I can see that getting dirty over time and you really can't clean all the interior angles. Overall I do recommend it as it is interesting as well as functional and I like the lack of dripping... definitely a design that could be built on and revised. One more tiny downside is that it has to sit on the bottom of the cage, taking up roaming space. Larger hamsters, gerbils, or rats would simply knock it over and be frustrated by the small accesses so definitely only get this for mice and dwarf hamster species. I would give it 4.5 stars if that had been an option.

I originally had just one of these, and I used it for months without an issue. I was keeping the temperature in my aquarium about 79 degrees - or so I thought. When I received another 2 pack, I put all three into the same aquarium to check them against eachother. I got 3 different readings in the same aquarium: 79.1, 82.4, 83.5. I attached a picture of the reading on the two newer ones. In addition to being poorly calibrated, the power button on both of the two new ones that I received did not work. These are cheap junk. Maybe three different readings with a range of 4.4 degrees is acceptable for some users, but it is not for me. I put an old fashioned mercury thermometer into the aquarium and found it was actually about 82 degrees. So the thermometer I had been using was off by 3 degrees, and none of these Zacro thermometers was calibrated. I threw all three in the trash, because I have no use for a digital thermometer that might be off by as much as 3 degrees. I'm going back to glass thermometers in my tanks.

This purchase was delivered as two separately wrapped 12-packs of 8 x 10 canvas panels. The canvas seems fine and is neatly wrapped and the gessso ground seems evenly applied. Of the remaining 19, 6 were questionably snug in at least one and sometimes two corners...I would be hesitant to frame them because cramming them into the frame so they fit would put pressure on corner joint(s) that can over time cause those joints to separate (I've had this happen before). So, 13 of 24 panels are perfect and problem free...I will use them for paintings destined to be framed and sold. I will use the inaccurately cut ones for studies or brushwork/color mixing practice work that will never see a frame. While I feel like the price is an okay value, I am only giving 3 stars because the product's inconsistent manufacture resulted in me having to do extra work to test and sort panels to avoid future problems with the ones that don't fit my standard sized off-the-shelf frames. I will probably not buy these panels again unless it was for a kid's workshop or something where framing wasn't a concern. I bought this at a LFS before reading any Amazon reviews.. Big mistake.. I wish I'd known it would turn a shiny white when in the tank and back under water.. if I had, I wouldn't have used as much or placed a dab on top of some roots. If you think it will remain clear as it looks right out of the tube you too will be mistaken.. now I'm wondering if I'll have to cover the glue shine on the base of some of my plants with java moss or something cause I don't believe I can paint over these shiny spots to get rid of that un-natural glare.. The Anubias Nana Petite plants themselves will take a long time to cover over these shiny spots.. Putting it on using the applicator and getting the plants to stick in place worked just fine. Its a gel and is not at all thin like a lot of Hobby store Super Clue which can b thin and very low viscosity.. This Gel is the right consistency for the job.. I'm amazed it works so well on two damp surfaces like it does. It probably can't be used that well while both are completely under water tho since the directions say to hold the plant in place for 20 seconds.. that's a very long time but I'm sure if you don't hold it at least 5 to 10 seconds the plant may fall off since the gel needs several seconds to set. CAUTION Don't get ANY on the tops of the leaves accidentally or they will be a shiny bright white too.. and after holding a plant in place that long, if any glue seeps thru and reaches your fingers, they'll get stuck too.. so my lesson learned is to use a lot less of it and expect it to look shiny bright and white not clear under you tanks LED lighting.

I've wanted to try da Vinci's travel brushes for years and finally bought a size 5. It arrived very poorly packed -- the capped brush was loose in a box with no protective packaging or filler and was wedged up under one of the bottom box flaps. If it had been a regular brush it would have been damaged. Thankfully, it was fine in no small part to the travel style's protective ruggedness. I'd take one star off for poor packaging except the error proved the brush was durable. I want to try the regular Cosmotops but will look elsewhere for a retailer who knows how to properly pack brushes. Off the bat I'm impressed with the spring of the brush. It holds a good amount of water though nowhere close to natural sable, squirrel, etc. Comparing it to my Cheap Joe's Legend Kolinsky the bristles are stiffer (they are synthetic, after all) but in a pleasantly responsive way. I like the durable black plastic handle setup better than the shiny brass the Legend and Versatil's have. I hope it holds it's point for a good while as it seems ideal to pair with my mini watercolor sketching kit. In reading reviews about problems with the points being splayed: for what it's worth, when quality brush manufacturers like da Vinci (i.e. W&N, Escoda, Princeton, etc.) ship brushes to retailers watercolor rounds are *always* starched to a crisp point and most non travel brushes are capped with protective plastic sleeve to protect the bristles. I see them fresh out of the box from those companies at our store. If out of the box your brush looks splayed and fails the water snap test something is definitely not right!

I've been struggling with fleas since moving to a new state. In the past, keeping my yard tidy and rubbing diatomaceous earth into my dog's coat worked well enough. But my new yard was a mess upon arrival, and so is the rest of the town. Lots of leaves and overgrown yards--everything fleas love(even in the dead of a VT winter). Oddly, the dog hasn't been the problem. I saw one flea on her, got out the old DE and she's been more or less fine since. But my cat, who doesn't even go outside, keeps getting them. It's only one or two at a time...not an infestation, but annoying. Both animals are older and have health concerns so I try to avoid the harsh chemicals. I decided to try Wondercide. We started with Lemongrass--my boyfriend finds the scent overwhelming, so on our second order we switched to Cedar which is much subtler and just kind of makes your house smell like Christmas, or firewood. It's helped keep the problem in check, but I haven't quite been able to completely rid the cat of them yet. The other day I saw one on the cat's belly and grabbed it off. The flea was still alive but hobbled on my desk so I sprayed it with the Wondercide that happened to be nearby. It died. So, it definitely works, but it takes real vigilance to stay on top of an on-going problem.

I bought this paper for making boats to float on a small pond. As a trial, we folded one sheet, added a burgee, and sent her off for a two day sail. She remained upright and intact during that time. We took her out of the water and she dried nicely, ready for another trip. She survived the second sailing without more than a few heavy stains from being constantly thrust against the rocks on the sides of the pond by the wind. The dirty areas remained waterproof and are still strong and she has kept her shape after drying out again. If you need waterproof paper for an origami project, I highly recommend this paper. It holds a crease, is not too thick, does not tear easily and is truly waterproof. I'm glad to know that it's recyclable, too.

I love chocolate, and especially dark chocolate. I don't like all the sugar that is present in the mainstream brands of baking chips, so for a long time, I had to bid a sad farewell to chocolate chips. And then... ...I found Lily's brand! It's sweetened with Stevia instead of table sugar or any kind of corn syrup. While I am not a huge fan of stevia as a stand-alone sweetener (like for coffee or tea), it works very well in the Lily's dark chocolate baking chips. My husband, who doesn't like the taste of stevia at all, and who is not a fan of alternative sweeteners in general, gives a two-thumbs up for these baking chips! I use these for making an alternative form of peanut butter cups. I melt the Lily's and spoon a little into the mini silicon molds (the ones about the size of a Reese's cup). I add some Teddy's peanut butter (peanuts only, no other ingredients) and then top it with another thin layer of the melted Lily's chocolate. I pop them into the freezer and then take one out a minute or two before I want to eat it. A great treat without all the added sugar! FYI -- if you have a Whole Foods near you, they are $7 a bag there.

This is a sturdy soft-sided carrier/crate that I used to housetrain my 12 lb Maltese puppy in. He also uses it in the car when we go to the vet or to the dog park, etc. As a crate, he loves it, sleeps in it, hangs out in it. It does tilt to the right side a little. I chose this carrier for * its generous dimensions - it's just a teensy bit short for my gigantic pup now to lay out fully, but there's plenty of width and height for him to turn around, curl up, chew on his toys... * being lightweight - before puppy weight * using as a crate for taking puppy to outdoor patios of restaurants and cafes - gives shade and ventilation (necessary in hot climates). The screens are the largest I have seen on any soft-sided carrier * using a portable bed in hotel rooms or friends' homes * its style and aesthetics My puppy may look sweet-faced, fluffy and gentle, but he's really a tasmanian devil, and he was even worse when he was 4 months old. However, he didn't manage to claw his way through the screens or chew open the zippers. And now he has finally stopped trying and just enjoys being in the carrier/crate, mostly. He does get car sick; so I will open the zipper on top and let him poke out his head a little bit. I have read to be careful about tethering onto the harness. My puppy moves around a lot, so I don't tether him, lest he accidentally strangles himself. Also, this carrier is too long and wide to fit into the foothold area of the passenger seat of a normal sedan car. So it gets strapped into the back seat. I crack open the windows for fresh air and put on some calming music, and lay down an old towel to catch any vomit. Under the black fleece and carrier base fabric, there is a stiff rectangle of cardboard (not waterproof) that cannot come out, so if it gets wet, it will expand and lose its shape.
My wife and I love them and our grandchildren are crazy about them.

Ok first off the best spider-man game ever. Could even be argued as the best super hero game ever. The game is amazing. Its so hard to put the game down as I find myself playing for 2 hours at a time and only stop because I have other things to do. The game's visuals are stunning. It looks and feels like New York City. The one thing I feared was....how would the web slinging work. From what I heard prior it was as real as possible mechanically. You cannot just shoot web. You need to be near something for it to work. Like in the comics. So if you are in the middle of central park hope for nearby trees but if you dive off the empire state building and the closest building is out of reach due to height you cant websling. But the game makes it very easy to figure it out as I have never lost someone in a chase. The gameplay i feel is like a combination of the Arkham games in terms of combat in fact very simular and assassin creed in terms of environment. Heck it even has eagle viewpoints which are radio towers in the game but act the same way with the map being blank till you do the tower for each area. The game has several side missions from the typical gang hangouts, to random crimes to saving the environment (not joking as spiderman does work in mini labs across the city and fixes pollution). The game does feature spiderman's supporting cast which are a staple of his mythology like aunt may, mary jane, harry osborn (through videos), and even a cameo from a very well known person. The story is so well done with spiderman reeling in the effects of the downfall of the Kingpin as a new villain tries to takeover. Along the way spiderman will encounter several villians including the sinister six. If you are a spiderman and/or a marvel fan...get this game. If you love video games...get this game. Its so good.

Well, actually I don't love it, but the cats do. My sister has a Bombay breed and a Maine Coon cat, both tend to be fairly big breeds and fairly big eaters. The Bombay is named George, a large and friendly jet black cat that just loves attention and The Coon is Thomas, a sneaky athletic cat that is starting to get very long, but still has a few more growing year. He loves to get at the food prompting it to be hidden away in a locked room. My sister calls the cats by a method she has dubbed "keying", she basically says a drawn out word "keeeeeeeeey" and the cats know from experience something good is there if they come. Sometimes cats are finicky as most cat owners know, but not for this stuff, George and Thomas were "keyed" and each given a portion of the bipack, Thomas inhaled his, George took his time to enjoy. After that my sister had left another unopened pack on the table, only to see Thomas try and tear into it until she chased him off. It now has to go in the locked room to keep it from being devoured in one night. Maine Coon cats can grow to four feet in length and 20-30 pounds, so I guess they'd be hungry. Bombay's can overeat and George is about 16 pounds, but at least at his age (about 8 or 9 years) he's not ripping open bags or packages of cat food for an extra feed. Thomas is about the same weight at less than two years, but still getting longer, after which he'll start to fill out, I figure he's gonna be in the 20 pound plus range and look like a bobcat. I think there was an incident in California, I read on the web where someone saw a giant Maine Coon cat in a bay window, and it was so big it was reported as an illegal wildcat. Anyway the cats say the food is good, at least the chicken flavor is, the no grain formula is healthy and provided the nutrients and protein an adult cat needs. I can't speak for the other flavors, but at least with two big cats it's a big winner. Recommended.

We have 4 cats: 2 girls are lovable, personable & mellow, 1 girl is skittish & anti-social... and we have 1 male who occasionally pees on furniture. He was the primary subject for the alleged calming affects this pheromone spray claims to offer (and we hoped the skittish female might see some improvement as well). We tried it based on our vet's recommendation. We also bought the Feliway room orderizer/plug-in & a Sargeants/Sentry 'calming' collar - hoping that any one or all of them would help. Unfortunately, I could detect NO difference in behavior when we used this spray on the blankets we place in our cat carriers whenever taking them to the vet; they still acted agitated & yowled while in transit to & from. Basically, none of these products worked on any of our cats: not the spay, not the collar nor to the room plug-in. For us, they were all a waste of money. This spray *might* work on your cat(s), but it didn't for ours. Obviously no company can guarantee 100% success. Purchase at your own risk.

Amazing game thus far, I just needed to add my two cents. I seldom play video games, mostly wait for a few select games a year to play (Last of Us 2!!) which is why I never upgraded to a PS4 Pro. I had read on how great this game is, and I was tempted to buy upgrade to a PS4 Pro to maximize the gaming experience and take advantage of my 65 in 2016 LG OLED TV. Long story short, from what I read, I was a bit disappointed that the PS4 Pro is unable to flawlessly play GOW at 60 FPS and at times even 30FPS in 4K. I'm sure there are other games that take more advantage of the PS4 Pro's abilities, but not GOW so much. More importantly than the 4K, I will say that ever since the first gen PS4 received an HDR update a while back, that this game looks AMAZING with HDR turned on. If you are fortunate to have a TV which can provide HDR (High Dynamic Range), you will truly enjoy the game a whole lot more. I can tell that there are some framerate issues at times depending what's onscreen, but overall mostly smooth from my 1st gen PS4 and nothing to make me have to go get a Pro. Truly, I can't even tell that this game is 1080p HD and not 4K on my TV which is great. In terms of the gameplay itself, I am extremely happy that they've taken the old recipe, annihilated it, and created something much more immersive and intricate. Highly recommended for any PS4 to play at least once.

I bought these in the mini size for a friend who said her chihuahua loved them and she DID love them. Only problem is the chews didn't love her. She had several episodes of vomiting after eating the chews, and resolved to give her no more than one a day. Then, when the vomiting continued, she resolved to give her no more than half of one per day. I also gave one to my four dogs. Two of them, who are 12 to 14 pounds did fine. The other two , who are 7 and 10 pounds, did not fare so well. The 10 pound dog had diarrhea. The 7 pound dog ended up hospitalized, with severe abdominal pain so intense she tried to bite when youd touch her stomach. She became completely anorexic. She resolved and began eating 18 hours later with supportive care, but X-rays showed some mineralization in her kidneys that was not visible on previous X-rays. (Im a veterinarian, so my dogs get X-rays and bloodwork whenever they're sick or hurt.) I am alarmed by the kidney mineralization. Remember the melamine crisis in pet foods? It caused kidney damage. Im not saying there is melamine in these treats, but there is something that is potentially dangerous and my own dog has mineralization in her kidneys that may have been caused by these treats. I think there is some sort of toxin that is dose-dependent. I cannot recommend this product and am reporting them to the FDA. If your dog has also had anorexia, vomiting or diarrhea after being fed these treats, please make a report to the FDA. You can also ask your veterinarian to help you do this. There is an FDA consumer complaint coordinator in every state.

Stuck in almost 10 years of development hell, I never thought this game would be released. Excited to finally get my hands on it, I dove in this past week and ended up binging it all the way through. The follow up to Shadow of Colossus and ICO has improved upon its predecessors in many ways in narration, graphics, and story. The interactions with Trico, your bird-dog friend, are incredibly believable and life-like. I've never felt such a strong attachment to a virtual animal before. They really nailed the animations and AI on Trico. Unfortunately, it also suffers from the same glaring issue in all 3 titles: the controls. The control scheme is so incredibly awkward and frustrating at times that it can really hold the game back. If you can power through it and train your brain to it it's not so bad but it does take some patience. Looking past the control issues, The Last Guardian is an incredibly unique game with a touching story filled with many jaw-dropping moments. It doesn't have the prettiest graphics or stable framerate but it makes up for it with life-like animations and an interesting story that starts off slow and goes out with a bang. One of the most unique games of this console generation. 8/10 Final Verdict: Looking past the controls, it's a unique slow-burn experience that requires dedication but pays off in the end with an unbelievably life-like attachment to a pixelated animal companion.

I am shocked by how well this stuff works! We have an overweight cat that has trouble grooming her back. She was so full of dandruff and greasy fur, I hated petting her, which made me very sad. We took her to the vet, and he thought it was allergies. We tried prednisone for a while, and it was clear she was less itchy, but the dandruff remained. The vet suggested different food. We already feed very high quality food, but she's been eating the same stuff for a long time, so we switched to a different flavor (different ingredients) in the same line, and there was no change. Our poor girl looked miserable. We decided to try a bath, but at 13 years old knew she wouldn't enjoy it. While looking for a shampoo for her, I came across this product and decided to try it before torturing her with a bath. After the first application there was a huge difference! After the second application, her dandruff is GONE. I'm dumbfounded. I dunno how, but it works wonders! She doesn't enjoy the sound of the spray, so it took two of us to get it on her, but by the second time she is getting more used to it and being a little more cooperative. We spray her down and then brush her. She is so soft and shiny! She finally looks healthy, and feels good to pet. I'm very thankful for this product. I don't have to be sad about my kitty anymore!

A frisbee made with West Paw's signature soft but durable zogoflex rubber should be a hit but hard edges make it a miss. The zisc is everything West Paw promises, it is durable and flies true. Unfortunately the very aspects of the design that make it such a great frisbee also make it a bad dog toy. Unlike other West Paw toys the zisc has some surprisingly sharp edges which can (and have) cut into a dog's mouth when being caught. I had to get rid of mine after I noticed that my 50 lb pit-collie mix was always cutting her gums on this toy. The damage was never significant but the cumulative effect of her cutting her gums open with this toy worries me enough to give it a miss in the future. We switched to West Paw's other frisbee the Air Dash and haven't looked back. The Dash is heavier and less aerodynamic so it doesn't fly as well as the zisc but it also doesn't damage my dog's mouth every time.

I bought one of these at the pet store. I like that it scoops and deposits into an odor-free bag that I can drop directly into the trash. I previously tried a couple of disposal systems, like Litter Locker, and found them to be smelly and difficult to remove and put in the trash when full. I have used small doggie poop bags when I didn't see brand replacements at the pet store. They worked fine and were much cheaper. It was awkward to use at first and required developing a different technique for scooping. I currently use crystal litter, so there aren't clumps, just solid waste. I can see that it wouldn't be satisfactory with clumping litter. I also have just one cat, though I don't clean the litter every day. Two of the tabs that hold the container to the scoop broke, so now it isn't very secure. May have been my fault and I am buying a replacement. I have only 2 minor complaints: It could have been more sturdy and the sifter part is a little too fine for crystal litter. It came with a stick-on holder for the little box of bags, but it got in the way. I finally found I could just leave the box inside the container, under the bag currently in use. It doesn't take much room and is convenient when I change bags. On technique: First, I make sure the bag is not blocking the door from swinging freely. I carefully ( to pick as little litter as possible) pick up any solids that are on or near the surface of the litter, sift and turn up on end so everything falls into the bottom of the bag. Waste can block the door from closing if is only part way down. Then I hold the scoop completely vertical to stir the litter and scrape the bottom, to mix the wet crystals with the dryer ones on the surface, stopping to pick up any more poops that turn up. This was the key for me. When I first used it, I held it the way I would a traditional scoop and all the litter went straight into the bag.

I bought the two pack of these hoping to make the kids a little more independent in the morning. No bueno! The design works well at first -- about as well as a full tube of tooth paste. But as the product is used up, the plastic container requires a harder and harder squeeze to get the next dose. Squeeze too hard and careful! A fat blob plops out. Unlike a regular tube, it's difficult to judge how much product remains inside -- the plastic exterior retains its shape and there's no telling which end to bang the container to get it to slosh to the tip. The second container's tip wasn't glued adequately to the container, so when removing or replacing the screw-on cap, the tip just spins and spins underneath. We broke open the first (empty) container to see how this thing works (you can press firmly on the bottom and it pops own; bending the tip aggressively will cause it to pop of the container). Inside is a very flimsy foil plastic bag. It's partially glued to the inside wall of the outer container. Ours had a big tear in it, and there was tooth paste smeared everywhere. Remember, this was our "empty" container! There was easily a week's worth of toothpaste left inside. My advice is to stick with the regular tube. Although we had fun opening this up to figure out how it works, it did not give my boys the additional independence I had hoped it would, but conversely provided me a little dose of frustration.

If you're would like a chatpad for your Xbox One but don't want to spend the $27 Microsoft is asking, I would advise you to steer clear of the Nyko Type Pad. My experience with this chatpad was horrible. Looking at the pictures, you wouldn't know that the Nyko's Type Pad comes with a USB that must be plugged into your Xbox. (This is in the item's description, but I didn't notice that) This was unexpected because the Xbox 360 chatpad just pluged into your controller. But before you say "cool! It's a mini keyboard", the Type Pad gets power from the controller. This means you can't plug the USB into your computer and use it there. The Type Pad also feels poorly made. It is cheap plastic, the keyboard is supposed to glow in the dark but doesnt, and is a pain to get in and out of the controller. In fact, it feels as if it wasn't designed for the Xbox One controller. As for the joystick on the top right, I don't know if it actually works. Perhaps it works on the Xbox One's web browser, but I haven't found anything else where it does. After my experience with the Nyko Type Pad, I wish I put that money to an official Microsoft Chatpad.

Comparing to A&E Sun Stop & Wick of the same size, in industrial sewing machines: This thread feeds worse, shreds easier, is less strong, and doesn't have any coating. I use this thread for indoor projects where the thread strength is not critical: overlocking, and decorative double-needle seams. In a domestic home sewing machine, this thread is probably a good, slightly stronger alternative to sewing with V-42. I would not use this thread for an outdoor project without first doing a true multi-year test of the UV resistance. I would not use this thread for any application where the failure of a stitch could mean damage to property or people.

Our rescue dogs (65lb Bulldog/Shepherd and 44lb Corgi/Lab) repeatedly suffer from allergies, especially food allergies as well as the occasional bout with upset stomach. They can also be a picky eaters when it comes to their treats, so finding the right ones can often be difficult. I always have reservations about trying new dog snacks as a large majority of popular brands are filled with terrible ingredients not to mention carrying a high price tag. I'm always willing to try a new brand so here are my thoughts on BIXBI Bark Pops All-Natural Dog Treats, Smoked Sausage, 4 oz; Pros + Smell and taste that my dogs absolutely loved! + Healthier ingredients compared to most treats on the market. + No artificial flavors or fillers. + Didn't upset my dogs sensitive stomachs. Cons - Super greasy and leaves a residue everywhere. - May cause upset stomach in more sensitive dogs. - Will get expensive if you have more than one dog and feed them regularly as treats. My dogs really enjoyed the BIXBI Bark Pops All-Natural Dog Treats, Smoked Sausage, 4 oz. They went through the bag quite quickly (as they do with most treats) and neither dog suffered from indigestion. My dogs found the smoked sausage flavor of these treats much more appealing compared to many other treats I've tried. My biggest gripe was the greasy and oily residue left behind after feeding them to your pets. I had to immediately wash my hands after every feeding and the oil took a bit to get off your fingers. However, if you're looking for a fun treat to feed your dogs you may want to give BIXBI Bark Pops All-Natural Dog Treats, Smoked Sausage, 4 oz a try. Just be aware if won't take your dogs long to wolf down these puffy morsels.

This is a phone that you are not afraid to take anywhere you go. It has voice and text, which is all you should need to stay in touch. Sound is fantastic, the volume exceeds any phone I have owned. There is not a touch screen or keyboard, so it takes some getting used to using the 4-way navigation key and T9 text entry from the feature-phone days of old. But once you re-learn T9, sending short messages is not difficult at all. Connectivity is 4G LTE and wifi. Works with my Project Fi SIM card (my phone has been GSM unlocked). I got a data-only second SIM from Project Fi and use that in my Nexus when I want to join the distracted. As a bonus, it runs Android 4.4 so you can install .apk applications through a USB debugging connection and ADB. I installed K-9 mail so that I can read emails. In settings/security enable "install apps from unknown sources". Dial *#*#2387#*#* and send button. You will see the developers options, scroll down and enable USB debugging. Connect to a PC with Android Platform Tools and install .apk apps. Permanently disabling the Push To Talk (PTT) Button: You need a T5 Torx screwdriver, spudger tool and a sharp knife. Warning: this will permanently disable the PTT Button forever. Do not do this unless you know for sure you will never use Push to Talk. Power off and remove the battery. Remove the 6 torx screws around the volume and PTT buttons on the left side of the phone. Use the spudger to pry off the button cover. Using a sharp knife, cut off the two silicone bumps that are behind the PTT button. Be careful not to damage any other part of the silicone. Reinstall the buttons and cover.

I'm not going to lie. I love jerky. If I could find a way to affordably live on it, I would, but for now it's a treat I indulge in from time to time when the craving gets too much to bear. Now, I'm not one for mechanically separated and pressed back together, "tender", and those things masquerading as meat/sausages as the gas station (mind you not all the fare at the gas station is bad, if you're careful). This stuff, however-- this is how jerky should be if I had the time, skill, and ability to make it myself. I should be able to see the muscle like it came off a piece of meat (obviously you say?). It should exercise my jaw (I mean come on that's part of the FUN!), and I should feel satisfied eating it. I should also enjoy the taste, and all these said things are a go. I really enjoyed this jerky. It's thin enough to rip with your teeth, tasty enough that I want to keep chewing on it, and it lasts longer because it takes more work to eat it (and that's a good thing, when you are like me and wonder where that little bag of jerky went after 5 minutes and you realized you inhaled it a little too fast). I would definitely purchase this again.. and again... and again... *eyedarts* Hell, I'd probably purchase a subscription if there was such a thing. Good stuff. Thumbs up.

A couple months ago, my cat started peeing on the bed occasionally (a new behavior) and having trouble using the litterbox. The vet thought my cat was feeling stressed and anxious (we had recently moved into a smaller apartment and I had been working a lot of long hours) and recommended trying the feliway diffuser. I was apprehensive at first but within a couple weeks I noticed that she was exhibiting a lot less stressed behaviors (returned to normal peeing, no more pee on the bed, less needy). Admittedly, this change in behavior coincided with other changes (I bought tons of enrichment for her, started working from home some days, etc) so I can't say with certainty that it was a result of the diffuser. However, I'm convinced enough that it helped that I'm continuing to use the diffuser. Also, I find that each diffuser refill lasts slightly over a month (about 6 weeks).

My nine year old golden retriever was constantly getting ear infections. We assumed she kept getting water in her ears from swimming in the pool, or the creek near our house, but even in the winter the ear infections persisted, and we couldn't figure out why. The cost for ear medicine and vet treatment was getting expensive and we were frustrated. We moved to a new state and started using a brand new vet. The new vet treated her ear infection and she asked us if we had ever tried using a dog food with very limited ingredients. We had been buying the same dog food since she was a year old, the Kirkland signature dog food from Costco. The vet said she thought our dog had developed an allergy to something in the Costco dog food. She recommended trying the Venison and Sweet Potato nature balance dog food to see if it helped. Our dog has been using this dog food exclusively for three months and her ears are better than ever. Zero ear infections!! We are so happy that our sweet dog finally has painfree ears. Yes this dog food is pricey, but we'll worth the cost in having a happy, healthy dog and less vet bills!
