I thought among the more experienced connoisseur reviews, there might also be some people out there like me who would benefit from a less refined but still specific perspective. E.g. Only a year or so into making your own grinds, not a particularly experienced palate, and with some health needs when it comes to purchasing coffee. I'm a weird duck. Everyone from doctors to researchers to popular coffee websites all seem to advocate that darker roasts are the way to go if you're an acid sufferer. But dark roasts have always, always made me sick. For days after drinking them - regardless of brand - I have horrible acid. For that reason, I've also come to personally dislike the intense smokiness that many darker roasts have. Lighter roasts, for whatever reason, contrary to the prevailing wisdom, don't affect me in the slightest. Maybe I have weird gut pH? Go figure lol. But I'm very pleased to say this coffee doesn't affect my stomach at all. I've been searching for a coffee, starting with medium roasts (and will soon work my way up towards very light roasts, and see how that goes) that is flavorful and aromatic in a way that isn't smoky or "roasty," but that is palpable and which doesn't hurt my stomach. And so far, this blend is a pretty good match for what I'm looking for. It's very mellow. It's also, despite my lack of experience, what I would say I think of when I envision a "true" medium roast. None of the beans are over-roasted. So many "medium" roasts can smell and taste like dark roasts to me, sometimes being even darker and "roastier" than other roasts labeled as dark! Not so here. Very, very mild, unless you extract for longer than 4 minutes. (I use a french press.) And even then, up to 6 minutes, it only becomes very moderately more "roasty." And darned if it doesn't faintly smell of blueberries as claimed, due to the Ethiopian Super Natural inclusion in this blend. It's not as present as a blend centered completely around that hint, but it's there, faintly but pleasingly. At 4 minutes extraction, with non-boiled but just beginning to steam water, it tastes like what most people would probably call "normal coffee." Mellow, mild, but with that great blueberry, earthy aroma. At 5 minutes it's definitely stronger and more flavorful, but still very mild. At 6 minutes, it's JUST beginning to taste "roasty" and a little more smoky, but still not bitter at all imo. If you love really dark roasted tasting coffee, this is not the blend for you. But if you just want a nice, smooth, mellow coffee, that also has decent body and a pleasing aroma (especially at longer extractions,) you might really enjoy this. The aftertaste is very mildly citrus, and doesn't "tingle" as much as other blends I've tried. I'm going to try some others, as I said moving up the roast lightness scale, but if none of them sit well with me, I could easily drink this as my daily coffee. No real downsides, not the most flavorful ever but definitely has presence and smells wonderful - although the aroma is as gentle and mellow as the flavor. I guess this is what people call "balanced" in the coffee world. If it is, I like it. Oh, and it has a bit less caffeine kick than some others I've tried. So at first I couldn't get this cat haven tree house tower to be stable. When I put it together it was completely wobbly as others had posted in their reviews. I tried loading the base with something heavy as one reviewer had suggested to no avail. I was frustrated beyond belief. I had my husband take a look at it and within 30 minutes he figured it out. Here is the missing piece of information no one has seemed to write into their review. The top piece platform that goes on last, the hole that you place the rod into actually has a screw thread in it. So in order to secure the whole tree as stable and not wobbly, without adding extra parts from the hardware store, you have to turn the top piece of platform to screw in the entire tree into place. You're virtually screwing the piece of platform on and as you twist it locks the other pieces and the center pole into place. Keep turning until it's all tight and locked into place. You won't need extra parts I promise. Now that it's assembled completely and stable, my cats LOVE it!!! I have 2 kitties and one of them is 20 lbs. He likes to jump on the top platform and survey his kingdom, and peek out from between the branches. Definitely get this for your cats! It's a great buy, comparable in price to other cat trees but it actually resembles a tree which makes it more appealing to both cat and owner.

I bought this for our small & toy dogs. I was not sure of the kibble size, and none of the other reviews mention it. Just so it's plain--it's not a tiny kibble, but it isn't a large one either. It's not all that different from other dog foods. When it arrived, and I saw the kibble size, I was not sure how our chihuahua, now aging and with the usual elderly chihuahua dental issues, was going to cope with this larger (for him) kibble. He had zero problem with it,and on the third day when it was time for kibble, he got unbelievably excited, something I had NEVER seen him do in the 7 years since we rescued him. Kibble is NOT his favorite,and excited over the kibble is unheard of from him. He loved it at first,but menu fatigue has once again entered the realm...and he isn't eating it as gleefully. Our doxie mix was rather blase about the whole thing. It's okay to her. Her biggest concern is Grandma's cats coming to steal her kibble,which does inspire her to eat it before they steal it. (That whole 3-4 kibbles is going to cause her to starve, you know.) She actually prefers Bella dog food over this, even if the ingredient list isn't as good. I would like the food better with a smaller kibble size. Seriously, what kind of pet parent with a large dog would buy a 4 lb. bag anyhow?? Even for us, it was a good size to sample a food before buying a larger bag, and I think back to when we had a large dog. The 4 lb. bag would have been 2 or 3 meals at the most! But the limited ingredients, including no poultry or poultry by products (big one for us) and no fish (I hate smelling that) meant that I have not had any incidents of vomiting. For me, that means no middle-of-the-night laundry too, since it is invariably the middle of the night AND the middle of the bed when one of these two get sick. (It is literally never in the middle of the day in the middle of the yard.) No upset tummies means a lot.

This PHILIPS SMOKE-LESS GRILL (plus Bonus Cleaning Tool, HD6371/98) is very expensive (about $280 on Amazon) for what it is. It cooks with two banks of infrared lights, but thats NOT why its smokeless. Its smokeless, more or less, because the drip tray is located well below the grill surface, and below the heat sources, so that grease drippings don't burn hard onto the drip pan (causing smoke). If youve ever broiled a steak in an electric oven, you know that most of the smoke comes from grease that is burning onto the broiler pan surface nearest the broiler coils. Thus, with this grill, youll still get some smoke if you cook fatty foods. My appliance didn't melt at the sides with first use, but there are a lot of things to NOT like about this appliance. First, the handles on the barbecue grid (the loose tray on top) get very hot at the four points where the metal joins the plastic handle insulation. Surprise for the unwary cook! Second, although theres a warning against staring at the infrared lights, its impossible NOT to look at them, because they're located right below the barbecue grid. You have to look down on them when you turn the food or check for doneness. Third, the grill takes 6-7 minutes to preheat, and it sends a lot of heat into your kitchen. You definitely don't want to use the appliance underneath your kitchen cabinets (danger of setting them on fire). My electric stoves broiler doesnt heat the kitchen as much. The appliance also takes quite a while to cool down so that it can be safely cleaned. Fourth, the appliance is big and awkward to handle. It takes up a lot of countertop or table space. Fifth, the drip tray slides out from the left end, so it needs a lot of space for removal. Sixth, the appliance isn't easy to clean, except for the barbecue grid and the drip tray (both are dishwasher safe). Grease spatters on the insides of the appliance, and must be scraped off. Otherwise, the grease will burn on and start to produce smoke. Finally, the scraper tool is just a paint scraper with double-edged razor blades (you get three blades). These are readily available at hardware stores. Im still scratching my head at the price. I wanted this because my electric ovens broiler produces some smoke, and because it burns grease onto the broiler pan so badly that I have to clean the pan with Easy-Off. But the broiler and broiler pan are much easier (and safer) to use and to clean than this grilling appliance.

I have been using this for about a month now before writing this review. On first impression I wondered why this was so expensive. It seemed simple enough of a product with albeit a somewhat strange look to it. I was not familiar with this type of device. Now that I have had some time with it, I can say that I do appreciate what it can do. It is one of those things that until you've learned the ins, outs and details of it that you might just write it off as marketing hype. There is a Foreo following, a community of users that appreciate, refine and push the boundaries of what a good skin care regime can be. I have not been an exfoliating user in the past. The most I have done is an occasional Olay Regenerist 2 part facial cleanser. There are quite a few bottles of partly used cleansing products on the bathroom closet shelf. I don't like the way most of them leave my skin feeling. This Foreo Face Brush has changed that. I have found that it can turn an ordinary or sub-par cleanser into a completely new experience. Cleaners that once did barely anything or left my skin feeling greasy, now leave it feeling clean, refreshed and tone. There is a complex micro-pulse technology to the vibrations that the Luna 2 creates that makes it more than first impressions reveal. After the first couple of uses I realized there is more to it. After using for a while and trying out different cleansers I have found that I makes just about all of them work better. I find it works best with the less abrasive ones. You can over do it. I have come to prefer a twice a week cleansing with it. That works for me. You can of course vary this. If you press more lightly and for a shorter duration, you can even use it daily. I would say my sessions tend to be on a medium scale of intensity for mid week and then the weekend session is a deep scrub. I have only been referring to it as a cleaner so far, but the marketing for men is as a pre-shave prep. In this regard I do find most of the marketing to meet the claims. I'm not sure that razor blades last up to twice as long, but it seems they do last longer. Perhaps they aren't having to cut thru as much dirt and whatever else unseen is on our faces. I'm not sure the reason for this. I can say it does give a better feeling shave and with less irritation. I'm tending to think this is because it really works the shave gel in and gets your skin hydrated well before the shave. It and warm water really make the shaving experience so much the better. You can use less shaving cream too. I find that you can use non-arisoled creams now as it tends to whip them to an airy consistency. I keep it in the shower on a higher up shelf. It is completely waterproof I think. I haven't had any problems with it in that respect. I find the USB rechargeable system to work very well. Everything is USB now a days and this works with my phone charger. Curiously mine only came with a cord and no charger. Not a big deal as I am changing out one bathroom vanity receptacle to feature 2 built-in USB charge ports on it. It is the one downline of the GFCI receptacle so it is shock protected. Going to add a few more around the house too. I have tried the massage feature a few times, although I must admit that I don't really find the time for it. If you are practicing a facial skin care program and are committed to it then I image it also has merit. I would say that eating an optimized diet and perhaps adding supplements would be a critical part of that as well. Don't forget stress management too. We are going thru an epidemic cold and flu season this year and I can say this really feels good on a stuffy face and sinus areas. I have found the time to use it for that. Overall this really has turned out to be more than I first thought it was. I am going to give it a 5 star rating because I really don't have any negatives to say about it. It is a bit pricey. I wouldn't have purchased it unless a friend or professional had really convinced me of it. All I can truly say is that everyone is different and results can vary. I do want to be thankful for the opportunity to try and be introduced to this as I would not have known about it otherwise. I guess my message would be that if you're already looking into this then it may be worth a try. I found it to not be just a gimmick or over hyped marketing.

The design flaw should have been caught by simply puttying the chart against the piano as a part of quality control before producing in any large quantities. If it was designed properly, the chart should have been about a half inch longer and it would not have increased the cost of the materials in any significant way. This product is a good idea somewhat lacking in attention to detail. It took me 10 seconds to put the product against the piano and find the problem with it. Please make version 2.0 of this chart because this product has a problem. I like the idea though and I was really excited about it until I try to use it.

I've tried a lot of different interfaces and mixers (Behringer's Xenex 1204, Presonus 44VSL, Alesis MultiMix 4 and 8 mixers amongst others) in my 10+ years as a podcaster and usually the biggest problem with nearly every single one has been not enough or noisy gain. The second problem has been trying to find an interface that would push out audio on to different tracks for post-editing. The third problem has been finding an interface that solves the first two problems but that didn't cost a fortune. The Behringer UMC404HD solved all three. Certainly not perfectly, but in that "Yes this will work" range. What I liked. The Midas (or kinda-Midas) pre-amps are clean enough for nearly every gain hungry dynamic microphone. I've tried it with their own XM8500, Shure's SM58, PG57, Heil's PR40, Electrovoice N/D767A, Sennheiser e835, and a few others and it didn't disappoint. I still use a Cloudlifter with my PR40 because, "hey I already had it so why not"? It's small enough to take on the road and can fit enough gear in a single backpack to record four different people with really good quality. You can monitor with nearly zero latency directly from the interface. All four outputs can go through USB to your DAW so if one track messes up, it won't affect the other (up to) three tracks. What I thought was just OK. The 2 track version (UMC202 and 204HD) of this interface could be powered by the USB connection from your computer, the 404HD has to be plugged into the wall. You can't adjust the sound of the outputs other than just gain. Out to a mixer or your DAW itself will have to handle those chores. Turning on Phantom Power (48Vdc) turns it on for all four outputs so if you have mic sensitive to that (like most ribbon mics), then you'll probably destroy them with this interface. Remember too that this outputs four mono tracks, NOT stereo. You'll need to set up your recording software or hardware to accept a mono source to get the input on both the right and left sides. Overall I've been very satisfied with the Behringer UMC44HD and highly recommend it even with it's few shortcomings because there's nothing else out there remotely close in this price range for what it does.

If you read my reviews, you know I have cats underfoot, cats that appear to spawn out of the ether and my wife and daughters seem to think they have always been there. Well one of these inter-dimensional creatures needed surgery and an extended recovery that meant no running or jumping or gnawing at his stitches or roughhousing with our other permanent 'visitors'. So I, got this. because that is what I do, I solve problems even when the problems are not of my making. This was a few seconds putting together. So I just clipped the two zipper pulls together at the bottom and that stopped that. He settled into his confinement for the next week and a half, with supervised visits outside while we did restocking, scooping and maintenance. At the end of the two weeks, he went to the vet for stitches removal and was free. Taking this down was 'a bit' trickier than putting it up, but a quick search on youtube showed me you have to take equidistant folds and make them meet in the center, then everything folds into itself after that. Nylon floor had a quick and easy sponge down and orange spray. Very easy cleanup. This is a genius invention. This is another 10 star product. I have a temperamental Siamese cat, Tony, that when things aren't quite to his liking, he has a behavior. Now these behaviors can be anything from urinating on clothing left on the floor, finding his favorite space in a closet and urinating, to pooping in the bathtub. Really?! That was my response when I first saw it. The funny part was I couldn't figure out why he didn't use the tub all of the time. It didn't seem to have anything to do with the cleanliness of the litter box or the number of litter boxes that I had in my house. Certainly when my daughter brought in a little male dog into the house a year and half ago, the problem seem to get worse. The now infamous battle between Pedro and Tony occurring and then when my boyfriend moved in, the poop started showing up on the floor. I was about to pull my hair out over my animals. I love them, but I didn't want my house smelling of dog and cat urine. I couldn't keep up with it. Everyday, I would check for poop and pee in the usual places, clean with every kind of cleaning product you can imagine to eliminate residue and smell. But it didn't matter. So in my search on Amazon, I found this product Angry Orange Pet Odor Eliminator. I read several reviews about how great it was and how it had stopped cats from urinating over and over in the same spot. Evidently, cats don't like the smell of oranges or citrus. Ah ha! I began to suspect that the reason Tony didn't poop in the bathtub all the time was the household cleaner my cleaning lady uses. Cat lovers you know there is nothing worse than cat urine. I was so frustrated with the situation, that I was even considering rehoming the cat that I had loved for last 9 years as well as the little dog. But my daughter persuaded me to continue my search. I decided after all the positive reviews about Angry Orange Pet Odor Eliminator, that I would try it. I mean what did I have to lose. In the process, I saw that others had also purchased Rocco & Roxie Professional Strength Stain and Odor Eliminator, so I purchased both. I followed the mixing directions on the bottle for the Angry Orange product and prepared my spray bottle to spray the bathtub. After cleaning the bathtub good. I first sprayed the Rocco and Roxie spray. Rinsed and allowed to dry. Then I sprayed the Angry Orange Pet Odor Eliminator, spraying a good amount because I really wanted this to work. Just as others have stated, it has a very strong orange/citrus smell. Very pleasant. And to my surprise, the cat hates it. He doesn't venture into the bathtub until several days without spraying. I now spray the bathtub every other day and I have had no repeat pooping. The cat, as is his nature, did find another spot to poop. :/ However, I cleaned the spot and sprayed it with the Angry Orange Pet Odor Eliminator and no more poop. He is now going in the litter box. I have also sprayed my carpet in my office with a light mist. My office now smells like an orange grove instead of a smelly dog kennel. I don't know this for a fact, but I don't think the dog like it either. I've noticed that Pedro has stopped marking as much, especially on his favorite spots. I'm not sure if this is a result of Rocco & Roxie product or Angry Orange product or both. Anyway, me, my boyfriend and the house are much happier. I highly recommend this product, especially if you have a temperamental cat like mine. Tami C.

Let's face it--cleaning the pool, even a small one, is a never-ending chore. Even doing a very thorough job does not prevent the next leaf, twig or insect from blowing in and making your pool dirty again. The 'right' way is to vacuum by attaching a hose to your suction line and sucking up all of the debris into your filter or into a collection bag. But vacuuming the pool is also time consuming and not particularly satisfying. Plus, as soon as you are finished, the leaves start blowing in again! These robotic cleaners come in several iterations: 'suction side' which connect to the suction side of your pool system (usually the skimmer or sometimes a dedicated suction line that you may have installed), 'pressure-side' which connect to a dedicated pressure line which almost always requires a booster pump be installed to provide the higher water pressure these devices require and 'electric robotic cleaners' which are usually self contained. An advantage of the electric robots is that they generally collect the debris into their own self contained filter bags rather than depositing it into your filtration system. An intermediate step is the rechargeable pool vacuum which attaches to the pole and allows you to spot clean debris as necessary.. These are $200 to $300 (Pool Blaster is an example) but I have been through 3 of these and while they are useful, they are not durable and ultimately, while a great idea, they are frustrating to use. I have had experience with all three systems in my 25 x 50 foot in-ground vinyl lined pool and I can tell you that they all have their disadvantages and advantages. None are perfect, most aren't even very good. The best you can hope for is a trade-off between a reasonably good cleaning job, product durability and price. If you have an unusual feature in your pool ( a swim lane or oddly shaped cove) many of these devices will simply get stuck and clean the same small patch endlessly until you haul them out. My experience with the Polaris robotic cleaner was mediocre at best. The unit is bulky and contains expensive electronic motors and circuit boards which will be left under water for hours at a time. The rest of the unit is plastic. The filter bag is delicate at best and expensive to replace. The rest of the parts are also expensive and these units are not designed to be easy to service by a homeowner. A previous version (the 9300xl) lasted just a few years before one of the wheels froze and was unrepairable--out went more than a thousand dollars of robotic cleaner. The Polaris was unable to figure out how to escape from my swim lane and would generally spend most of its time happily cruising back and forth over the same 50 foot patch--while debris accumulated elsewhere. Still, they all serve a purpose-- if nothing else, any robotic cleaner will disturb any debris that is left on the pool bottom and possibly allow the circulating currents to draw the debris into the existing pool filtration to be cleaned. But heavy debris (leaves after a storm, twigs or sand) will not be cleaned properly by any of these devices and will require you to haul out the vacuum head and the hose to do the job manually. My advice, after many years of experience is to buy the simplest and least expensive brand name suction side device that seems to fit your needs. One can be had for a little over $200 (the Barracuda G3 for example) and requires very little else to do the job. If it lasts two or three years, you have amortized it to about $70 per season-- about what it costs to have your pool cleaned a couple of times professionally. So long as you are aware that ALL of these devices are very limited (no matter what the price or what the manufacturer claims about its cleaning capabilities) and are comfortable with using a pole and skimmer net from time to time and willing to drag out the gold standard vacuum hose when needed, these devices may be able to spare you a few manual cleanings during the season. However, if your expectation is that you can ignore your pool while the plastic robot does the heavy lifting, such a device has not yet been invented.

I've been dealing with a major fungus gnat problem after foregoing soil sterilization on one of my indoor plants. Prior to ordering these I had been using other brands of yellow sticky traps and, while they are very sticky and will catch some gnats, I always noticed more gnats floating around nearby. I was having to cut them to the shape of the pot to get any effect. The blue traps on the other hand catch almost 50 gnats for every one that lands on the yellow trap (see attached picture). They actually seem to actively avoid the yellow traps as I've seen a number hover around them before slamming into the blue. I can't say how well these will work for thrisps or other insects but for fungus gnats they're amazing.

The sprayer which I received works as advertised. Specifically I experienced no leaks at all and it worked as expected without any issues at all. Having said that, I will agree that the unit is not a "professional grade" device, but then it is not priced like one either. As noted by others, I would probably pay twice this amount for a heavier unit with a larger capacity. This device is specifically designed to use premixed, concentrated LIQUID plant food, however you can use dry fertilizers if you dissolve them before hand. I had some how managed to accumulate several pounds of Miracle Grow plant fertilizer and wanted to begin using it on my rather large garden. Mixing a gallon at the time and hand applying was not an option, so I purchased this product. According to the Ortho Customer Service people, if one mixes 32 tablespoons of MG in a gallon of water and then set the Ortho unit to apply at a rate of 4 oz per gallon you will deliver 8 gallons of properly mixed fertilizer. I personally prefer to use the "weakly, weekly" method and cut down the amount of fertilizer to 1/4 the recommended amount and apply weekly. All in all, the Ortho unit is an inexpensive and handy little unit which works as advertised.

I gave this 4 stars and not 5 for a couple reasons. 1. I wish there was a way to adjust more distance from the wire for only certain parts of the yard, not just the whole thing. For instance, the part of my yard that faces the road, I would have set back further from the wire but the part of my yard that faces the woods I would have set closer. One setting works the entire fence, you cannot pick and choose which parts of your yard. 2. Where I have my flags set is where I want the strongest correction to be, because it goes in stages. The closer the dog gets to the wire, the harder is zaps. If it zapped hard at the moment she hits those flags, she'd have no interest in going any further. But she is learning that the flags mean bad things will happen. 3. I have a pitbull that weighs 58 pounds. This system goes to 7 strengths of the "zap". I had it on 5 and she ran right through it after a squirrel, did not even begin to stop at the flag. So I upped it to the highest which is 7. The next day, she got to the wire after she ran after a frisbee, and got zapped so hard that she actually yelped and has not even thought about going through since! If the frisbee goes over the flag, or if she sees a rabbit, she will chase until she hits those flags and then she stops! The manual said that only 7 percent of dogs need it on level 7, well I guess I got one of the 7%!

After seeing commercials for this game and how it boasted a more hack 'n' slash fun feel than a scary horror feel, I kind of took a pass on this game because I always assumed that the commercials were just making this game out to be a parody within itself. Years later, I saw this game for cheap at a local mom and pop store and decided to give it a try. I didn't really expect much from it, but it actually surprised me. Despite all of the negative reviews that popular gaming sites gave this game, I actually really enjoyed it. Though it is more of a hack 'n' slash type game than a scary horror game, there are some small jump scares and even some psychological horror elements to be had in this game, but nothing major that you would get from a Resident Evil or Silent Hill game. The story was pretty good and kept me interested. The graphics are decent for a 360 game. The environment is nicely done, and the character models aren't too bad either, although you will be seeing a lot of the same enemy types pop up throughout the entirety of the game, but that didn't bother me much. The only gripe I have about the graphics is that when playing, the main character's arms always look like they are detached from his body. Now the meat and potatoes of the game, the gameplay. A lot of people have criticized the gameplay saying that it was unresponsive. This is partially true, but only for the walking aspect of the game as the game for some reason sometimes doesn't register that your foot is placed forward in order to make the character walk. There is an auto walk feature however, but it is disabled in certain sections of the game forcing you to manually control your character's walking. Also if you want to explore the environment, you'll need to manually control the walking. Exploring the environment in this game is necessary for finding collectibles such as audio tapes and tarot cards, and for finding new weapons to use on your enemies. All other aspects of the game such as picking up weapons, opening doors, fighting and turning your character around are really responsive and work well. Note that there are some sections in the game that can be somewhat difficult such as some of the quicktime events where you'll have to run across a sinking derailed train or dodge traps. I know that the Kinect is not perfect, but it is innovative and works well enough to make this game playable. The controls do take a bit of time getting used to, but once you get used to them, you'll be slicing enemy limbs off in no time. Overall, I really enjoyed the game for what it was. It reminded me of a haunted house simulator more than it did a "fight for your life" type of game. The only complaint I have is that the game felt really short. If your a fan of horror games, a gorehound or if you just want a good cardio workout, then you'll probably enjoy this game.

Was pretty skeptical regarding the hype around the game, and in first few minutes I found the controls hard to get used to (FYI - I just finished playing through the arkham trilogy, so circle vs triangle for counter was hard to change). But after playing for a couple of hours and getting more comfortable with the control - wow - this game is fun. I've spend several hours simply traveling through the city and finding radio towers and backpacks. Regarding the "search parts", so far I prefer them over the Arkham riddler trophies because once you find them, you have them... rather than having to solve a puzzle. But in regards to puzzle, Spiderman has these really intriguing puzzles related to science that are actually a blast, and I haven't heard them talked about much in reviews. Things like building circuits and identifying material with a spectograph (sorry if that's wrong - first time I've dealt with one since highschool haha). I'd say so far my largest negative is I haven't felt too compelled to dive into the story missions when the side material is so much fun. Hopefully when I do that it's not an extreme let down. Regarding the web traversal, my experience has been very in line with what you may read in some reviews. At first it feels very tricky and overwhelming, but once you get unleashed into the city and can experiment and practice over large expanses, you really start getting the hang of it. I am so delighted to have this little pail. Yes its not a tall kitchen trash can or hamper height. Its what Ill cal discrete. But it has reduced the laborious task of my scooping and bagging litter several times a day. My cat has been recovering from multiple health issues including hyperthyroidism. She goes to the potty very often. And generally want it cleaned before she goes again. Or there could be accidents imminently forthcoming. And to insult injury it would be quite smelly. (Sry). So while I try to be green inspired, I doggie bagged her poo and her pee separately each time. (I know). But I did use compostable doggie bags. ... anywho. ... still who wants to do this 5 times a day. Give or take. So I was looking for a life improvement solution. Thankfully I came across Litter Genie!!! Now maybe almost anything would have been an improvement. Ok. Probably. But let me tell you I just love the simplicity and functionality. After I got my Litter Genie I was unexpededly under the weather for a week. I had not even opened the box. I was in bed sick and recovering for several days. I almost hired a cat sitter and merry maid to come assist. But I remembered the Genie. So though very fatigued I assembled it and started to use about 3 days after I got it. And I had managed to just barely keep things from going to south before then. However right away I appreciated how I just scooped and deposited in the Genie. Close the lid. Pull the slider. And done. No doggie bag. No getting on my hands. No putting doggie bag in kitchen bag and then storing kitchen bag in a back room multiple times through out the day. I was done and could focus on my health more. Based on some of the low reviews, I was concerned that the Litter Genie would get too full too quickly and that the smell would start to overwhelm me in a day or so. But it didnt. I was able to use it for almost 6 days straight before disposing the whole bag and starting a new one. (Which was also much easier than I expected) I would say that by day six it was difinitely time. But now I know the upper limits. And have a good routine. It may not be perfect for all. It could be improved Im sure. Buts its a huge upgrade for what was my nightmare daily routine. And just perfect for now. Five stars!!!!

This is a solidly 3 star product, due in part to deceitful marketing and a few cut corners. The primary claim I take issue with is that the lure is "good for an entire season," the instructions specify 12 weeks. It isn't. It has peak effectiveness for a few days, works well for a couple weeks, and will continue to trap beetles for over a month although at a diminishing rate. The issue is that you (presumably) are using this to PROTECT a plant the beetles are attacking. At three weeks, even up wind of said plants you're trying to protect, this no longer works even in a 3 foot radius of the bag in all directions let alone further away. I replace the bags frequently, so this is not due to a buildup of the aroma of dead beetles, but likely combined factors of the trap color fading from sun (badly faded in just a month? Come on!) and the scent lure simply wearing off. The bags also do not drain all the way to the bottom in rain, which is likely a deliberate oversight to increase the number of bags sold. More honest marketing regarding duration and efficacy, UV resistant plastic (crazy idea for an outdoor product), and transparent bags which drain better would all be able to bring this product up to 5 stars. I'm certainly not upset, I have more lures on the way, and will continue to use some brand of this trap in future years.

These are great! but can be a little tough to get on until you get used to them. My husband and I can get them on our grand-dogs easily if we work together (though my daughter can do it on her own, and I know others who can do it alone, too). Tearing has not been an issue. Occasionally there will be a lost bootie, thus all the spares in the package; Im assuming it is due to one not being put on as well as the other three. Our one grand-dog (minature poodle) lived through two winters without an issue (out in the country) but this year was refusing to go outside when it was minus zero out. It had been two days of refusals except for real quick potties then mad dashes back into the house without taking time to do the other. So they put these on, and once they took him out, he ran around with such glee as if set free! It was crazy to witness, especially to my skeptical son. You wouldnt think a rubber balloon on their feet when its that cold out would make a difference, but it did! My other grand-dog (shih tzu) is a recent rescue from down south. The cold was a big issue for him, as well as the snow itself; he hated both, and was very reluctant to walk. The salted sidewalks were a concern, too. A velcro bootie was first tried and he just stood there refusing to move. Wearing these Pawz booties he adjusted with ease and became a champ! He could walk a mile easily, leading the way, without lifting his paws because they were getting cold. But if he loses one in the snow, hell soon stop walking and it'll be the one bare paw he lifts up (little fellow then gets carried home). Wont face another winter without these booties!

So, I have been a Canon printer lover for over 20 years and I never thought I would leave that brand. Sadly their printers have declined in quality and performance every time I have bought a new one. I wish I had kept my old printer about 4 printers ago that still worked, but I wanted something with a few more features (I don't think they make ink for it anymore though). The last Canon printer I had was the MX922 and when it worked it was okay - never fantastic because of the size and weight (25.7# makes lifting it up to refill paper a major pain, and it needed a support under the printer drawer on my desk because it was too heavy). When I attempted to purchase a new printer to replace it (I got tired to having to align the print head due to wavy lines reappearing over and over), I couldn't obtain a new Canon that worked out of the box. So, on to the HP OfficeJet Pro 6978. The printer feels solid and not flimsy in any way. The set up was simple with easy to follow instructions. Everything went smoothly without a single error message or problem. It printed an alignment page and scanned it successfully, perfect on the first try. The software is easy to use and straightforward. Scanning is easy and it is also easy to select which format to save the file under and the location to save it to. The application is also nice because you are able to see your ink levels right within the It was simple to set it up on my wireless network. It is recognized by all of my devices, computers, phones, tablets. Printing via email is also easy, and I was able to effortlessly change the print email address to one I can remember. I didn't test the fax capability because I do not have a telephone line in my den, I use Tiny Fax on my phone for faxes and find it to be a simple, easy, low-cost method to send faxes the 2 or 3 times a year I need to send a fax. If I was a frequent fax sender, I would certainly have a phone line installed in my den and use my printer to fax instead. For how rarely I use it, it's not worth the cost of a phone line or using a 100 ft telephone cord to connect to the nearest phone line. I signed up for Instant Ink, I print less than 50/pages a month and at $2.99/mo for ink, it can't be beaten. That is less than I would spend to replace the cartridges one time a year. If I find I print more, I can always upgrade to the next higher plan, which is still less than replacing the ink tanks one time a year. Also, I signed up for the free trial and entered a code and ended up getting 5 months of Instant Ink for free - awesome! So far I love the printer, I will update if anything changes.

PROs: Good quality herbs, fresh aroma and flavor. This product would certainly merit a 5-star rating if the containers were better quality. CONs: The containers are thin flimsy plastic, and have an obvious and unpleasant aroma. There is no inner seal under the lid. I purchased 4 different herbs/spices from Marshalls Creek, and all 4 had powder on the outside of the container, because the lids were not twisted tight, and are held on only by a loose-fitting plastic wrap. That was very disappointing. After tightening the lids properly, the powdered contents are unable to get out of the container, if it tips over for example. But the seller *should* have a process in place to assure that the lids are tight; clearly, they do not. I will transfer the contents into glass containers with air-tight lids, as the plastic containers these herbs arrive in are unusually thin and flimsy, and have a detectable odor.

Job: 3 downed Bradford Pear trees that have been down for two seasons. All smaller (a ton) branch branches (if you have bradford pears you know what I am talking about) already removed previous season and put in brush pile. These were large trees for being bradfords. The tool: 12" Greenworks battery powered chainsaw with 40volt 2ah battery: Got the battery powered chainsaw today and could not resist. Charged the 2ah battery and got to work. I should have opted for the 4ah battery but my back said Thank You to me for not doing it. Being the only one cutting, carrying, pulling, throwing, and stacking is rough. I bet some of the pieces I lifted were over 200lbs. Using a metal chair to use as a brace to cut without getting the chain in the dirt. Of course cutting was on and off work so it is hard to estimate battery life. But rough guess is about 25 minutes run time. Using a Greenpower battery chainsaw. It is very different than a gas powered chainsaw. Had to adjust cutting style for it. With a battery powered chainsaw slow and steady wins the race. My first thought upon opening it was, toy? First time pulling the trigger thought was so slow and under powered. I have to admit it did surprise me. You can NOT muscle your way through with it's style cutting. Very gentle pressure or you will over heat the battery. It will chew its way through, just give it a little more time than a gas power. Good Thoughts: Nice not having to yank and yank and yank to get it started. Just trigger. Lighter than a gas powered so easier to handle It is a lot quieter than a gas powered No gas smell around or on me, period. Bad thoughts: Slower than a gas powered, a lot, so jobs take longer Chain kept working loose and coming off if not tighten between just about every cut (pain in the butt) Relatively short run time but that is to be expected with just a 2ah battery (but back says thank you) Oil gets all over your hands when putting the chain back on (when I go back out shop towels are coming with)_ The job is not complete yet but over all pleased with the amount it did. Currently waiting on battery to recharge for round two. I did go out for round two yesterday evening after battery was charge. Almost finished the already down trees before the battery gave out again. Today I will be going out again to finish what I can. But for the really really thick parts I will need someone with a real chainsaw with a 16" bar. I really hate Bradford Pear trees. Will update review if anything changes. Greenworks 12-Inch 40V Cordless Chainsaw, 2.0 AH Battery Included 20262

Always have Minor Soup Bases on hand, including the Clam Base. Very concentrated, so we save money over expensive and often hard to find clam and Clamato juices. Clam Lover's Chowder recipe: Start with 1 teaspoon Clam Base for 2 Cups water, 2 Cups milk, 1 Cup diced potatoes, (fresh or frozen), small amount minced onion and cooked bacon crumbles. Heat in large pan over low heat until potatoes tender, about 20 minutes. Add 1 large (51 ounce) can chopped clams, a couple pinches of white pepper, thyme and dill and continue cooking 10 minutes more. One tip is to thicken with 1/2 Cup instant mashed potato flakes rather than flour, and add 2 Tablespoons butter toward the end before serving. Taste before adding additional seasoning. You can always add 1/4 to 1/2 teaspoon more Clam Base at the end of cooking but the only way to decrease over-salting is by more liquids/potatoes. 5 Stars!

Foreo's Iris Illuminating Eye Massager is advertised as an electric version of finger-tapping that will promote "younger-looking, refreshed eyes", presumably by increasing the circulation to the area. The device is 5 inches tall, 1 5/8 inches wide, and covered in silicone in the color of your choice. Each treatment takes one minute, 30 seconds on each eye. The gadget has two modes: Spa Mode (press once), which combines the tapping with vibration, and Pure Mode (press twice), in which the massager simply wiggles to create a sensation similar to tapping. According to Foreo, the Spa Mode is for older customers who need a little more help around the eyes. You can also adjust the frequency of the tapping/wiggling with plus and minus buttons, but you will probably not use those. First, read the instructions if you can. I required a magnifying glass to read the enclosed user manual. The downloadable manual on Foreo's web site is more legible. Second, charge your massager. This requires a USB charger; it won't plug directly into an A/C outlet. Foreo claims that one charge will last up to 140 uses or 140 minutes. That's possible. I've been using mine for 3 weeks (about 20 uses), and it's still going strong. Press the center indentation to turn the device on. Press twice if you prefer Pure Mode. Apply some moisturizer or serum to the eye area. Move the massager along the brow bone above the eye and underneath the eye just above the bone until the massager skips or pauses. This indicates that 30 seconds have passed, and you should move to the other eye. After one minute, you will feel 3 skips. The device turns itself off after 3 minutes. Wash the Iris with soap and water. It is better to use moisturizer than serum, as serum will soak in before you get to the second eye. You want some lubricant to be present. Otherwise, the silicone tugs at the skin. Foreo claims that this tapping and vibrating helps eye creams absorb better, but I don't know why it would. I found the Spa Mode uncomfortable. The Pure Mode was less so. I'm in my forties and showing some puffiness under my eyes. I tried the Iris to see if it might improve circulation enough to reduce the puffiness. After three weeks of using the massager once daily, I do not see any difference in puffiness, and it is uncomfortable to use. If it felt good, it would have redeeming value, but it doesn't. The packaging is nice; the quality of the massager and battery seem good. But it doesn't do anything.

We're all aware of the fact that the news these days can be somewhat exasperating, and perhaps even depressing at times. Let's start a new trend "laughter ambush". Let's follow the example of the other reviewers who started putting googly eyes on things to surprise unsuspecting co-workers, friends and family. I have even become quite adept at putting eyes on things in public places. No harm done, they can easily be pulled off. Since there may not be time to stand and pull the backing off the eyes, I use velcro dots to get them prepared. (Sold on Amazon). All I have to do is remove one piece of plastic to stick them on things. I use the large eyes for a better effect. I was alone in the post office yesterday and took advantage of the situation when the clerk went to get my package. I put two eyes on his package scanner, and left before he discovered them. I watched from the window and really enjoyed his reaction. Again, the eyes can be easily removed if desired. Since I am generally considered a conservative, quiet type, no one would suspect that I am the one who pulled the prank. That makes this whole idea even better! Good luck, and have fun! Before I realized the paper backing came off, I used a drop of glue, or my glue gun, and the velcro dots (sometimes cut in quarters) to attach the eyes to my craft projects. I even pulled the white backing off the eyes to glue the black spots where I wanted them, to create cross eyes, or eyes looking somewhere besides the center. (Yes, I'm retired and have a lot of time on my hands). The backing is pretty easy to pull off on most sizes, except the very tiny ones. As for the box, well, it does let the sizes get a bit mixed up, but I don't care. I sometimes put different sizes on things just to look even funnier. This is a very generous seller. He included two extra bags of eyes in our order! You get a tremendous assortment and amount of googly eyes for the money with this seller. Fast shipping too! You will become quite obsessed with these great googly eyes, I'm sure of that!

I agree with other reviewers who have stated that the picture is not accurate. The fitting is turned down to a round pipe-like end on all three arms of the T - unlike what the picture shows. I deducted one star from the review for the inaccurate and misleading picture. In terms of functionality, in most cases this will not affect the use of the fitting. These are brass 1/4 NPT fittings not 2 steel fittings - You do not crank away with all your strength with your biggest monkey wrench to make the fitting leak-tight. Personally I use Teflon thread tape on the threads, some people use thread goo of some type to make the fittings absolutely leak-tight. In any case, brass fittings are generally more accurately manufactured and turned so they do not require as much pressure in tightening the fittings as larger galvanized iron fittings. I found the fitting to be exactly what it is - a 1/4 NPT brass Tee, female all three ends. The Threads seem accurately made and fit exactly with my male 1/4 NPT pipe fittings. By the way 1/4 NPT is the size frequently used on compressed air fittings in the USA. The 1/4 refers to the nominal inside diameter of the pipe. The diameter of the threaded hole is obviously larger.

My Hamsters hustle through my Habitrail system of tubes. You can make some interesting, pleasing to the eye designs, with a space age look. There can be a problem with some of the inner ring connecter rings sliding along with the outer part of the ring, thereby not applying any pressure and hold to the connection. A screw driver holding inner ring while turning outer part will create the necessary pressure to hold the joining pieces. This can be awkward and require an extra pair of hands, or propping both sides of the tube pieces as they have to be well aligned to to make a secure connection. As other users of this product have noted if the connecter rings are not turned just the right way, it can cause system failure immediately or later, allowing Hamster escape. Another problem is the added pressure of the tubes can cause the thin, plastic of the base pod/home (not included) to flex and cause the the door latch to not function. This can be remedied by adjusting the configuration of your tube system, or adding a screw below the latch and a short strong band to hold them together. I still like the system though-most of the time, and bought extra Ovo Adventure Packs for system expansion as it is more economical than buying separate pieces. I also buy the Critter Trails Add on Night Lites (they fit this system), and add the different colored lights at key points of for a very cool machine/metropolis look.

The quality of these units has really gone down hill. This is my second unit and at only going on three years old, is already having issues. I was seeing algae buildup in the pool and was wondering why. I got out my test kit and the chlorine level was much lower than expected. I open the cover on the AquaTrol and the setting was at 36% and no warning lights. I bumped it up to 45% and the next day, there was no improvement. I flipped it up to super Chlorinate and within 10 seconds, both the check cell and check salt lit up solid and the unit shut down the chlorination cycle. I had the salt tested when I open the pool and it came back at 3000 ppm so I know that salt level was not the issue. Just to make sure, I tested it with my own kit and once again, it tested at 3000 ppm. I removed the cell and it looked clean to me so calcium buildup is not the issue. The display was then showing 0 ppm for salt. Fortunately for me, I still have the salt cell from the original unit but it did look dirty. I ordered a cleaning stand and picked up some muriatic acid from the big box store. I cleaned the old one first and it initially also bombed out but the display was still showing 0 ppm on the average count screen. Like the AquaRight unit, you can set it to the instant reading on the debug screens and did that. It was showing 3000 ppm on the instant reading screen and after setting the average to that, it began working properly with the old cell. Cleaning the new cell appeared to get it working but the ppm still was showing a little low at 2800 ppm. The next day chlorine was still low and setting it to super chlorinate caused it to fail once again after about 10 seconds. It is currently running on the old cell again and if it continues to run properly for a few days then I might consider ordering a new cell. Otherwise, its time to get a new unit from another maker. I understand that even with quality units, you can get a dud every now and then but I believe in this case, the quality level of this unit has been diminished. I still have the manual for the older unit that I am using the cell from and it had a warranty of 5 years, the same as the AquaRight and their other high end units. To be truthful, it was really a 3 year warranty because the last two years they were only covering 40% of the repair cost. The newer unit manual shows a 3 year warranty for the higher end units like the AquaRight but only 1 year for the AquaTrol. That is a significant reduction from 5 to 1 year and there is usually a reason for that. I would suspect that they still make their high end units and have outsourced the manufacture of the AquaTrol to some second rate, low cost mass production outlet where QC is at best a passing thought. With such a crap warranty for a fairly expensive product, I would look at other makes before buying this one. If it turns out to only be the cell, that is still a cost of well over $200 on top of the cost of the original unit and at this cost, it would be far less costly to just add chlorine manually and not deal with the corrosion issues using salt water causes.

I use this stuff to sweeten blends of fruit tea I concoct from things normally tossed out. For example, orange rinds, apple peelings and a few other fruits. The teas turn out quite good and, at least on a small scale, our household produces less garbage, while producing fruit and herbal teas the family enjoys. The Stevia leaves add just enough sweetness to my blends without adding a gylcemic load to them. I add them directly into the dry tea blends and don't know how one would extract the sweetness while leaving the leaves behind. A word of caution is in order. The leaves, are potently sweet and should be used in small quantities, for whatever volume you're sweetening. If you're trying them for the first time, take a pinch and place it on the tip of your tongue while munching it with your front teeth. This gives you the experience you need to begin using stevia without overdoing it. I recommend this product highly. The quality is excellent.

I put off buying a shock collar because I did not want to inflict pain on my dog. I love my dog but decided enough was enough. He developed a destructive habit of stealing socks and running under the bed with them. He would growl and try to bite whenever I would reach for the sock and all the training videos on youtube weren't helping ( yes, I was consistent). Anyways, this collar advertised itself as having three modes: beep, vibrate, and shock. I did NOT want to shock my dog so I decided to give this a try. I charged it and tried it out on myself first. The beep mode is just a beep, thats it! It's pretty loud so your dog will definitely hear it. The vibration has a setting from 1-99 with 99 being the strongest. I put the collar up to my neck and dialed the vibration setting to 99. If you want to know how it feels, grab your phone, set it to vibrate and press it against our neck. Thats how strong it is. The shock mode also has a setting from 1-99. The prongs come with a plastic covering and the instructions say to leave those on and that the shock can still be felt. For this mode I started at 1, lol. I put the collar up to my neck and didn't feel anything. I didn't feel anything until I dialed it to around 20. Even then, it wasn't a strong shock. I wouldn't even call it a shock, it felt like static and could feel a little tingling sensation on my skin. I stopped at around 70. This was as much as I could tolerate. It wasn't painful, but uncomfortable. I decided if I ever had to use this mode on my dog the most I would use is 70. Thanksfully, I never had to reach that point because my dog responded well to the beep. Whenever he would grab something and run under the bed I would press the beep button and that would startle him, which would cause him to drop whatever he had in his mouth. At one point I did use the vibration button but I know it is not painful. My dog responded well to the vibration mode as well so there was NO need for me to use the shock mode. Also, I would like to add I used this on my small mixed breed dog that weights around 10 pounds. The collar included was too big so I used a clip to tie off the excess collar. I would definitely recommend this for those who want to discipline and train their dogs without having to shock them.

I use the frontline on my 2 small dogs, however whenever we would go for a walk, they would come back with ticks. On one particular walk, I pulled 5 unattached ticks off one of my little furboys and 3 off the other, not good. 1 Tick is 1 too many, and my smallest dog is very allergic to ticks and flea, he will itch uncontrollably when bitten. I knew that I needed something that wouldn't just kill them little buggers after they bite my furbabies, I needed something to repel them before they even get a chance to bite my boys, also so they won't be crawling on me either. So in search I went and came across the Wondercide product, read the reviews and decided to give it a try. I purchased the small bottle of the Cedar and when it arrived, quickly sprayed down my boys. It's been about a week, and I've sprayed them only twice (no bath in between), and so far so good. I used this Kiva Certified UMF 15+ Raw Manuka Honey to rid my body of a severe tooth infection. I had a lingering infection four years after receiving Penicillin and Amoxicillin for a tooth infection that kept flaring up due to my wisdom teeth needing to be removed. I lost an extreme amount of weight, was very sickly and fatigued, my hair was thin and dry, and my nails were not growing healthy, I was nutrition deficient and had a lot of resulting health problems. When I was in very bad pain with the infection (a deep hole in one tooth that went below the gum to the point where I could see my jaw bone, and it had developed a small bubbled up area that was extremely sensitive and PAINFUL), I was applying Orajel every night, and I struggled with eating. I did some reading on Manuka honey and decided to give it a try. There were no instructions online about exactly HOW to use it for a tooth infection, so I decided on my own method of taking it. I put a small amount on a spoon and licked it onto my tongue, then I took a small sip of water and held it in my mouth, then I swished it for a bit (to let the water mix with the honey so that it coated my entire mouth), then I swallowed it. I repeated this every four hours for two months (when it came to sleeping I didn't set an alarm for every four hours, I instead took a dose right before bed and started again when I woke up, sleep is important for healing). I was very surprised when it actually worked in killing off the infection, the bubbled up area flattened, and the pain went away so that I no longer needed to apply Orajel at night. I gained my healthy weight back in the two weeks after the infection was removed from my mouth and bloodstream. I have not decided if I will continue to take the manuka honey as a preventative measure, or only do it if and when an infection comes back. I wish there was more info available online on how to use manuka honey for infections and serious health conditions, that is why I felt the need to share what I did in case it could save a life. One note, I also did not swish hydrogen peroxide as part of my treatment. I found research online that hydrogen peroxide does not lessen an infection or have any effect on it, and more than that it actually kills off your repairing cells that are working to heal you (could be incorrect info, but it seemed legit), so I made sure to stop swishing that and as soon as I did the manuka honey began healing things up pretty quickly.

I'm an experienced fountain pen user who's tried lots of papers with various pens. I bought this paper after reading many glowing reviews, & tried it with several pens/nibs combinations. The pad arrived sealed in plastic. Apparently, the plastic and/or storage affected the paper adversely, because out of the plastic, this pad performed horribly, with even a fine nib/dry ink producing severe bleeding through. After a few hours in my house, I tried again, with much better results. Pros: the paper is very thin; normally, that's a bad thing, but in this case it was good because the paper holds ink well (in most cases) without feathering or bleeding. I used 3 nibs (18K medium, 18K fine, & 14K medium, all with no customization, with good results. Cons: the paper is very expensive, at 16 cents per page at the time of purchase. So I wanted to see how it fared with an "extreme" nib, a broad stub with customization. Not so well, with a slight amount of bleeding. Recommendation: this is a good paper for most common uses, but not so great at handling a broad nib. I recommend Rhodia pads, which are smoother, do not allow bleeding even with a broad nib, & are not cheap, but considerably cheaper per page than this Tomoe River paper (10 cents per page).

I mainly play shooters so i was looking for a backup controller to my Scuf Impact and stumbled across the Hori FPS Plus. Now i owned all of the Xbox consoles so i am very familiar with the stick layout but have zero issues with the dualshock 4 layout. Upon opening the Hori I wad not super impressed Yes i knew it was going to be light due to not having the vibration motors but it just seemed kinda cheap and plasticky. Its similar to the 360 controller design and didn't feel that bad in my hands. Loaded up Fortnite and any negative feelings about the controller disappeared immediately. The triggers despite how they appear in pictures work great. I can swap weapons with L1 and R1 so much faster it isn't funny. Then the L2 and R2 have shorter travel distance than that of the dualshock 4 or my Scuf and makes it much easier to win gunfights. My accuracy also seems much better this far. My kills per match has doubled easily. Also note there is no headphone jack but I use a wireless controller so i don't need it. And the instructions are in Japanese so you will need them translated to figure out the special buttons. Im not using them nor did i plan to so i haven't used rhe turbo feature. BEST FRIEND! You may have heard that the once so important "drainage layer" is now being considered not only unnecessary, but actually very very bad for your potted plants. I won't go into it too much, you can google it, but its basically just physics that keeps the water in the soil regardless the drainage layer- so by adding the layer, you are bringing the wettest part of the soil closer to the roots. Not Good. One of the downsides to not having that drainage layer though, is that you loose soil out of the drainage hole. BUT you don't loose it with these guys! Just throw one over the hole on the bottom of your pot or if the holes are on the sides, I press them against the holes and hold in place until covered with potting soil! Wish I had purchased them a long long time ago!

I wondered how Rhino Ramps could be certified by the ASME if collapses have been reported. A cylinder with tapered sides supports the platform. Once the weight is centered on the platform, it looks very strong. The pebble gravel at my house might not provide a stable base for some devices. I put the Rhino Ramps on the gravel, backed my car onto them, set the brake, and shoved the back of the car sideways. The only movement was from the flexing of the tires. That satisfied me. Under that load, I didn't need plywood to support Rhino Ramps. Kissing the stops At the back of one ramp, the base had dug into the gravel slightly. The other end of the ramp was now about 1/2 off the ground. Theres a stop 2-1/2 high at the back of the ramp. My tire must have hit it hard enough to cause the ramp to rock back. That kind of thing might be the usual cause of collapses. Id overlooked the instruction to press the ramps against the tires. Theyd been slightly uneven so that one ramp had borne the brunt of stopping the vehicle. Theres a 1/4 lip at the top of the slope. From there, its 12 farther to the stop. Now I saw that Rhino Ramps didn't allow 12 to stop. The stops are high enough that my tires hit them 6 after the lip. Raising a truck on steel ramps, stopping in 6 had been easy because the truck would creep up the ramp in low range. My car has no low range. Precisely controlling its speed by feel was a challenge. If I reached the top at 1 mph, Id hit the stop 1/3 second later. That wasn't enough time to move my foot from the gas to the brake. I put a strip of masking tape across the sill of the drivers doorway and another strip across the lid of a coffee can. With the ramps positioned, I measured 26 from the center of the wheel to the top of the slope. I put the coffee can beside the car with the tape 26 behind the tape on the door sill. Backing up the ramps, I let the car come almost to a stop as the strips met. I didn't use the brake to stop because it could do more harm than good. Afterward, there was no mark on the gravel where the ramps had been. Most of those who demonstrated Rhino Ramps on youtube were excellent drivers. One, however, hit the stops hard enough to skid the ramps on concrete. He rocked them when he demonstrated them on asphalt. There, but for the Grace of God, go I. Riding mower I use these ramps more for my riding mower than my car. If the underside of the deck is clean, the airflow will let me mow even long, wet grass without leaving clumps. When I finish mowing, it takes only two minutes to fetch the ramps and the floor jack, back onto the ramps, set the brake, and raise the front with the jack. With the skirt 10 off the ground, its easy to remove caked grass with a Wonder Bar, which gives me a foot of reach and a choice of three angles. To see what Im doing, I put on a headlamp and lie beside the mower on a mat. I find the least caking after mowing wet grass. I guess that proves caking is caused by sap and not visible water. Raising the mower also makes it easy to check blades, remove them for sharpening, and grease spindles. I can lower the deck farther than is possible on the ground. That makes it easy to remove debris and lubricate.

This is quite an amp, and by this I mean it is QUITE A LARGE amp. I'm embarrassed at my expectations. Other 1x12 amps I've had certainly don't have this scope. That said, this is one hell of a tube combo amp. While die-hards may only recommend a Made In Britain Marshall, you could do a lot worse than this DSL. Very sturdy, well made, and not at all a fragile piece of gear. The options and versatility of dialing in just about any Marshall tone is at your fingertips. So why did I decide to return it? Portability. While not too impractical, I have a hard time lugging this bad boy around. Consider that if you travel a lot for band practice. I'd give my kingdom for a gosh darn set of side handles instead of the silly briefcase handle at the top! So what did I settle with? Marshall actually released 1 watt amp heads for their 50th anniversary back in 2012. While limited in versatility (for example, no reverb), they are incredible bedroom practice amps, my intended purpose for this DSL. Best part? They are all limited edition (around 1200 pieces of each model) and will only go up in value with time. And, generally speaking, you won't pay much more (at the moment) for one that you would this, and they are all made in the UK. Love my JVM-1H, and the fact I can take it and its cabinet separately to disperse the weight. Plus, a head and a cab just looks cooler. If you do end up buying this DSL40C, I recommend getting it used. You'll save as much as $200 or more as this isn't necessarily that in demand in the used market.

I bought this smoker to replace a similar Masterbuilt that I'd had (and enjoyed) for several years. This one was a comparable price and has a number of small improvements over my old one (e.g better design for drip pans). Since the older smoker had a dedicated remote, I figured the Bluetooth would just replace it and work the same way. That's true in theory, but in reality, the Bluetooth app is just as bad as everyone says it is. Every time I used it, it spontaneously heated the smoker up to some temperature over 800 degrees. (Fortunately, the reviews alerted me to this possibility so I turned it off before it got really hot.) There's no pattern to when this happened; it seemed completely random. I thought perhaps that if I set the time and temp on the smoker itself and used the Bluetooth just to monitor, that might work. But it didn't. Everytime I used the Bluetooth, I had this same problem. The app is about two years old and many, many reviews describe the same problem. It's inexcusable that Masterbuilt hasn't even attempted an update to fix this problem. That said, it's not a dealbreaker for me because I don't often use the remote/Bluetooth--only when I'm closely monitoring meat temperature with the probe. Most of the time, I set the temperature and go by time and weight alone. (For example, when smoking chicken, fish, or ribs, the probe isn't practical.) If you don't use the Bluetooth app, the smoker works great. It is easy to program and keeps an even temperature, plus it's easy to add more wood. It has a lot of capacity for the size of the cabinet. Bottom line: if having the app is the ONLY reason you're buying this smoker, stay away. If you want a great smoker for a reasonable price, forget about the app and operate the smoker manually. You'll be hard pressed to find anything as good anywhere near this price point.

I developed an air leak in my pool pumping system that seemed invisibly to be coming from my old Pentair strainer pot. It kept my pump from priming during summer morning start-ups, and took me months to localize as it got worse and worse. No water leaks were noticed while pumping, but squirts of water were observed at the bottom of the old pot during pump shutoff. I had to cut the PVC plumbing and unbolt the pot to get it free, and sure enough, there was a crack at the base extending through the O-ring junction connecting the pot to the impeller. I purchased this exact replacement strainer and installed it using a removeable 1-1/2" PVC Connector (to facilitate replacement if/when needed). Now my pump system is perfectly sealed with no air bubbles or priming difficulties. My original pot lasted 20 summers, so I expect good service out of this one for a long time.

It was easy to setup and start using. Probably 10 minutes to assemble the 36" auger and 12" extension. I drilled five holes so far. It works reasonably well and isn't too heavy. My soil is totally soaked at the surface and harder packed clay below. There are occasional rocks. If you don't have a tamping bar, you should get one with a chisel point to break up hard to reach rocks. It's not too difficult for one man to control if you're reasonably athletic. The thing that took me a while to figure out is when the auger stalls out from hitting a rock or something, the shock absorbing spring can get wound up 1/4-1/2 a turn. If you try to just take your hands off the handles it will unwind rapidly--it bent my thumb back uncomfortably that way. You can easily unwind it with your upper body and maintain control, though. It gets a little laborious to remove from the hole once it's in there a couple of feet, but other than that it's pretty easy to lift and move. It does the job really well. Good value, and so far, pretty reliable. No trouble starting so far. The gas tank is a little leaky if you leave the unit tipped onto that side. ---Update I drilled 22 holes about 40 inches deep through a layer of ~1 foot topsoil and the rest is clay to set some posts for a pole building. It is extremely laborious. If there are multiple rocks or roots in the hole, it took at least an hour to get to the desired depth. In ideal conditions it was about 30-45 minutes of very hard labor. If you have any back or cardiovascular problems, I'd think twice about using this extensively. If your project is in an accessible location, you should definitely rent a skid steer/auger type contraption. You could probably drill 10 holes in the time it takes to drill 1 with this. On the other hand, I used this to drill holes to plant 2 new cherry trees and it was nearly perfect for that task. So, all in all I would not recommend it for a big construction job, but I would for drilling holes for planting, or for occasional use, like setting a post for a mailbox or similar things.

The ink is a bit thin and will bleed through some paper - if it's typically thin legal pad, notebook or regular paper. I did also use it in a heavy gauge paper in a journal and that was all good. No bleed through. Also, depending on how much pressure you use the ink can run quickly causing a thick line (medium nib) or if it's inadvertently turned, the line can thin out and eventually disappear due to lack of contact with the paper - still I couldn't beat it for the price!!!

I've been giving this to my kittens for about 2 months, they are now 8 months old. My boy has developed missing hair patches on his shoulders while my girl is perfectly fine. Winston, my feline son, was hiding all day and seemed melancholy all the time. He would come out at night to cuddle on my chest but that's it. He would lie on me for hours if he could at night but during the day he was MIA. I decided to stop giving them the Lysine as in some cats it causes an allergic reaction. Today is the 3rd day without the Lysine and yesterday I already noticed a difference in his attitude and saw him more often during the day. Today he has been out all day, he's lying by the sliding glass door, accepting loves and even jumping on his sister a little bit. He ate his breakfast and is acting like a normal cat again and I am so happy. I think this product should have a warning on it for the allergy issue explaining what to keep an eye out for. While it didn't seem to bother his sister, it really did a number on him and for about 4 weeks, he was not a happy kitty.

As the years have pasted, I have used GROW!T, Hydroton, "Clay Aggregate", "Expanded Clay Pebbles", "LECA Media" and whatever else it's been called. I'm especially liking two things about HYDRO CRUNCH which was lacking in the other brands. The SIZE of the clay "balls" is most consistent AND the "balls" are NOT perfectly round. This solves two past problems. Other brands have beads ranging in size from 3mm to 17mm in the same bag. This left all the small beads on the bottom and all the large beads on top (this would also end up happening in the planters themselves). Second, the irregular shapes of HYDRO CRUNCH allow for more surface area contact which gives me better wicking.

Yes. Will I ever buy these again? Absolutely not.

Saute 1 onion, 1 celery stalk, 2 garlic cloves, and 1/2 cup flour in 2 Tbsp vegan margarine. Add 1/2 cup mushrooms. Add 1tsp thyme, 1/2 tsp sage, 1/2 salt, 1/4 tsp black pepper. Reduce heat to low, add 6 bread slices turned into cubes, 1/2 cup walnuts or pecans. In separate pan, cook 1 cup of this product (Basmati and red Quinoa and white Quinoa) and add in 2 1/2 cups of vegetable broth. Mix both pans together, bake at 375 degrees for about 30 minutes. This stuffing is SOOOO wonderful. I like to pair it with mashed potatoes, green peas or green beans or squash, and cranberry sauce. It is good for Thanksgiving, but also good for any time of the year. It is too good to wait until Thanksgiving to enjoy it.

The item's text instructions do not relate to the accompanying table that together attempt to explain programming steps. The LED indicator codes also do not match the table (odd shapes in some cases), and they do not appear in the order that the table/text indicate that they should appear during programming sequences. This controller was either defective out of the box (I returned it,) or the instructions are so hopelessly out of sync with the LED readouts that you cannot know if you are programming it correctly. Also, the temperature change function (C to F) does not operate as instructions say it should, or, the unit was/is stuck on centigrade. The controller seems well made and was well packaged, but with these operational errors/issues I can only wish potential purchasers 'good luck.' I ordered this for its two outlet capability so I could run two heat strings at once, but after this experience, I am buying another reptile tank heat controller and using 2 side-by-side instead--the reptile tank controller has a limited temperature range compared to the InkBird product, but it works, will keep soil at right temp for planting beds, which was my need; also, instructions were simple; text guides relate 1:1 to what is seen on the LED.
