Operation seems a bit needlessly complicated. When answering a call, in addition to picking the phone up off the base you have to push the answer button or it rings right in your ear. Clearing the voice mail indicator and the call log is a bit tedious - press this, then press that, then press this, then press that... You can't set a specific ring tone for a particular number logged into caller ID and, personally, the selection of ring tones are not to my liking - either very industrial and irritating or very "techno" and irritating. There's a light indicating the phone is charging when it's on the base but it would be nice to have that light blink when a voice mail has been left so you don't have to get up close and peer at the screen to know someone called. Contrary to what appears in the picture, the screen and the numbers are not lit when the phone is sitting on the base, they light up for about 10 seconds when it's picked up from the base. The phones are pretty lightweight, which for some might be nice but I prefer a sturdier case. I dropped one on a cement floor and the battery cover went flying right off. Thankfully it didn't break. That said, the phones are not expensive, they do seem to work OK, the sound quality is good enough, with normal vision it's easy to read the screen, including caller ID, it's nice to have a battery indicator on the screen, there seems to be good range between the phone and the base, and maybe this is standard now but it's nice to have all phones display the changes made on one. I do miss my old Uniden set, though.

I'm happy with my order and give 3 stars as the package was mostly what I expected when I was placing the order. My review below is to give more specifics for those interested. OVERALL - This is a budget build. The board is flat, no incline. There's plenty of room for small Donner pedals and less room if you add large pedals (common sense). Good small board for a handful of effects and a power brick. CASE - Most reviews have complained about the case, though I don't have any gripe with it. The strap is a thin nylon and there are plastic hooks to attach to the bag loops. Bag loops are oddly halfway down the edges of the gig bag, which will mean it'll flip over if carried upright. "VELCRO" /pedal tape / hook & loop - Mine came with hook and loop (not rolls as pictured, but a folded and flattened bundle). It came with 38" of hook and 39" of loop. I had purchased 3M Dual Lock Reclosable Fastener TB3870 250/250 for all my gear instead of hook and loop. DIMENSIONS - exact measurements are 8"x19-7/8"x13/16". Measuring the pedal deck - the larger two horizontal decks are just under 2" wide. The smaller deck surface is 13/16" wide. My pedal tape is a full 1" and I don't mind if it overhangs. The back pocket (possibly for a power supply or cable management is 4" wide x 11/16" deep. HEIGHT - The rubber feet are riveted on and give the board 1/4" clearance from the floor. This leaves little room for cables underneath. From inside the pocket to the floor is 1-1/16", not enough to hide the Donner pedal supply (1-5/16 without adhesive tape) beneath the board. Issues - mine is out of square and will need a shim in one corner so it doesn't rock back and forth. I plan on modifying it to be inclined by adding some small legs or wedges to the sides/back and so that the power supply can be installed underneath. For the money, it's a good board.

Are you buying warehouse size bags of sugar? Are you swarmed by inquisitive little critters when you go outside? Have they taken to staring into your windows and chirping angrily when supplies start to run low? Then this is the feeder you want. Ive literally tried every major feeder brand out there, and while this feeder is pricey, it is worth the money. Heres why: Every part of this feeder can go into the dishwasher, except the little wire hook. Plastic parts on the top rack. If you want to feed Hummingbirds, sanitation is absolutely essential. Feeders can quickly get black mold, and if the birds catch it, they die a slow death by starvation, because their little throats swell up, and they cant eat or drink. If you won't clean your feeders, much better not to feed them at all. Being able to clean in the dishwasher sanitizes the feeders so that you don't have to scrub with those itty bitty brushes. The top of the feeder screws on to the glass reservoir. I cant begin to tell you how this is design genius. Most feeder tops are glued on, or are attached by a metal cage, which means they can never go in the dishwasher. It also means that eventually the glue will fail, your feeder reservoir will fall off the hook, and youll have a mess on your lawn or patio. The Feeder top has a space that doubles as an ant moat, so you don't need a separate one. Just a couple of concerns: When filled to the top with sugar water, this heavy duty glass feeder weighs about 9 lbs. Make sure to hold it from both the top and bottom when you carry it, and make sure you have somewhere sturdy to hang it from. The wire hook that comes with it looks flimsy, but its not. Guard it carefully. The opening in the top of the feeder is tiny, and good luck trying to contact the company about a replacement (Im thinking your best bet would actually be a zip tie).

I've used this sprayer, including its Gilmour predecessor (apparently Gilmour sold this sprayer to Chapin a few years back) for the last 7 years to spray weeds on my 2-acre property. I'd previously used the Ortho Dial-a-Spray, but it was quite "leaky" and the settings were not well calibrated. When I'm searching for a product, I pay particular attention to the negative reviews... as they are often not legit, including this sprayer. So.... I decided to comment on the following concerns about it not working well. 1. "Doesn't spray 30 feet". Of course not with the deflector attached. Common sense. Remove the deflector and you can spray a stream... maybe 30 feet... depending upon your water pressure. 2. "Leaks all over the place". There are 4 places that could be "not tightened enough" and would cause leakage. Make sure those are all tight and try again. This sprayer might leak a bit also, but not as bad as the Ortho. I always wear nitrile gloves when spraying. 3. "Doesn't syphon". Sprayer works on the Bernoulli Principle. Meaning, "it HAS to work... if the system is in working order"... there are no moving parts....fundamental principle of fluid dynamics. 2, places which can cause the syphon function not to work. 1. Syphon tube. It could be blocked at the bottom from debris or if the chemicals have acted together to form a bit of coagulation... which collects on the screen and prevents fluid moving up the tube. The tube may not be properly seated in the receptacle at the sprayer head, or there may possibly be a crack in the tube. 2. The "selector wheel". The wheel has a calibrated hole for each setting. It must be centered to work properly. (If you're curious as to why, look up "Bernoulli Principle".) The wheel centering can be just a bit off causing the sprayer to not work. The tiny hole in the wheel can also get plugged with debris and stop it working... happened to me once, though I can't imagine how dirt got into that tiny hole. To be sure you're getting proper operation, suggest including a "dye marker" in your chemical. I use a blue one. The dye will indicate whether the syphon is working or not. When you 1st start your spray, make sure the output includes the marker and adjust the wheel a tiny bit as necessary. 4. Flushing after use is essential. If the pathway gets gunked-up with dried chemical, it may be impossible to "un-gunk" because you won't be able to reach the clog. 5. "Another thing is the "pilot hole(??)" in the metering dial. It shoots water (I hope only water) out the hole all the time." Can be caused by either/both (a) shaking the sprayer up and down as you spray, and (2) air in the water line. Solution... don't shake the sprayer up and down, and purge the hose of air before attaching the sprayer.

I've been trying to give up using plastic bags since they are so bad for the environment, so I've been looking for some eco-earth bags that would leave me feeling a little less guilty. I found these bags that break down (unlike plastics) and I use them as trash bags. The main reason I bought these was that they had handles and would be easy to use with my "over the cabinet bag hanger." I've been using plastic t-shirt bags because they are small, and I like to take out the trash every day. I don't like using tall kitchen trash bags because I don't like garbage building up and getting stinky in my kitchen. These Pogi Poop bags are fantastic for an alternative. They are smaller than regular bags (see photo for reference) but they work just as good. The box comes with 300, but it's a compact box that stores in my cabinet well without being bulky. I was really surprised with how sturdy, thick, and strong the bags are. I was worried that they would be really thin and rip easily. I have tugged on them and stretched them, and yet they hold up great. They also hold up fantastic for heavy things too. I keep a couple of these bags in my purse as well for random needs. Sometimes they come in handy when I go to the grocery store. I use them occasionally as produce bags. I get weird looks, but hey! I'm doing it for the environment.

i liked this product until i read some reports about the bad effects on your health that are very possible from trehalose. i got sick not long after using this up. i wonder if it was related? i recommend against using it , based on articles that detail the detrimental health effects. From Dr Mercola's website: "In related news, researchers have also found an intriguing link between processed foods and drug-resistant disease. Here, a sugar called trehalose is a preferred fuel for two of the most problematic strains of clostridium difficile (C. diff), microbes that cause severe gut infection and can lead to death. Deaths associated with this infection increased fivefold between 1999 and 2007, in part due to C. diff strains having developed resistance against antibiotics. Moreover, researchers found that more virulent strains of C. diff were outcompeting less harmful strains inside the human gut. To determine how and why, they tested more than 200 different sugars and amino acids to see whether these more virulent strains were somehow able to use some food sources more efficiently than others and this is precisely what they found. 32 As reported by The New York Times:33 Trehalose occurs naturally in mushrooms, yeasts and shellfish, among other things. It has historically been expensive to use, but in the late 1990s a new manufacturing process made the sugar cheap. That was good news for companies that manufactured prepackaged foods, because trehalose works great for stabilizing processed foods, keeping them moist on the shelf and improving texture. Since about 2001, weve added loads of it to everything from cookies to ground beef. What Dr. [Robert] Britton and his colleagues contend is that, in doing so, weve inadvertently cultivated the most toxic C. diff strains, driving what has become a scourge of hospitals. As evidence, he points to the timing of recent C. diff epidemics. The virulent strains existed before 2000, but they didn't cause as many outbreaks. Only after large quantities of trehalose entered the food supply did they become this deadly Britton also found that mice infected with those virulent strains of C. diff that consumed the sugar fared worse than infected mice that were not fed the sugar What this research shows is that people should be considering the ecological impacts of food stuffs, Britton [says]. Our gut bacteria are being bombarded with things that we never ate or never ate in the concentrations we eat now.

The mower has some ergonomic issues that could be improved, the handle is too long which makes it a hassle to turn around in tight spots. The nose is too long, just infuriating when you are trying to get a stubborn blade of grass near the bottom of a tree going in head first. My other major issue is the blade is near flat and offers no suction, so long blades of grass do not get cut, just rolled over, compounding the problem for your next mow. The high lift blade is not available for this mower in the USA. Im a little frustrated. If the battery issue gets fixed and the high lift blade is offered it will change my tune. Update June 2018: new 5ah battery came in the mail just as the 4ah battery went caput. Ego customer service came through. I did have to wait 2 weeks for the new battery, but at least they honored the warranty. Only Time will tell on quality of the new battery. The blade issue still needs to be addressed, needs more lift.

Gosh, these things are expensive. As I said in another review, I hate every time I am required to order a new one. But for printing high-quality photos on Canon paper, these Canon inks can't be beaten. Color accuracy, operation, and the all-important "end result" makes these the item of choice whenever printing a high-quality image, and the "BK" color is as important as the primary ink colors. I use mine on a Canon Pixma iX6820, a printer that uses two black cartridges, including this one, and when I print in high-quality mode on Canon's best glossy or matte 13 x 19" (A3+) papers, it still shocks me at just how good the results are. All inkjet prints will fade over time, particularly if you have the print in an area where sun or humidity are at play, but these genuine Canon inks will stand up over time more than off-brands. Noteand this is very importantthat this is the "XL" version of the cartridge. It is the same physical size as the CLI-251 BK, but contains a higher quantity of ink than the standard cartridge. I try to purchase the "XL" versions whenever I can because of some increased (minor) economy, and the fact that the cartridge does not have to be exchanged quite so frequently. So, how do I handle the cost? I use third-party inks when printing handouts on plain paper for the office, but when printing photos, I stay with these Canon inks. In spite of the price, their superb, consistent performance make them a Five-Star item.

I bought some AmazonBasics index cards a few weeks ago and they were really thin (AmazonBasics Ruled Index Cards - 3x5 Inches (10 Packs of 100). I did not recall index cards being paper thin. Well, in a apparent bid to save costs, there are now two types of index cards -- thick and thin. Note Amazon seems to be mixing card types in a single product page; I'm currently reviewing the "AmazonBasics Heavy Weight Ruled Index Cards, White, 3x5-Inch, 300-Count". Be careful what you order. It might depend on your specific project, but in my case, I was looking to make flashcards. The thicker ones, a.k.a. AmazonBasics Heavy Weight Ruled Index Cards, feel much better in my hands. Thinner index cards tend to have line bleed on the blank side and it's also easier to read through them. For flashcards, that's a no-no. The slightly more heft is just nicer overall and you do pay a price premium for it. Otherwise, these came neatly packed, and the line side has "college" rule blue lines with a single red line at the top. The cards are made in the USA and are reasonably priced. Lesson learned: make sure to check the "thickness" or paper "weight" before buying index cards again.

Yucca is amazing. Particularly for flushing and keeping organics and fertilizers in suspension with one another, preventing nutrient lock-up. This explains why compost teas made with yucca foam like crazy when shaken/mixed. It also feeds the microbes without causing any blooms, because it is made up of long chain sugars which are slow to be consumed by the microbiome. I'll say it again, Yucca is amazing. It is a secret held by many good composting companies selling soil. Look for Yucca on the ingredients label of good compost However, I have one complaint. I have overdone it with yucca (put in about 5-10x the recommended amount on accident), and the soil began locking out water. The water literally would bead off the surface of the soil about 1 inch down, and would not penetrate even when I would mix the wet top layer with the beading under layer. It would literally take about 10 gallons of water to soak through the barrier to water one of my squash bushes which was watered with the overpowered mixture. DO NOT OVERDO IT WITH YUCCA. It won't cause any nutrient lockout, but it will cause insane water beading causing runoff to other plants and a water proof barrier of soil over the plant you're trying to water. Very weird. A little bit of Yucca goes a long way, but a lot of yucca causes water proof soil. If you're still reading this, it may interest you that the beading from excessive yucca use is the same as using oyster mushrooms for plant mulch. I found mulching with pleurotus ostreatus mushroom fungus causes the same kind of "beading and water lockout" effect, preventing water from getting through the top layer of soil.

There are quite a few variations of these lights, even if they look the same and have the same branding. I have one set that was ordered as a gift in December that is vastly different from the set I ordered in January. The key differences are: -The December set has a thick grounded power cord where the January set has a thin, vulnerable, ungrounded power cord. -The December set has smooth RGB mixing with very good color palettes. The January set is jerky on intensity adjustments. -The December set has 6 DMX channels where the January set has 7 Channels. The programming for Mode (channel 6) is completely different between the two, and the addition of the seventh channel for mode speed is strange. Thankfully, my DMX application is flexible, so I added the second batch of fixtures as a 7 channel mode to the same fixture type. On the whole, these lights are incredibly bright. The cases feel relatively durable, though the XLR DMX connectors may not be. The quality of what you receive seems to be luck of the draw. These have fans in them, where the cheaper lights have higher speed fans... the better built lights have slower fans. If you get the lights with the thin mains lead, I would definitely replace that ASAP.

After retirement, I use them for the constant notes Im making about ideas for things Im building in my workshop and things I need to keep track of. I have a smartphone and multiple tablets and laptops, but the cards in my shirt pocket are handier, don't need to be charged and contain more info at a glance in one hand while Im holding parts together with my other hand. These do allow my nearly hairless doggy to do her business in the backyard in relative comfort, and even to take walks, in the winter in our New England city. Just keeping the salt and slush off her paws keeps them "warm". And these actually stay on! We've tried numerous other boots on her in previous years, some very expensive ($35-60 worth!), but none would stay on her as she walked. These "boots" work out to be over a dollar each. They are basically extra thick balloons. Although I will pay because I don't have a better alternative, I did give only 4 stars because the price really does seem ridiculous. She isn't thrilled to have them put on, but she is fine once they are, and actually seems to dread them less than her much more expensive, fancy boots. She almost seems to appreciate that these paw-balloons let her access the outdoors again! Putting them on isn't too hard. Maybe my years of shoeing toddlers helps give me an edge there, because I notice a lot of people complaining here about them being too difficult to put on. Hint: it helps to put your fingers under the paw, in between the large pad and the toes, to guide them into the boot. Dogs are incredibly sensitive on the top of their paws, so working from below minimizes the flinching response, and actually gives you more flexible control of the placement of their toes into the boot, so you can avoid catching the claws on the rubber. It also helps if you have a reasonably cooperative dog... You can save a lot of wear and tear on the Pawz, as well as make it easier getting the dog's paws into the Pawz, if you keep the claws cut short and filed smooth. I have to clip my dog's claws about twice as often as normal, when using Pawz.

The tubing and supplies arrived on time, however, had it been packaged differently, I would have given it 5 stars... The tubing is coiled in a ziploc baggy with the rest of the supplies, but zip-ties are used to keep the tubing coiled very tightly. I would think that this is done to keep the tubing from unraveling and becoming a mess in the bag, but this should be done LIGHTLY with twist-ties, NOT cranked down zip-ties because as you can see in the photos, it causes major indents in the tubing - which compromise the strength of the line. This tubing was bought to attach to a CO2 tank for our aquarium, so we're going to have to cut those sections out to ensure we don't get any leaks. Overall, it is a good deal for everything included, but a little more thought and care needs to be taken in how it's packaged!

My 115 year old stone foundation house has mice. Not a big deal, keep a couple traps baited, when bait goes missing, or I catch a mouse, step up the baiting. Usually in the fall I get the most activity. This November, I saw a rat on the security camera in the basement...yikes, not the same thing. Got this to get him. That country rat never went in. Tried blue cheese, sesame seeds (which he would eat on the floor) dried cranberries and dry cat food. He would not go in. Not even close. I finally got him with the first wood victor I bought after it was in place for 4 weeks. This smart rat would not touch anything new. cheese/seed combo tempted him after all that time in the wood victor neck snapper. So, why 5 stars? because it is the best mouse trap I have. The day after the rat was gone, I saw a mouse on the camera, he went right in and died. Two days later. One mouse, one kill. Eight days later, one mouse on camera, one dead. It seems this is 100% first day I see a mouse on camera, he is caught. I have a roller bucket trap, plastic snappers, and wood snappers, all have caught mice, but it usually takes a couple days, they steal the bait. Not with the electric death house/ This thing has killed first day, three for three sightings. Easy to clean. I use the bait that was in it when my killing streak began. A tiny crumble of blue cheese, maybe 5 sesame seeds, 5 dried cat food pieces and a dried cranberry. Poor mice, just get to smell their last meal.

Anyway, as a result of my research here on Amazon, I finally found a different fly paper product, named "Sticky Fly Ribbons,Fly Catcher Ribbon,Fly Paper Ribbon,Fly Paper Strips,Super Value 16 Packs by JCHope", and that product works about twice as well as this product, because the adhesive is stronger and more uniformly applied, and because the adhesive has an odor that attracts house flies more quickly. This is my 2nd order. I was not aware of any termite problem in my property, so I installed the 1st set to check the presence of termites around my house. About a half of the previously installed stakes popped up in 2 months, but in a close inspection, there was no termite inside the tubes. I suspect, as some other reviewers suggested, it was the moist in the soil that decomposed the cardboard material holding the spring-loaded indicator pin. Or it might be just that all termites went on a vacation when I checked. Nevertheless, since you'll install these in wet soil, the false-positive problem will prevail. I'm re-installing these again one more time, just in case. Whether it was real or false positive, two rounds of treatment should take care of my worries if this were to be cost effective termite solution.

I hate to say it, but I love this stuff. Ive tried the gentler stuff and I keep coming back to this. As soon as it gots hot in the San Diego desert the flies come out in full force; big and little. They are into people and especially your ears. Thank goodness they don't bite but they can drive you to distraction. Obviously Im not going to spray it around my head so I spray a little on my fingers and dab it around my ears. That seems to do the job. It lasts all day too. Ive also started spraying my hat real good. The top and bottom of the visor and all over the outside. I let it dry then put it back on. If you put it on wet you can smell the fumes, but not after its dry. For my pugs good ole Amazon has Flys-Off made for dogs and cats. I spray some on my hand and pet their head, ears and under their chin. It works great. At first one pug would get upset but after she realized the benefit she now appreciates it. The other pug just gives me a thanks mom look. It does work but isn't recommended for humans. For my sinuses Amazon has Xlear a saline spray with a couple mild additives that is incredible. It even got my ancient saliva glands going again! It comes in a pump or squeeze bottle. So bugs beware...were not afraid to use the tough stuff. Watch out or Ill plug in my 70s electric bug zapper and then youll be real sorry! (Also available you know where!)

The Black OtterBox SYMMETRY SERIES Case for the Google Pixel 2 XL fits the phone snuggly while offering openings for the charging port, fingerprint reader and the camera. The case material is hard and slightly textured with a more pliable, rubbery material on the sides of the phone where you would squeeze the phone. The case does add a bit of bulk to the width of the phone as well as the thickness since the case rises up past the sides of the phone to protect the screen. The buttons on the side of the phone are covered by the case and you must press pretty hard on the case to press the buttons. This is a real disadvantage to the OtterBox case in my opinion since pressing the buttons shouldnt be so difficult. I was able to easily install and remove the OtterBox black case. I also got the clear case which I don't recommend because I couldn't get it on my phone. It was either just too small or just too rigid to install. Prior to the OtterBox, I used a Spigen Neo Hybrid Herringbone case which was pliable enough on the sides that the buttons could be easily pressed. And the Spigen case protected my phone through multiple drops while running on pavement. The Spigen case is pretty torn up, but the phone and especially the glass remain in perfect condition. The OtterBox case is nice and definitely gets 4 stars. It would get 5 stars if it wasn't for the difficulty in pressing the buttons with the case on the phone. Based on the difficulty in pressing the buttons with the OtterBox case, I would recommend the Spigen Neo Hybrid Herringbone case instead.

My blood is boiling. I am pissed off. That means I am mad. That means I am angrier than angry. Hurtta has failed me miserably with this product. Mind you, I do not only criticise, but I also give credit where it is due. You can check out my past reviews. I have praised where compliments were deserved. I also wrote a glowing review of Hurttas Torrent raincoat for dogs. A few hours ago, I received the Hurtta dogs boots I ordered, and I put them on my dog. They were just far t o o o o big. And I mean BIG. Before I placed my order, I measured my dogs front and hind paws. C-o-r-r-e-c-t-l-y, OK? I know exactly how to do it. The width of each of my dogs front paws is 2; that for the hind paws, 1 15/16. So, I ordered a pair said to be for 2 (meant for the hind feet) and a pair said to be for 2 (meant for the front feet). On Hurtta's size chart, the former is designated Size M; the latter, Size L. Size M, which is actually meant for the hind feet, is usable for the front feet. Size L, which is meant for the front feet, is, I am absolutely certain, not even usable for the particular Hurtta designers whatsit. Please take a look at image A. The boot on the left, made by a competitor, is rated Size 3 and is meant for paw width of 2. It fits my dogs front foot. The boot on the right is Hurttas. It is rated Size L and is meant, as stated on their own size chart, for paw width of 2. Remember, the width of each of my dogs front paws is 2. You compare the difference in size, and right away you can see why Hurttas is just too darn big. And take a look at the extra length of the strap. Incidentally, that tape measure of mine is made in Germany and not in some Third World country; therefore, it must be accurate. Now take a look at image B. That was taken from this site. The idea imparted of the strap fitting nicely is deceiving. You can tell by looking at the upper part of the boot. The strap has not been tightened around that part! Now look at image A again. The boot on the left has two straps. The Velcro runs the whole length of each strap as well as on those parts of the boot where they come in contact with. There is great holding power. Whereas with Hurttas, there is only one strap, and it is held by a piece of Velcro cut out in the shape of the letter H (presumably standing for Hurtta) fixed on the underside of the boot. Furthermore, when you tighten the upper part of the boot sufficiently to ensure a tight fit at your dogs ankle, the strap will go in a slanting way and, therefore, it will come in contact with only part and not the whole of the H. All that means far less holding power. And you want to know what also makes my blood boil? Ill tell you. It is a big hassle for me to return any item I order that proves unacceptable. I live in the mountains, and it is a tiring ride to and fro the nearest city and to go from here to there to somewhere else in the city to do what I need to do to return the unusable item and then later to collect the replacement. So, it is US$70+ (including shipping cost, etc) down the drain for me. You can go ahead and buy these boots to try out if it is not inconvenient for you to return something you have ordered and to wait for a replacement. And good luck to you.

Everything about Donkey Kong Country Tropical Freeze is improved upon from its direct predecessor Donkey Kong Country Returns. We experience the realm of DK for the first time in High-Definition. The graphics and worlds are so much more lush and there is so much to explore and unlock in each level. Retro Studios couldn't go wrong by bringing back David Wise, the composer from the original DKC series in the mid 1990's, and it really shows with how immersive and memorable the music is. The worlds and enemies are distinct from each other and this has always been a strongpoint of the DKC series when compared to other platformers. I'm enthralled by the return of Dixie Kong this time around and I hope she is here to stay. Cranky Kong is also a playable character but I try to avoid using him as I find his abilities harder to control than Diddy Kong's jet-pack or Dixie's hovering ponytail. As for the animal buddies, we are still left with just Rambi The Rhino and some background appearances from Squawks The Parrot. I sorely miss using Enguarde The Swordfish during the underwater levels among some of the other animals which had extremely useful abilities to help you soar through levels. Retro Studios did away with the hoard of Tiki characters from DKC Returns and instead replaced them with Nordic Animal Vikings threatening to freeze DK Isle for good. While I like the Viking enemies much better than the Tiki characters that were used before, many of us DKC veterans are still left longing for the return of the rightful villains to the DKC universe, King K. Rool and the Kremlings. Are they really owned by Rare? Is this the reason for their unexplained absence 2 times in a row? My only other complaint, and this is a massive one, is the extreme difficulty of the game following the same issue with DKC Returns. Now, you're hearing from someone who completed all 3 DKC games from the original series on the SNES and the GBA versions respectively. Even the Donkey Kong Land series on the Game Boy and the Game Boy Color version of the original. Yet something in the translation from straightforward 2D to 2.5D has made these games nearly impossible to master in the later stages of the game. Same thing with DKC Returns, except they gave us Super Donkey Kong to complete the levels for us if we were unable to do so ourselves after multiple attempts. Such feature is absent this time around and there was really no reason to remove it because of how hard this game becomes. Even in the early stages, you can sense how hard this game is going to get later on. It's not even funny, I was stuck on the 5th World for over a year with this game. I'm not removing any stars for this, but simply pleading with Retro Studios to make the game more accessible for beginner to intermediate players in the next sequel. Now in 2018, with the upcoming port of this game coming out the Nintendo Switch, perhaps the inclusion of Funky Kong as a playable character will make this game easier to master. I can only hope. This was an incredibly hard game where I didn't exactly feel like I accomplished anything significant once I beat it, just sheer exhaustion. Still, this is an awesome game and memorable addition to the longtime series.

I did so much research on which die cutting machine I should purchase, even asking friends who own different brands and came to the conclusion that this was the die cutter for me. Here's why... hope this helps others who are looking. 1. The smaller size allows me to run materials through with less cranking of the handle. 2. The handle is built to reduce the number of cranks you need to do to run the material through. 3. It turned out that I work better by running through a couple of dies at a time (if they are thinlets, shapelets etc.) and although I was concerned the 6" base would be too small, it holds all the dies I want to run through for that color and then some. 4. The side fold up making storage easy. This also makes it easy to transport. Sometimes I want to craft in my studio, other times in front of the TV, so it's super easy to pickup (via the handle) and place wherever I want. 5. The brand is Spellbinder which is very high quality, so I know it will probably last me forever. 6. The die cutter comes with everything you need to get started. Just purchase the dies you want to use for cutting and you're good to go. 6. They sell the plastic replacement cutting plates in a set and as of the writing of this review, they are affordably priced. 7. It came with a super easy to understand instruction booklet about how to setup the plates and the base plate even has assembly instructions for how to make the sandwich of plates for whatever you are trying to cut with. I am a total newbie at this and didn't know what people were talking about with the sandwich of plates and this manual got me from newbie to expert in a matter of minutes. 8. It is heavy which is good! This helps to keep it in place while running the dies around. That way it's not sliding around all over the table. Yeah, I can't say enough good things about this little cutting machine. I own a Silhouette Curio and a Silhouette Cameo, (which I LOVE!) but nothing cuts felt like this machine. It's a work horse and handles well. This is my new favorite tool in the studio. Very Highly Recommended!

I love these pouches. I don't know why, but my son prefers eating applesauce out of the little cup containers, but I personally like buying these squeeze packets because I know he won't spill applesauce everywhere--especially when we are snacking in the car on long road trips. I do urge parents when they buy these in bulk to take a little taste of their kids' snack packet once every few packets to make sure that they are still good. Every now and then, it happens that the vacuum in these sealed foods fail and then air gets in and the food can rot and go moldy. (Then you see these images go viral online.) But if that were to happen to a pack of bacon or a jar of sauce, you could see through the package that the contents have started to go back b/c the seal failed -- and I've definitely seen the occasional package of food on the grocery shelf that's suffered this fate but hadn't yet been tossed by the staff. The difference with these squeeze packets though is that you can't see when the seal has failed and the food is bad--the only way to test is to taste it. Meanwhile, I really love the flavor of the banana applesauce. It's not my favorite combination, but it's nice to mix it up with the plain applesauce or the peach applesauce flavors.

My dachshund loves toys, especially those that make noise. This Babble Ball appealed to me because it looked like it could live up to his roughness (he tends to destroy toys within minutes), and it was something different than a squeaker toy. I bought the size recommended on the site for my dog's weight. When the toy came, he loved it. Even though it was hard, he was able to get it into his mouth and bring it to me to play fetch. After a few rounds of fetch, he knocked it around the room. Again, he loved it. The sounds were annoying to me, but he loved it so it was fine. However, about 10 minutes into his playing with it by himself, I noticed that the room was suddenly quiet. I looked up, and my dog had the toy in his mouth -- stuck there. There was no danger of it going down his throat -- the ball was too big -- but he got it into his mouth so tightly surrounded by his teeth that he couldn't get the ball out. He was stuck there looking at me with his mouth wide open and the ball shoved into it. I immediately ran to him to get it out (which took a few seconds -- it was really stuck), and proceeded to immediately throw the ball in the trash. I shudder to think how long he may have been like that, mouth wide open with a ball stuck into it, if I hadn't been right there. So, be very careful with a ball this hard that has no "give" to it. If it gets firmly into your dog's mouth, they cannot squish down on it to make it smaller to get it out. I feel like a catastrophe was averted here.

At age 10 1/2, my 65-pound, always energetic Labradoodle has finally started to show her age. She can still bound around the woods on our hikes, but jumping up in the car has gotten to be challenging. I have an SUV, so jumping up to the back seat is quite a leap. (Jumping down is not a problem.) I put her on a joint supplement, but she was still having trouble getting in the car. So I got this stairs set. It snapped together with some encouragement from a rubber mallet. It's very sturdy, and the fact that it's wide makes it better for a big dog. I put the stairs in place by the car. The dog was a little tentative the first time, and I patted the top step a couple of times to encourage her, and she gingerly made her way up the two steps and then easily climbed the rest of the way into the car. The second time, she used the steps more readily, and again ***easily*** made her way to the back seat. I am no longer having to spend 5 minutes coaxing her into jumping up and then watching her struggle to make that one big leap. This is the perfect aide for my old girl. Highly recommended.

My cat has Inflammatory Bowel Disease, so finding food that he can digest is a huge challenge. For a long time, all I've been able to feed him was frozen raw meat (usually raw chicken or turkey) because he is so sensitive. It worked, but it's so hard to keep up with. You have to worry about proper food storage, thawing it out, using it up before it goes bad, cleaning every surface it touches... and it's hard to find someone comfortable handling raw meat if you have to go away for a few days. I recently started feeding my cat Stella & Chewy's freeze-dried raw. I read the ingredients carefully, because there is so much he can't have. Even the smallest addition of grains or vegetables can make him extra sick for days. But I was pleased to find that it truly is mostly raw meat, organs, and bones - the best kind of diet for any cat, not just a sensitive one. He was a little hesitant about the texture at first (both wet and dry), but it didn't take long for him to accept it. I've been watching him carefully for a couple of months now, and so far he seems to be doing just as well with it as he did with the frozen raw. It's such a relief, because while this is certainly more expensive than frozen raw, it's so much more convenient. Not to mention it's easier to come by, especially since I can set up a monthly Amazon subscription and forget about it. He hates the salmon/cod flavor, but so far chicken, duck, and turkey are all winners. I'm so glad I took a chance on this food.

This is a preliminary review: we've assembled the crate (with very little difficulty), but haven't used it yet. All the pieces were in the box, it was well and safely packed - the box had a bad hole in one corner, but the crate sides/top/bottom were undamaged. There is a very small scratch on one edge of the top, but nothing the stain pen I use for scratches on my floor won't fix - if I even bother. We did notice a little smell to it, so it's on a patio table for the night, off-gassing. I expected that because I read the reviews, and it doesn't worry me because after a couple of hours, the smell is already almost completely dissipated. Other than that, the pieces fit snugly, the holes were all lined up properly, and it looks just the way we hoped it would. Our plan is to use this in our travel trailer. That way, we have an extra table surface, and we don't have to carry the home crate back and forth - which gets the dogs too excited. (We have two Westies, and they share a crate, so we have the medium size; it's just a bit bigger than their home crate, which is cloth.) Because the floor is rigid, we will have to fold up a towel or blanket to fill the "gap" that the slide-out floor leaves, but we have to do that for the other crate, too, so it's no biggie. We think this crate will be warmer when we're camping in colder weather. Our dogs are 4 years old, and not chewers, so we took off the plexiglas door covering (and replaced the screws so there wouldn't be holes that we'd have to try to fill). We think circulation will be fine when it's warmer where we're camping. The color looks to us just like it does in the picture. We like the way the door opens fully inward (as well as outward), so we can leave it open during the day. Our dogs don't take toys in, so mat readjustment for pulling the door back out to close it at night is not going to be a problem for us. I'll try to remember to update this review after we've used the crate a few times. We *are* going to take the crate pad from home the first couple of times, or use the new pad at home for a while, so that the pad, at least, smells familiar to the dogs. In theory that will make getting used to the new crate easier. Also, it's a little taller than the one we used to take, but it should still fit under the dinette table where we normally set it at camp. We are really looking forward to having this in the trailer - it looks really nice. :-)

Worked like a charm in an iPhone 6S and 6S+ (both of which are Verizon phones in USA). Did not pre-activate. Also did not start trip in UK. We just powered down while on the flight over the pond, removed the Verizon SIM card and installed the Three SIM. Upon landing in Italy, had immediate voice (only to UK numbers though), text and 3G data. We had no issues with iMessage and FaceTime; they self-registered with Apple right away. This SIM also worked similarly in France. Received 4G LTE and sometimes 3G once we arrived in UK. Other parties in our group were roaming on Vodaphone and Orange networks during this Italy/France/UK trip and we noted a few occasions where they had better coverage than on the Three network, but overall this Three SIM worked great. The only thing we had to work around during the first two countries was the inability to call Italian and French phone numbers without topping up; and there was no way to top up until we got to UK. There are Three stores in Italy and France but they could not top up a UK travel SIM. Nor can it be done online using a US based credit card. However, using a VoIP app (like Skype with a few dollars on the account) allowed us to make voice calls using cellular data to Italian and French phone numbers. WhatsApp also worked. On the flight back home, we reversed the SIM swap process over the ocean and Verizon re-activated immediately upon landing in USA. iMessage and FaceTime also worked immediately back on Verizon.

Original 1 star review: I'm extremely disappointed with this hose. Within 15 minutes of it arriving I removed it from the packaging and hooked it up to my hose reel. I then filled it with water as recommended on the water right website, and left it stretched out in the sun for an hour or two. Upon winding it back on the hose reel however, it developed a PERMANENT kink about 1/2 from the end of the strain reliever. This kink completely blocks the waterflow unless I unwind the whole hose from the reel every time I want to use it, and even then I have to massage it back into shape repeatedly to keep it from closing back up. I bought this hose because it wasn't supposed to do this. Additionally, I went to Water Right's website and used their support form, but nobody ever responded. I can find no warranty information either. As it stands, it's going back. UPDATE: Water right finally did get back to me when I emailed them directly. It looks like my initial request got caught by their spam filters or something. I ended up repairing the hose simply by heating the kink with a heat gun, not too hot, still safe to touch, and that caused the kink to spring back to its initial shape. Once cooled, I see no weak spot there or tendency to crimp in the same place. I've re-wound the hose on the reel taking a little extra care not to bend the first course of hose too acutely. It's performing as it should and I've seen no recurrence of the problem.
SATISFIED.

-1 star for poor print bed design, -1 star for customer service I got this for Christmas, as a CAD operator since 95 I have dreamed of having my own 3D printer since I first saw one at GE 22 years ago, but that one was a 1/4 of a mil. For the money I think it is a good 3D printer but it has its flaws, especially in customer services. Heat Bed Problem See picture(and terrible customer service) The head bed helps your printer stick to the print bed, without it your print could pop off or it would warp up. I notice after having my printer for 2 weeks the prints started warping that's when I noticed the bed wasn't heating. There are 4 wires coming out of the printer to the build plate 2 for heating 2 for temperature reading. One of the wires got snagged because the bed moves in and out and the wires feed through a grommet. I contacted mono price, I emailed them, they never called me. I tried to use the chat thing and they canceled the chat session, I have the email to prove it. I didn't want to fix it myself as I was afraid of voiding my warranty. In the end I had to rewire it, because after I fixed the red wire the black wire snapped. I ended up by passing the feed, adding wire on the outside of the unit, haven't had a problem sense. Pros Its Cheap Does a good job Easy to set up Cons Its cheap Terrible customer service (or lack of customer service) Heat bed is a terrible design SD card reader only works on FAT, no SDHC cards useable Very little documentation Doesnt play nice with USB printing Leveling the print bed is a PITA (but once leveled you're good) Bottom Line If you're thinking about getting into 3D printing and handy with a soldering iron and you don't want to spend much this is the printer for you. If you have the money though I recommend buying something better. I plan on using mine for a year but then I will probably sell it and use the cash to buy a bigger and better unit, something not Monoprice, which makes me sad because I am a huge fan of Monoprice and buy lots of things from them, but I never needed tech support in the past. Tips for New Users SD Card the SD card reader is small, its fine but if you want a second one with more storage get a 2g card and format it to FAT. I wasted too much time to find out that SDHC cards don't work, as there is 0 documentation telling you about the card reader. Print Bed The print bed will wear out over time and tear, you have lots of options to replace it, you could use painters tape, or buy a replacement bed or use glass. A 5x7 picture frame at the dollar store works, clamp it down with binders clip. On the glass you can use hairspray for a light hold or Elmers Glue Sticks, the glue sticks are made from PLA so PLA prints will hold strong on them. Removing Prints Prints are hard to pop off if you have a good stick, the plastic print tool it comes with won't get under most prints. What I do is I get it started with a razor scraper blade and then use the plastic tool the remove the rest, just be careful to keep a low angle as to not cut into the print bed. If you move to a glass then is less of an issue, and FYI yes the glass will transfer the heat. What to print? If you don't know how to draw in 3D you can download STL sites from many different sites, you will not run out of things to print, and some prints that are 2 big for your print bed you can scale down. How do you print? - You get an STL file then then you open it with a slicer program, this printer comes with Cura but there are other ones out there. In the slicer you set up how you want it to print, you can print fine layers for a better looking print or a thick layers for a lower resolution stronger print. You can say what temperature to print and even how much material you want to print between the shell. 10-20% will be fine for most things. The slicer program will generate a gcode file, you copy the file onto your SDcard (or you can print straight to your printer with a USB cable (but pray it doesnt fail)) then in the printer you pick the gcode file and tell it to print. Hope this helps!

I really hate the fact that there is such a mix of products on this Amazon product page. I'm only Reviewing the K51 white keyboard and I've not tried the other products that this review will be mixed up with. This is sold as a hybrid mechanical keyboard. It feels nothing like a mechanical keyboard. There is no clickiness to it. You press the keys smoothly till they bottom out. They key caps are closer to the style you often find on mechanical keyboards, but this is a membrane keyboard through and through. Membrane keyboards are cheaper and more liquid resistent. I currently have 3 mechanical keyboards and there's no comparison. I'm not saying this is a bad keyboard--but if you want a mechanical, get a mechanical! That being said, this is a very nice backlit keyboard. I wanted a wired keyboard for the computer the kids use in the living room and I wanted it to be backlit so it can be used when others are watching movies. I also didn't want a mechanical because I wanted a quieter keyboard. This fits all my needs and is a nice keyboard! Also, I have three girls who LOVE the rainbow colors. You have six different options of different types of motion or two options of solid color. There is barely any customization for the six motion options except you can sometimes change whether it moves right to left or left to right. You can also always change the brightness. All of the motions are too quick for me, but the kids love them. The two options that aren't motion are solid color all across the keyboard and one where you can program each of the six zones to different colors. I don't think programming the zones to different colors is worth it because if the keyboard is unplugged or the computer is turned off, the settings are all reset. I just use the solid color option myself. Each key does not have it's own backlight. There are six zones from left to right across the keyboard. This doesn't really matter, but it means you can't just light up wasd or anything like that. The backlight is very bright, so having the zones doesn't cause issues unless you expect to be able to light specific keys only. The good side of that is because it's so simple you don't have software to install, and you can set it however you want with a couple of keystrokes. The biggest downside is that it doesn't save your settings if power is lost or it's unplugged. Not only that, the default setting is a rainbow motion setting. It would be nice if the default setting was a solid color option, but all I have to do to fix it is to hit "FN-5". So it isn't too bad. Overall, I like it pretty well and my kids love it. We had a pretty boring keyboard before this, but it go messed up by spills over time. I think we're going to get a good life out of this and my kids think the colors are amazing!

Flimsy, cheap construction. The base is solid enough, BUT, the heavy shelves are held only by a flimsy thin support rod in the center. The longer thin flimsy support rod is "connected" to the base by a small connector. Even tightened as far as it could go, the rod is flimsy & flexes too much, and does NOT keep the shelves steady. On top of THAT, the instructions state "turn the shelves until they lock down" -- yet the shelves don't "screw" onto the rod. Their center hole just slides onto the support rod. You can't "screw them until they lock" because there's nothing to lock down ON. The "trunk" is flimsy plastic with sharp edges (that I cut my finger on as I was putting this thing together), and the attached leaves are cheap & poorly attached -- my cats detached a number of leaves just by batting at them. Even with the wood hubs of the shelves fitted into the plastic trunk pieces and everything tightened as far as they could go (and the shelves simply would not tighten, no matter what -- again, they simply slide onto the support rod), the entire tree flexed and barely stayed upright. Our smallest cat -- a small, 8 pound tabby -- jumped onto the top shelf from a nearby regular cat tree and nearly knocked this tree over. Luckily we were still standing right there & caught the tree before it fell over with our kitty on it. Needless to say, we've taken it apart, packed it up, and are returning it. For the price, this item is a major ripoff. It's a dangerous hazard for adult cats and I wouldn't trust it with kittens, either. There's much better trees available for the cost of this one. The seller should be ashamed to sell this. It's a shame. It's a cool looking tree & a good idea, but badly executed.

I bought this for my recently acquired Squire Vintage Modified 70's Strat. Firstly, there is very little difference between the two bridges, in constructions or materials, though the thicker block on this trem bridge is what I was after. Replacement was straight forward except for several very noticeable QC issue with this product: 1. the pilot hole position for the whammy bar in the block was drilled at an angle so the hole was not aligned under the base plate hole and there was no way for ANY trem bar to be inserted. It appears the block moved in the drilling jig. That happens but not discarding the block is a major QC lapse. Second, the base plate was not flat. The corner at the back on the trem bar end was bent down a slight amount (~1mm). Depending on what I wanted, this would have made set up tricky. I could have hammered it flat, but again, the part appears to be identical to that on the Squire, so I just replaced the base plates (upgraded my MIM-level trem base plate with a Squire base plate?). Next, the saddles on both bridges are stamped steel (not cast) and marked "Fender". However, the chrome on this bridge was poor and very thin and discolored. In contrast, the chrome on the Squire saddles (also stamped and marked "Fender") was a bit thicker, shiny and overall looked a LOT better. Again, I upgraded my new MIM-level bridge with my old Squitre parts. The Squire trem arm does not fit the new bridge. This was anticipated, but why buy a new (thinner) trem arm when I had a perfectly good one on the Squire? So, I drilled out the hole, thouhg slightly undersized, and tapped with a 6mm tap. My trem arm fits perfectly with none of the slop common to even American Strats, but still easily swings out of the way when I want it to - no wobble, backlash or wiggle. Overall, the QC is unacceptably poor on this product, but combined with a few superior components from my Squire VM, However, with a little "re-engineering", I now have an awesome sounding guitar with a lot more sustain than with the original trem bridge, and a better trem arm set up than Fender offers on even it's upscale Strats. If you're only looking to upgrade the thin, cheap block, on a Squire VM (I do not know if the same Squire quality bridge is on other Squires), I DO NOT recommend buying this and recommend you buy an after market steel block for less than this bridge costs.

Wireless is the way to go. I was going to buy a 3rd party charge stand and then realized I'd need to buy a Fast Charge capable transformer to replace my original that I prefer to keep in my laptop bag for travel use. Buying a new 2A transformer which most of the 3rd party wireless chargers require you to do immediately runs the cost of the "lower cost" 3rd party stands up to within a couple of bucks of the cost of the Samsung. If you don't travel and/or don't mind using the Samsung transformer that came with your phone, then there are lower cost charging stand options out there. I also just happened to catch a momentary price drop on Amazon which actually made this cost about the same or a little less (I would have bought it anyway without the price drop), so for me, the included transformer made this just perfect and I got an original Samsung instead of...? Fast charges while the S7 remains in my Spigen Rugged Armor case and does so with the phone vertical or horizontal. I love not having to mess with the cord anymore. This just sets on the hutch above my home office desk within easy reach and if I see the phone is low, all I have to do is just set it on the stand to charge. No muss; no fuss. The charger stops charging when the phone is topped off as it's supposed to, so my phone is ready to go and I can interrupt charge / restart without even thinking about it when I need to grab the phone.for a call or whatever. Being round (4.5" dia) on the business end, it is a little larger than I feel it needs to be which is about the only annoying thing about it, but the extra size probably helps a little bit if you rotate the phone between vertical and horizontal for charging which I do if I happen to be using a wired accessory. It has decent heft to it and is solid and well finished. It includes an internal cooling fan that comes on in charge mode but is dead silent so if you want this in the bedroom, go ahead. Mine is black on black and very unobtrusive in appearance despite the size/shape. With the phone charging a relatively low brightness blue LED comes on just below the small shelf the phone rest on. When fully charged, it changes to Green. The outer "ring" is finished with a rubber soft feel coating which just keeps everything from sliding around if you're not paying attention. I like the convenience of wireless charging so much, I'm thinking of picking up the "pad" version which lays flat instead of upright just to travel with so I can just set the phone down wherever I am and charge.

This medicinal food is just what I needed to bring my fish back from the brink of death. I have a tank of about 20 loaches, and one of the clown loaches was obviously skinnying. All of his tank makes were plump and racing around and he was swimming up and down the tank glass and his head was sunken in. I fed this food exclusively for about three weeks, to the whole tank, even though the label says only three days, because he was in such rough shape and I was worried I was really going to lose him. He was one of the original six I bought five years ago and I was pretty attached to him. I can safely say, about three weeks out from the end of this treatment, the entire tank is doing great and my skinny clown loach is back on the road to good health. His belly can actually get round when full its amazing what a difference this food has made for this fish. I think all keepers of wild-caught fish commonly known to carry parasites should treat with this medicinal food as a matter of good fish keeping. Put the medicine right where it is needed: in the fishs gut. Plus, since I was feeeling nothing else for three weeks, it helped to know it was a quality food I was feeding and the tank was not being malnourished during treatment. NLS is good stuff! LOVE LOVE LOVE! Now, I feed it maybe once a week, and only a little as a supplement. I will treat all new fish from now on with this product.

This review is regarding the cartridge used on an HP 4500 printer that is in excellent condition. I usually print approximately 20 pages per week in a small home office and have always used HP cartridges until recently. For years I've been frustrated by how expensive ink is and how fast they need to be replaced. It makes my reasonably priced printer an expensive piece of equipment. HP claims approximately 200 printed pages per cartridge, which is about 7 1/2 cents per page. I'm not sure what they mean by "approximately," but I believe we should get within 20% of their rating either way; 180 to 220 pages. Then, I did some work for a client that included being reimbursed for supplies, so I had to log every page I printed for months. After using 5 of the regular size HP cartridges, they averaged less than 150 pages. In other words, I paid about 10 cents per page. Wow! That's a big difference if you print a lot. I scoured the Internet to read other user's experiences, talked to HP, and bought cartridges from several sources before writing this review. Many other users report a similar experience to mine; it prints far fewer pages than HP advertises. Nobody commented they printed more than 200 pages, but it's less likely they would mention it online; so I decided to ignore it. I tried a couple of re-manufactured brands and did not see much of a difference in performance or quality, although one of them leaked slightly when I tried to install it and had to be thrown away. However after averaging the cost, they are still cheaper. My final decision is that the product from this vendor seemed to work as well as those I purchased through other sellers, but HP cartridges in general have a problem with meeting the buyer's expectations.

Ive been running this pump for two months now. It's a definite improvement over the single speed 1.5 hp pump that originally came with the pool. I didn't give it 5 stars because despite what Pentair claims, this pump is not "whisper quiet. At least not at 3000 rpm. Standing next to the pump when its running at the higher speeds and its noisier than the pump I replaced it with. I have the pump in a shed with the heater and filter so the noise is non-detectable from the pool deck anyways. But at the lower speeds, I can't hear anything when I walk by the shed. The installation was a breeze. This pump swap was like-for-like with the old one. I didn't have to make any electrical or plumbing modifications. Pentair supplies slip-fit unions on the suction and discharge ports. The electrical hook-up is a no-brainer. The only wire that has a designated connection is the ground (which of course is the green screw). The black and white wire leads can go on either terminal of the motor. No way can you make a mistake there. My supply was a pair of 15amp 120v breakers. My old pump ran at a constant 3400 rpm delivering 36 gpm at 7 psi. The Pentair pump at 3000 rpm pushes 44 gpm through the heater at 14 psi. The benefit here is more a greater volume of water going through the heater than before. And water is pulled through the skimmer much faster as well so the pool's surface cleans itself more effectively. Right now Im running the pump for the majority of the day at 1800 rpm. Just enough to keep the heater running now that the pool is up to temperature. Programming the run times and speeds for the pump are super-easy. The pump also has a "Quick Clean feature. No matter where the pump timer is in its cycle, pushing the "Quick Clean" button will ramp up the pump to whatever speed is best suited for cleaning the pool. As of this moment, the Pentair pump is a huge improvement over the what I had in my pump shed.

Last Friday night, we discovered our 80-lb rescue is terrified by thunderstorms. She crawled under the end table (it is open under the table) next to me, shaking for two hours and refused to budge until she determined it was over. My husband and I were concerned for three reasons: 1. This is the start of the thunderstorm season. In fact, fierce storms were expected on Tuesday (yesterday). 2. Intense fear like that is not good. 3. She could have gone under the end table on the other side of the sofa which has a glass bottom shelf. So, I spent a good part of Saturday goggling what we could do to help her. In addition to CDs that play soothing music with gradual louder levels of thunder, I found Thundershirts. My trainer had mentioned them as a way to reduce our dog's barking at people and especially dogs when she is in the car with us (in addition to counter conditioning). So, I read the reviews here--most glowing 5s. The only complaints were fitting problems and it not working. So, I thought why not, especially since it comes with a money back guarantee. I was thrilled to find I was eligible for free 1-day delivery and that I would get it on Sunday, giving some time to acclimate our dog to it. It arrived at 8 PM. I was excited. I read the instructions and called our dog. I put it on the floor to act as a treat plate and scattered some treats around. Then I picked it up and laid it across her back. She didn't budge. I attached the straps, adjusting several times to get it to fit her contours. She was an angel. When I was done, my husband took her outside to do her business. He was stunned at how docile she was. When he took the leash off, she walked up the stairs like a severely arithtic 14-year old dog instead of bolting up in 3 steps like the very active 3-year old she is. She laid down in her living room bed next to me and didn't budge for the couple hours. At 10, I took it off her. I tried to do some training with her, but she wasn't interested even though this involves super treats. Instead after about a minute, she retreated to her crate (a place she rarely goes) and wouldn't come out. She seemed to have totally lost her zest for life. I felt terrible. I immediately initiated a return. My husband moved her crate to another room, in case she associated it with the Thundershirt. It took about 12 hours for her to totally recover. We took it to UPS on Monday. Good riddance. We will stick with BAT (behavior adjustment training, where you give your dog sufficient distance from what scares her or him, and patiently let your dog how close to get and how fast--has worked well for us), counter conditioning and slowly acclimating her to thunder and fireworks using the CDs. No more instant fixes. And definitely no drugs like Prozac. We want a joyful dog, not a comatose dog. And, no, the shirt wasn't too tight. If anything it may have been too loose because of her body shape.

These casters are absolutely awesome... I built a solar rack out of heavy old pallet wood to replace one that had grown too small for my needs. (Plus, it got rid of some junk wood from the property!) But, with my old set of two fixed 5-inch wheels and a pivot point, the thing was nearly impossible to move as needed to track the sun manually. I was a bit wary of the price for these casters--when a set of four 5-inch casters costs as much as one of these--but I decided that a bigger, rubberized wheel would be better for the desert sand and rocks out here. At the last minute, I decided to get 4, instead of using two and a pivot. I am glad I did. With one of these casters at each corner, the rack moves so easy, far easier than my old rack with half the panels and much less than a quarter of the weight. The wheels do have an odor when they arrive, as mentioned previously. It was not too overwhelming at first, but after a few hours, the smell started bothering me with the package inside the house. I had to move them outside. These things are heavy duty, with a nice, thick steel plate. The pivot has a grease fitting to keep the casters spinning, but none for the wheel bearings. They should be sealed well enough to work, anyway. My only concern is how they will hold up over time in the sun, which is absolutely brutal here in West Texas. If i get a year or two out of these, I will be happy.

This product actually worked with restoring my centipede lawn that had been damaged by a national chain fertilizing company. Essentially entirely too much nitrogen was thrown down on my centipede lawn therefore destroying it. I cancelled the service and decided to use Lawn Restore, after all I figured if my grass is going to be killed or destroyed, I may as well have a hand in doing it myself versus paying someone else for total lawn destruction... In the span of 11 days my lawn started recovering. I was planning on reseeding the bad areas however at the rate of recovery I think my lawn should be fully thickened within 30 days, will see how this pans out. I've attached somewhat of a picture storyboard, the first picture is of my lawn being healthy last growing season (2017), the second picture is with the damaged caused from the national chain fertilizing company (May 2018), the third photo was taken 11 days after applying Lawn Restore. I'm impressed with the results thus far and plan on probably only using this product from now on. I did also purchase several bags of Milorganite at the same time of my Lawn Restore purchase, it was slated to be my second application, but I will hold off on the Milorganite until I get my lawn back to it's healthy state using Lawn Restore. I highly recommend Lawn Restore, it costs more but it works, remember it costs green to make green...

I really love it, and I was hesitant because of some of the reviews, but their stated issues had a simple fix, for the most part. If the bobbin is hitting the metal arms, take it off and reseat it and it works great. It's hard to get the cone on the right way, or it was with mine, but now that it's firmly in place, no issues whatsoever. My old winder, the spindle/bobbin/cone would flop around like a crazy fish and that's how it wound the yarn, but I had to have tight tension or the yarn would skip (or the gears!) and I had to hold the yarn in the "sweet spot" height or the yarn would skip and wind above or below the ball. It was annoying. This one, I don't have to do anything but just run my yarn through my fingers to keep knots out, with very little to no tension, and the metal spinning arm does all the winding and directing. I don't have to exert any extra effort and I get great yarn cakes. They're a bit looser than my old winder, but that's because my old winder I had to hold tightly to the yarn to get it to work. But if the balls are loose, then they won't stretch the yarn out, eh? It does make horribly loose balls if you only have a few ounces to wind. I'll have to figure that out. I have noticed that any sort of tension (like winding from the outside of a skein onto the winder, when it tugs) isn't really well-tolerated by the winder. I'm afraid I'll break the plastic gears if I hold tightly. I think that with practice, I'll find the right method to get comfortably tight cakes.

Doing some basic math, there's around 180 teaspoons in this jar. Or 90 doses - or enough for 9000 gallons. You'll go through a LOT more sprinkling it on waterfalls and rocks. I recommend using a sifter or window screen to disperse it on rocks and so on. For vertical waterfalls you'll waste a bit. I usually measure out 10 teaspoons at a time and flick it at our waterfall. I try and calculate whatever winds up in the pond and use that towards the overall pond dosage. It does work well - you'll find crud floating on the pond the next day. Since waterfalls need to be off during cleaning, I run a couple of large bubblers to make sure the pond stays oxygenated. A couple of doses and the pond is clear!

My husband said this is just enough, when she barged in on him in the bathroom to hide them behind the toilet. She does this with me as well. I copy this review from the page for San-J Tamari 64 ounce Gold Label variety: Top customer reviews: 2 out of 5 stars. Scarletrose. Love that it's organic - but beware of plastic bottle number. May 7, 2014. Verified Purchase. I love this soy sauce because it is organic and wheat free. I also try to avoid GMO's. It is a little on the salty side but I just add water to make it a sushi dipping sauce and add less in my cooking which I prefer anyway. I use it a lot in garlic honey soy marinades so decided to get a large bottle so it wouldn't run out so fast. The smaller bottles come in glass but the larger one (for service use) seen here is plastic. That would be fine except that it is type 7.. Which is difficult and less commonly recycled and worse.. More likely to contain harmful endocrine disrupters such as BPA. I've decided not to take my chances again. To the manufacturer: Please change your plastic to a safer material. Glass is preferable but to another plastic that is likely to be less harmful. I won't be purchasing this particular size again until a change is made. 24 people found this helpful. I ordered (the Black Label variety) hoping that a smart company like San-J would have taken note of this important advisory by now, 4 years, 2014 to 2018, but Im sorry to report that the bottle is still plastic number 7. I heard this useful mnemonic: 5 and 4 and 1 and 2, All the rest are BAD for you.

Adopted my cat from rescue agency when he was 6 weeks old to find he was extremely I'll upon his first vet appointment. Vet bills were expensive that first year until I found these lysine cat treats. He is a picky eater but loves these. I break one piece into six small ones and mix it with his other cat treats every morning and he gladly eats them. I leave them out and by night time he will finish them. I used to give them only when he wwas sick but he was getting suck so frequently I now give it to him daily. It has been almost a year now and he has been free of URI's. I bought this cage for a road trip we will be taking in a few months since it has the divider in the middle and two openings. Here's a few pros and cons to this cage: Pros This is a decently sized cage, especially if being used as a travel cage. Comes with 4 food/water bowls. Comes with handles that you can put almost anywhere on the cage. The divider in the middle is removable. Price was pretty decent, at $42.99 with free 2-day Prime shipping. Cons This cage is kinda flimsy, and I had a little bit of difficulty putting it together. The instructions weren't very specific and were hard to figure out. The two doors slide up and do not hold themselves open. This cage comes with 4 cheap, flimsy plastic perches, one of which came broken. It didn't bother me much because I'm not using any of them for my birds. The bottom grate does not come out, so cleaning is a bit difficult. All that being said, it's pretty okay and will work just fine as a travel cage. If you have more than one bird and have to transport them without being in the same cage together, this is a good option.

*** 1 month update *** Now FIVE stars instead of four. Previously I complained about the price (around $26 for full price), but I take it back as black (my choice color) seems always much cheaper. Worn almost every over a month, washed several times. It held very well until I accidentally put it in high-heat dryer. Now it has loose threads, and unlike other reviews, shrank a little. Still durable considering wearing daily, so I'm ordering another one. ***** My dog's length/chest/weight is 19/25/32. 3XL was way too big, sagging around the leg holes and belly. Then, XL fit perfect. I wanted to use it more like as a thermal undershirt under the harness and winter jacket, so two size down was the right size for me. 2XL might work better if wearing all day long like my lazy sweater. What's really great about this product is how well it covers the underbody, especially around the belly area, where the dog's fur is much thinner. Most dog jackets only cover the upper side, leaving the underbody pretty much exposed. My dog loves to dive into deep snow, and I can see how well the fleece protects him from snow and cold (see the attached pictures). One star off for the price, I could get it for $18, but the current price $27 seems a little too much although Im interested in another order... Still, a great product and highly recommended.

Don't be tempted by the cheap price of this pump and remember you usually get what you pay for. In this case, I ordered the 5,500 gph pump thinking it would be even more powerful than the pumps I have used in my pond in past years. WRONG! When I took this pump out of the box, I could tell by looking at the materials used and the type of construction of this pump that it was not on par with the more expensive pumps I have purchased in the past. I requested a return authorization on the pump and shipped it back the next day...did not even plug it in. When the Half Off Ponds company issued a refund, I received less than 70% of what I paid. The company kept $72.25 as the restocking fee and return freight. I had been quoted less than $10 for return freight, which was fine. I am still trying through Amazon Customer Service to get the rest of my refund from this company. So be aware, not only is this a cheaply made pump, but it could likely cost you more than a well made pump in the long run. My advice, stick with one of the well known pump brands and just pay the extra money up front and you will be satisfied your purchase of a well made product. Just not this one.
