UPDATE: I had to revise my previous 5 star rating to a 2 star rating -- I received two bags of this dog food, direct from the manufacturer, and the kibbles were nothing like what I had previously received. Previously, the kibbles were large, very uniformly cut, each piece identical, with virtually no wastage in the entire bag -- I was able to hand feed them easily to my dog. But the last two bags I received contained kibbles that were half the size as previously, most of them were crushed, and about a third of the bag consisted of pulverized meat dust, and my dog wouldn't touch this stuff. Plus it's virtually impossible to hand feed tiny crushed pieces of leather and meat dust. I'm totally disappointed in the quality control of this product -- I have written to the manufacturer to see what the problem is and why the product deteriorated so drastically just since the last time I ordered from them. Previous review: My 3 year old Bullmastiff loves this food! And that's saying a lot -- he has severe intestinal adhesions due to a botched surgery when he was 5 months old, and has to be on a highly nutritious low fiber diet. Due to his stomach and intestinal issues, he is an EXTREMELY picky eater and it's really hard finding dog food that he CAN eat and that he WILL eat consistently. Many times in order to coax him to eat I have to hand feed him several small meals a day -- these kibbles are cut into nice treat size chunks, so he feels like he's getting a treat but I know he's getting a nutritious meal. I also keep them in his food dish, and he will often even eat them out of his dish when he's hungry! These kibbles are expensive, but totally worth it for my peace of mind!

I have tried out, bought, used, and even broken several cutting tools in my 68 years. Axes, machetes, hatchets, swords and saws are all made at various pricing points, offering a confusing array with types of steel, various lengths and weights, shape and sharpness of the blade, different materials used for the handles, and whether it's full tang or not. Ontario has been cranking out these 18" machetes for about 70 years. Of all the cutting/chopping tools I've used over the years I've found that machetes are the most versatile and useful, the one cutting tool I would carry if I could only have one This mil-spec Ontario machete at $22.99 is one of the most value oriented sharp tools that I have ever used. It holds a good edge and is easy to sharpen. It is well balanced and easily performs any cutting task assigned to it. My absolute favorite is the Esee Lite Machete but that one comes in at around $81.00. You might consider the Ontario machete a poor man's Esee Lite, but at a cost of only one quarter of the Esee it represents a true value for what you get. Since I work so much in the Southwest Florida wetlands I always spray the handles with fluorescent orange. So much easier to find them if dropped in dense foliage. A good machete can be used to chop down large diameter trees, clear brush, slash through hanging vines, sharpen tent stakes, cut up firewood, and makes a great defensive weapon against creatures large and small. If you need to have a good cutting tool with you this Ontario 18" Military is a great way to start. It is simultaneously one of the best and also reasonably priced.

We have had a small black ant problem at our home for several years. They pop up from time to time all over the house and we are well aware of sugar/borax based products like Terro which works slowly and helps a bit. The same is true of Advance which is a protein based ant poison. All of these products are designed attract the ants and entice them to carry the food/poison back to the ant colony. I thought they worked well but they were far from perfect solutions. Then I discovered this product which works like magic with black ants. The warning labels are scary so use caution when using this stuff but I have to say it works well. I basically use the Terro gell and/or Advance product to attract the ants and start the poison process of the colony. Once the trail to the colony has been identified, I spot spray the opening where the ants are coming in with this product. It kills a few ants on contact but the magic starts after it dries. The ants track the dried poison back to the colony. It takes about 3 to 4 days and that is the end of that particular colony. It seems to last a long time. Unfortunately for us, we basically live on an ant hill and there are many colonies on our property. However, once this product is used it seems to kill that entire colony and it doesn't seem to come back to life - Ever. The Tarro and Advance products are good but they don't completely eliminate the problem whereas this product seems to wipe them out. I hope this helps.

Premium saltines has always been my choice of saltine crackers. In this cold weather season -10 this week, I love to have soup and crackers. These crackers are just enough. Very easy to make in your Slow Cooker. INGREDIENTS: 24 ounces red potato, diced 1 (16-ounce) package frozen corn 3 tablespoons all-purpose flour 6 cups chicken stock 1 teaspoon dried thyme 1 teaspoon dried oregano 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder 1/2 teaspoon onion powder Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper, to taste 2 tablespoons unsalted butter 1/4 cup heavy cream DIRECTIONS: Place potatoes and corn into a 6-qt slow cooker. Stir in flour and gently toss to combine. Stir in chicken stock, thyme, oregano, garlic powder, onion powder, salt and pepper, to taste. Cover and cook on low heat for 7-8 hours or high heat for 3-4 hours.* Stir in butter and heavy cream. Serve immediately with Premium crackers and a green salad. Recommended. prisrob 01-08-18

This is a cute duffel bag for your sewing machine. I bought it to cover my Brother CE5000PRW sewing machine, which is about 16" long, 12" high, and 6" wide, including all knobs and sticking-out-things. It fits in the bag perfectly with a couple inches to spare on all sides of the bag except for the height, which has about an inch of available free space. Thus, I wouldn't recommend this for any sewing machine over 13" tall, at the very most, or anything much under the measurements of my machine, because it may rock and roll all over the place inside. When this bag arrived, it was packed flat, about 3 inches tall. It unfolded nicely to a surprising large bag, measuring about 13" tall, 18" long, and 12" wide, including the front pocket when fully expanded. The tote bag itself weighs about 1lb, 12oz, measured with my kitchen scale. It has absolutely no padding and is completely unlined, made of a thin double-sided fabric which has a pretty fabric tapestry material on one side and the usual crinkly plasticy cheap-backpack material on the inside that, in my experience, often tends to rip, crack and split with age. It also makes a sound like a loudly crumpling paper bag every time you move it. Since it is unpadded, you can feel the knobs and such of the machine through the bag, and it provides almost no protection for your machine against hard bumps, being dropped, or having things stacked on top of it (which may be tempting, because it LOOKS so sturdy!!). As a dust cover and carrying bag though, I think it'll be terrific. The main part of the bag has two black metal zippers, regular ones like you'd find on any backpack, not heavy duty, that can meet anywhere - in the middle, both on one side, etc. - and they both unzip all the way down to the bottom of the bag so you can insert your sewing machine from the top. Nitpick alert - although they do zip all the way down, the material is stiff enough that the sides doesn't fold apart and stay there, they want to stand straight up and mostly closed, so you'll get one side of the machine in and the other side will want to fold inwards, you fix that side and the other corner slips under and folds in... it would be nice if the sides would stay open wider. Inside the main part of the bag, there's a rectangle of thick cardboard riveted to the bottom, covered with the same black plasticy material, which prevents the bag from sagging as you carry it and gives it shape. It's only attached to the bag with 4 silver metal rivets, one in each corner, so small things like loose pins, flat machine feet, etc. could definitely roll under it and become trapped. On the outside, the bottom of the bag is the same tapestry material and has 4 little plastic feet which SHOULD keep it elevated when you set it down so the bottom doesn't touch any surface, but they aren't quite tall enough to accomplish that. You won't want to set this down on a dirty floor or a table where liquids may be spilled, things may soak right through. The handles of the bag are made of two thick black woven straps that are sewn down the front and back of the outside of the bag and meet at a long seam on the underside. They are reinforced by a thick shiny black pleather strip sewn across both the front and back. I don't think the straps have any chance of ripping off from the fabric, which can be a problem with some totes that only have them sewn on halfway down the sides. I am a little wary of the fact that the bottom is not all one piece - with both that major seam for both sides of the bag AND all 4 ends of the straps meeting there, combined with the weight of a sewing machine, reason says that it may be a stressed area, but it seems sturdy enough. Only time will tell! As for storage space for extra sewing bits, there are no pockets on the inside, but there's one really spacious single-zippered pocket on the outside front. This pocket measures about 13" long, 9" tall, and expands to about 2" wide. I put my sewing machine pedal and cord in there and they only took up about 1/3 of the space. There's definitely still room left for a basic needs kit - fullsize scissors, thread, bobbins, etc! Lastly, there's a seam tag inside that says simply, "Made in Taiwan". There's nothing that says the material it is made of or directions for cleaning care. I imagine it's probably some sort of cotton blend for the tapestry side, polyester/plastic for the inside, and gentle spot-clean-only for both. It is definitely not waterproof, and I don't think water-resistant, either. Well, that's all I can think of to include that I'd wanted to know before buying. :) Overall, I really like this tote as a dustcover/carrying case for my machine and think it'll last at least several years, barring any incidents with the zipper (usually the weakest part on a bag). I think the price I paid - $29.99 in December 2017 - was about what it's worth, if it lasts. If not, I'll be back to update this review! :)

You are either from the Buffalo area where there is hardly a kitchen that doesnt have a jar of Webers horseradish mustard in it, or you were looking for something else and landed on this page accidentally. Webers is only sold in Buffalo and by a handful of online Buffalo specialty stores and is sought after by ex-Buffalonians all over the world. It is made in a tiny warehouse building on Buffalos south side where it has been made by the same family unchanged except for the jar design since 1922. Webers mustard is an acquired taste. If you grew up with it every other mustard tastes weird. If you are new to it, as I have exposed it to some extended family and friends, your lips will probably pucker and you may wonder how anyone could stand the stuff. Or, you may become a convert. If you don't like horseradish, don't worry, thats not the predominant taste. The taste is actually hard to describe and Buffalonians will agree that its not how it tastes by itself but what it does to certain favorite foods that makes it so special. Buying Webers can be tricky. My choice has been to buy it by the case directly from the factory but its hard to get through a case and have the last few jars still taste fresh. You can get it per jar from some specialty online places but unless you are ordering enough of it or other stuff the freight charge is proportionately too high. This worked out to be my best option. A 32oz jar is the size of a large mayo jar and to me is the perfect size to stay fresh until its gone. With prime same day it works out to be equivalent to three 12oz retail jars at 4.50 each, close to what you would pay in Buffalo. And the date code for the jar I ordered in January 2018 is good through May of 2019. I know you can buy cheap generic yellow mustard any day for a lot less but if you weren't raised on Webers you cant possibly understand why we natives don't fret about how much it costs.

During the course of the year, our pool plaster gets stains from wooden tables sitting on the shelf and metal toys left on the bottom of the pool and the occasional blue algae killer poured onto the shelf. We use citric acid to remove those stains. I dumped the whole 10 lb sack into our 40,000 gallon pool focusing on stains. I could have started off with half that amount and waited to observe the results but this is the 9th year we have done this and I always end up adding the additional amount. I do this treatment once a year in February right before the temperature starts rising so that there is no chlorine in the water and the ph is 7.2 or higher. The chlorine won't hurt the process but it destroys the citric acid. You want the ph high because the citric acid is going to drop the ph way down. We turned the filter off for 24 hours but you don't have to. I avoid rainy days so as not to dilute the water. After two days we start adding bleach (from the bottle) to gradually bring up the chlorine level. Once we have the chlorine up to an acceptable level, we start adding salt back in. When the chlorinator registers adequate salt, we turn the chlorinator on and gradually start increasing it. All of this is done when water temperatures are 60 degrees or higher. I've got notes to add 2 quarts of Metal Free by Natural Chemistry after a couple of days but I never do. Our pool never requires anything to raise ph level as it does that on it's own. My notes also say to not use shock for two weeks The process returns most of the plaster to a sparkling white but it couldn't reach the rust stains at the bottom of the deep end to completely remove them.

I love the Kukicha or bocha tea which is in fact a mix of a few Japanese Green tea leaves with a higher amount of stems. this one is a Kabuse tea which means it is cultivated in the shade. I bought some Kukicha for the first time a few years ago at the Palais des Ths in Bordeaux. it was a Kukicha from the Shizuoka district and I fell in love with it. It is slightly different from this one as, among other things, its amount in theine seems a little bit lower, but the difference is subtle. This twig tea is organic and its aromatic taste is both subtle and persistent in the mouth. Kukicha was considered for a long time to be the the poor people tea and like many old time peasant recipes made with basic ingredients this tea became more and more appreciated by the higher classes of the society inside and outside japan and consequently it is nowadays very trendy. I like this tea because I like loose tea and green tea is a great anti-oxydant. I also can drink it at any moment of the day. Let 6gr of loose green tea infuse 2 or 3 minutes in 30 cl at a temperature of 176F and enjoy! This tea is not astringent contrarily to the one I usually drink and it is not as cloudy either. I think it is on the sweet side so it can be even drank cold with desserts. Personally I like to drink it warm while I eat my meal. I did not know this brand before and I am very happy with my purchase as this tea is of great quality. You can prepare this delicate tea in a warmed traditional Japanese tea pot made of porcelain or, even better, earth. All in all a very aromatic tea like an ambered liquor at a very decent price.

I struggle to understand the rationale of these products.... Except for the Jackfruit, we have wonderful mango and pineapple produced right here in our own country. I do understand trying to help boost the economy of poor African countries, but I do not see how that is happening in this case. It seems pretty ridiculous to bring small quantities of dried fruits into the US from Africa and sell them at exorbitant prices. There are plenty of hungry people in Africa, why not dry the fruit and pass it around and trade it there. So, three stars for effort--and that is stretching it, by my standards. Trying to find a market in the US for fruits that are lesser quality than what we already grow here seems a losing battle. But, hey, if you want to jump on this merry-go-round, I'll tell you what I think of the three fruits offered here: Features of all three fruits--jackfruit, mango, pineapple: The slices and tidbits are dehydrated, with nothing added. The pieces are not regular shaped. The pieces are not pretty, as nothing has been added to prevent oxidation. The pieces are extremely chewy and they clump together. Some of the pieces are so stuck together that they tear when separated. In my opinion, the best tasting of the three is the mango. The slices retain a nice sweetness coupled with a bright tanginess. The mango slices are not stringy. At least this is what I experienced in the tiny, 2 ounce portion, that was in the bag. The jackfruit flavor is like a dull banana. The pieces are quite ugly. As a snack, you could make them last quite a while, because they are very chewy. In my opinion, the pineapple was unacceptable. Poor flavor! Why bother with this when fresh is so abundant. Even canned is a better alternative.

Finally, a toothbrush that fits in my 4lb chihuahua's mouth! Tip from a professional dog groomer: If your dog hates getting their teeth brushed like mine does, wait to do it until they're sleepy. Don't try to do it if your dog is hyper and running around. Don't chase them with the toothbrush or turn it into a game! That will make it a lot harder. I wait until my chihuahua is sleeping and I bring the toothbrush to him and just do the outside of the teeth, keeping the mouth closed and lifting the lip (not trying to go on the inside near the tongue, unless he's being really good). But be careful if your dog is easily startled or is a biter. I always talk to him in a high voice and comfort him during it. It doesn't matter if you don't get all the teeth right away. It's better to ease your dog into it so they don't have a bad experience. If you wet the toothbrush before putting toothpaste on, it will be more gentle on the gums. Be careful of the gums because they can bleed just like ours!

This stuff is great. You just press a pen or pencil to it, then move the pen or pencil while touching. You get a visible line where the pen or pencil was touching. You can orient the lines to produce small symbols, and using a number of small symbols in a line (conveniently there are lines on this product for lining up your small symbols), these symbols together can represent a usually spoken sound which represents a thing or an idea. Then you can leave a gap to the right of your small line of symbols and then you can make another group of these small symbols to represent another thing or idea. You can keep representing as many things or ideas together. You go from left to right on the lines. When you reach the right hand edge of this product, you start again on the lefthand edge. You can continue on and on as though you have things and ideas one after another, as many as you want. When you get to the bottom right corner and completely run out of space, you lift the leaf up and start another "page" at the upper left hand corner. You can go on and on putting more and more things and ideas together until they all come together to form a really really big , complex thing or idea. This is such a great product.

Update as of march 2018.. continued to occasionally use this .. especially on rejected canned food.. in my effort to get more canned ( dry is low in moisture and very alkaline.. even grain free due to plant material), i tried duck and sweet potato wich totally upset her belly.. pudding poop every day.. even a tiny bit of blood.. i felt sooo bad.. so i started gradually increasing this and used a little pumpkin patch.. better but goin on 2 weeks worried me along with some meows here and there when going... so the vet suggested a dose of diagel.. i picked it up two days ago and things are looking up and shes seeming more comfortable so far.. it was 25 a dose but i saw it on amazon for 15!.. its worth a google for details but is for short term loose stools from stress/ food change / "garbage gut".. i will always keep this and pumpkin patch and probably an emergency dose of diagel.. its worth it to have these on hand for immediate use!! ----------- update.. ordered some.. chose petwish pro. .. had a winter snafu but they were on it and sent another right away.. it came with a nice long exp date.. i believe this is one of those things where it really matters who you order from.. i will continue to sprinkle a little on everyones food a few times a week ( i never use whole packet).. all poops are lookin good and it helps em finish when ive chosen a not so popular flavor ; ) ------------------------- dealing with a urinary thing either from stress/ some crystals/ or bacteria.. Not exactly sure as it comes and goes.. i think it started as stress and became bacterial.. but we are addressing all of it so that has led to 2 rounds of antibiotics so i asked about this because i myself am not a fan of obliterating gut bacteria but with psycho kitty we had to err on the side of caution since i have to drug and muzzle her to get there.! I was all ready to click order when they called back and said YES highly recomended and we've put some aside for you.. since theyve been so helpful i didn't have the heart to say i was gonna order here.. but i will next.. I only sprinkle a little at a time.. more hers but all get some.. and they like it.. one day just to check.. i sprinkled some on the cold leftovers ( they often eat all the squishy and leave bigger pieces).. added a tiny bit of water.. and to my surprise cujo psycho came outa nowhere and mowed it! So now in my effort to get more liquid in her.. i leave a little in can.. then add water and more of this and keep it in micro till its just her and i.. and then i give it to her and voila..she drinks more.. sometimes i can do it twice if theres a little left in there! I also switched dry to purina one urinary ( a maintenance not super acidifying) was using bbuf but it must have been to alkaline .. and i needed a dry that all could eat.. I bought the petsafe $120 timed feeder so there is always something in bowl but not a pile. . Im using kings homeop urinary.. uromaxx for her in canned to acidify existing crystals.. and feliway products for stress. . All here on amazon. . And a head to tail treat for stress.. .. and canned food twice a day vs once.. Hoping she stays well.. xray showed little bit of crystal action but not a lot.. And after talking with two very nice vets the nutshell i took from it is.. 1- crystal formation is common with dry food heavy diets especially in cats like her who don't drink a lot of water 2- a lot of urinary issues are stress induced 3- some are no known cause MOST will right themselves but we have to treat the symptoms because they are miserable and the symptoms can cause stress wich then makes symptoms worse SO anyone in same boat.. i would surely try things like these first ( and i did although i dosed the uromaxx as 11lb.. and found out she was 16) .. i would watch to be sure there is urine passing as a blockage is deadly! I would take them to vet if symptoms persist ( in our case first episode was 16 hr. . Then fine for a week ( i started treatment first day i noticed) second was day n half.. upped doses.. okay 10 days.. third was 2 days so i took to vet 2-3 wk antibiotic shot and upped doses now that i knew shes a bigger fatty.. good 2-1/2 wk then started.. so on second day took back. . Re did shot plus xtra anti inflam .. and went home with liquid pain med at small dose.. found even a half of that dose helped get thru till drugs kicked in.. and thats when i switched dry food gradually and discovered i could make gravy in the can with this added to get more liquid! I was told at this point get water in anyvway possible keep on with new food current supplement s... and use pain med if needed GOOD LUCK to all!! Beware of too much dry food especially low water drinkers! Especially males ( tinier tubes get blocked easily).. and keep sharing all your helpful info!!

I read a lot of reviews about these before I tried this one & 1 review stuck in my mind. A reviewer was worried about the temperature of the unit in heavy use as he said his got hot. So this fired my inner enginerd big time. I built a test fixture to evaluate this on. I made a mounting, used a proper plastic wall box & cover plate, mounted it to an extension wire & sat it on a test bench. Inside the wiring box itself I mounted 2 thermocouples to measure temperature of the unit itself & inside the box. I used a non contact thermometer to measure the surface temperature of the faceplate itself & of the plastic wall box too. I connected a DROK USB meter to an outlet (what a neat meter!) and then plugged 2 depleted ipads into the outlets to see what happened. It got warmer. Not hot, just warmer. Maybe 10 degrees Celcius above ambient room temperature. Obviously it doesn't stay there as the charged ipads stop drawing power eventually & there is no more heat generated. I repeated this exercise about a dozen times over the next couple of weeks & it was very repeatable. Yes it gets warm,yes it feels warm, no my sample size of one did not get hot, or anywhere near what I would call unsafe temperature hot. So I took it out of it's test fixture & installed it in the wall by my bed, where it has been happily charging i devices ever since with no issues whatsoever. I will be getting a few more of these as I feel like adding them to other outlets in key places in the house, as I am now satisfied that they are not as risky as they might be, with the heat they generate in a small wall box. Points to note though: You will need to have pretty much the largest wall box possible. 20cu inch or bigger as they are very large devices. Larger than a GFCI in width, height & depth. The wall box will be very, very crowded with the wiring, wire nuts & this device. Be warned. Older houses may not have metal boxes, or even plastic ones big enough as I think a 16 cu inch box will be too small. Check first. Always be safe with wiring, make sure you shut off all power before you do anything, make sure that the device is grounded & if you are not sure what you are doing, don't do it & call an electrician. Getting it wrong can be fatal - to you or one of your loved ones.

Once upon a time I laughed at guitar players who used Fast Fret. I assumed it was something shredders used as an affectation or something. But then I got into a steadily gigging band, playing indoors and outdoors in all kinds of weather, in a place where humidity can be an issue year round. I'm not super picky as a player, but one thing I absolutely hate is when my guitar neck, or my hands, start to feel sticky from humidity. Fast Fret is the answer. I put it on my strings as well as the back of the neck, and sometimes, in a pinch, on my fretting hand, as well. I simply can't play well if my hands are sticking to the strings and/or the guitar neck. This stuff helps. It's not ideal and takes some getting used to - ideal conditions for playing guitar for me don't include oily fingers - but it's really not a big deal. Fast Fret has saved me from having a miserable gig many, many times. Too many to count. Sure - it cleans the strings and the mineral oil will only help your fretboard - but that's not why I use it. Also - it lasts forever. I've never finished one - I always lose them before they're empty. I've been using this for probably 25 years and haven't seen a need to try anything else. It does what it does very well, and I can't live without it.

Kitty loves it, I feed half a can with diluted water to my kitty with a little bit of dry food sprinkled on top. After kitty got blocked from eating primarily dry food (it was high quality but problem is lack of moisture content), I've been looking for high quality wet food alternatives. No throwing-up, and after a month, kitty's got a pretty shiny fur coat. I've also been looking up the history of this company and this is what I found: - The company is headquartered in Austin, Texas, and their foods are manufactured in Kansas and Nebraska. Both of these FDA-approved facilities are annually inspected by organizations including the American Institute of Baking and BRC Global Standard for Global Safety. - All ingredients are sourced from a network of suppliers who represent Nulos quality standards. Fish ingredients, for example, are sustainably-sourced and preserved without ethoxyquin. None of their ingredients, including vitamins and minerals, come from China. The foods are low-carb and low-glycemic, high in protein and low in carbohydrates: - At least 80% of the protein in every recipe comes from animal sources, meaning that they're biologically appropriate and highly digestible for your carnivorous cat. - Nulos Zero Promise states that each formula contains zero corn, wheat gluten, soy, or any artificial preservatives, colors, and flavors. Nulo foods contain a patented BC30 probiotic: - Its the worlds first probiotic strain capable of surviving high-heat processing. This ensures that the food retains its probiotic power after cooking and extrusion. Sounds pretty good for me. 4 stars because I've read bad reviews about quality back in 2011, so I'm still skeptical.

The culprit was a small piece of plastic snapped off from an undetermined area of the cartridge. ((SEE PICTURE)) I removed OEM Brother cartridge and installed the LINKYO. After the printer grunted its usual sounds etc when installing a new cartridge she was ready to go. I hit print and she quickly shot out 15 USPS shipping labels. I picked up the OTHER sealed bag with cartridge two inside, but did not open. There IS a similar rattling sound. Same tiny piece snapped off? Don't know. Started buying these for my GSD when she hit 1 year old after a few nylabones. In the last year and a half I've bought 4 of these, at $10-12, so $40-48. If I'd kept buying cow hides or bully sticks I'd not only have a constantly smelly, sticky dog but I'd have spent well over $200 just in chews in the same time span. These are well worth the money not just in terms of cost of product, but also in keeping me from having to buy new furniture due to an overactive dog. Typically she has 2 bones at all times, 1 new for the daytime (not chewed down to about 5" or less) and 1 older chewed one. I swap bones that have been whittled down with newer ones and only let her chew smaller ones under supervision. Chunks do break off if it gets too small, so I'd recommend watching or listening to your dog. There's an audible snap that occurs when she takes a too big chunk, that gives me the cue to tell her to drop it. I'll give her the older bone after dinner and it helps clean and maintain her teeth perfectly. This is not only a money, time, and furniture saver but it's one of her favorite toys overall. Yes "toy", she loves it so much she'll toss and chase it around the room just like a ball. Highly recommend, just with the caveat that you have to stay on top of the size of the toy. Always take it away if it gets too small, besides that it's the best toy for dogs who get bored and need to chew to stay active.

My dog had VERY itchy red skin and a yeasty smell, especially on her belly. We tried putting cornstarch on it, then tried baby powder. Everything seemed to help a little bit for a little while--but then the furious itching would return. She spent so much time chewing on her skin it was like she did little else while she was awake. And then she would wake in the middle of the night, scratching and chewing even more. The vet saw her in the middle of my powdering attempts, and he said to bring her back if it didn't get better. I know from experience that there isn't a lot they can do for itchiness. Unless it's something simple like fleas, it's a long battle with various drugs and topical agents that aren't wonderful for dogs to ingest. Or the dreaded Prednisone, which works wonders in the short term, but is terrible for long-term health. So, I decided to try this stuff before going down the veterinary medicine road. Honestly, the first day she was BETTER. She fell into a deep sleep on the sofa like she was SO relieved. I am very skeptical of these things, and I immediately emailed a friend with more experience--don't dietary supplements take weeks to work?? The next day, there was more itchiness, more chewing... But it was less, and it went away a bit more the next day. Over the next few weeks, it would come and go instead of being a constant thing. After a month, the yeasty moist skin, redness, and non-stop chewing was completely gone. We finished our first jar of probiotics today (I think it lasted about 3 months and she is a big girl). She smells better, isn't chewing and scratching anymore, and seems WAY happier. I don't know how this works, but it really did the trick for my dog. I think it depends on the cause. If your dog has allergies, it may or may not work. I'm pretty sure my dog had some type of skin yeast infection, and this did a great job of wiping it out! I'm waiting for my second jar to arrive! My dog is 65 pounds and had no digestive issues with the powder. I want to keep her on it since she's getting older and will be more susceptible to health issues. Since this isn't a drug, and it definitely helped, I'm hoping to head off other problems by continuing to use it.

I have been having behavior problems with my inside cats for the past 6 months with visitors outside marking territory (feral cats) and visitors inside having accidents. I could not stop the bad behavior my cats were displaying, (spraying, hiding, fighting) that had cropped up in the past 6 months. I bought a black light and was absolutely disgusted at my carpet. What I saw was invisible to the eye, but when you shine the black light on it, WOW!!! Walls, curtains and especially carpets were an absolute mess. I ordered this wishing it was the size of a drum but thought I would put it into a pump sprayer and do the walls and floors where I saw a stain with the black light. I followed the instructions and waited about 15 minutes and then I cleaned the walls and shampooed part of the carpet so I could see if there was a difference. The next day I put the black light on the areas again, and there was absolutely no difference in what the stains looked like in the treated area and the area not treated. There may be a difference in how it smells but truthfully it did not smell beforehand (that I noticed) until I put my nose to it. It does not smell, but my sense of smell is about 2% of what a cats sense of smell is. I have no idea if it works or not. It did clean the walls of urine and that I did notice with the black light. Three stars for that. Expensive soap?

After trying this just once, I didn't find that I could get very good results with this using the Prima silicone molds. The clay left crease marks on the surface even after conditioning it. You also have to be careful with smaller items, as the clay shrinks as it dries and as it dries the bottom may not remain flat (at least in my case). If you don't mind creases, I guess it could give your piece a bit of texture (I was making flowers, so no, this was not something I wanted) or you could put small micro beads over the "flaws" etc., then paint (which is the only way I see myself using this in the future). I was expecting to get much better results from the reviews I read here. I think honestly that the Sculpey Polymer Clay works much better (you can condition polymer clay really well using a pasta machine so you won't got little crease marks etc. on the surface of your finished item). I think this is a hit or miss product where sometimes you might get ok results and sometimes not. Be prepared for some failure rate. I definitely would not use this to make furniture knobs etc. I think this is strictly in the craft category. If you have pre-teen children at home this could be a fun art material for them to create things from their imagination and paint later on. I'm not really seeing this as anything spectacular for use with silicone molds to create craft embellishments for mixed media etc. or to create decorative molding for furniture.

There's a lot of things to discuss, but I want to keep this as short as I could. This game is by far one of the very best games in terms of story telling - much like Final Fantasy VII and Muv-Luv Alternative. It's extremely well done, even the five endings, which are part of the 26 endings (most of them are silly ones), it gives a better understanding of how cruel and screwed up the world is in Nier:Automata. The character depth and creation gives Nier:Automata an edge in such an extrodinary way. The music is mind-blowing in every way - just when you think that you've explored most of the music in the game, in Route 3 (final route) new music entries make their way to the player. In fact, what strikes me the most is the True Ending and how they've formulated such an ending. No game has *ever* done something as Platinum Games. The combat is superb in every way. I do feel like the system itself lacked more unique skills - perhaps a skill tree or something alike could have spiced things up to the edition. Either way, this is a masterpiece that fortifies itself in such a unique way to the players. Excellent RPG in every-way and nothing like the other RPGs we've been getting this past several years. In comparison to other games - Zelda and Nier:Automata are very hard to compare to each other as both use different graphic engines and have different skill-sets, story, music and so on and forth. Nier:Automata does indeed exceed Final Fantasy XV in many areas (character, story, music, design), including combat. FFXV's graphic are much better than Nier, though. Nier:Automata and Horizon: Zero Dawn are somewhat similar to each other in some categories. I feel that Horizon bests all of the other games in term of Design and Graphics and several important story elements which makes it compelling to me, yet Nier gives us a much more focused character development and background story on the essence and history of the world and gives us that feeling of what it is like to be an Android. Both games are so good that it is hard to say which is better. Nier:Automata has a more bitter-sweet story than Horizon, though Horizon does have some bitter-sweet stories here and there but they're mostly indirect to the main character as opposed to Nier:Automata's main characters. Get the DLC for this game to play the additional Colossum challenges and to unlock outfits for 2B, 9S and A2. Looking forward to the sequel that's currently in the works.

Direct replacement for the 20 year old Hayward 1-1/2 HP pool pump we had. Piping lined up perfectly and I can re-use the nearly-new parts (debris basket and end suction plug) I recently bought to repair the existing pump. Two complaints- the wiring terminal on this new motor is horrible. If the motor manufacturer intended on the installer using terminals to connect the wiring, they should include some in the package. It takes some contortioning and patience to get the new wiring in and under the screw heads to hook the wiring up. Second, there's no room to put a conduit lock nut on the conduit end that screws into the motor. With many conduit ends, it isn't needed if you have a nice washer or O-ring, but it would be nice to be able to get the lock nut on. Otherwise, this is a fairly easy job to replace the pump/motor. I'm sure most local pool stores are charging $500 for this pump/motor and $150 to install. I hate that there are some bad reviews online about this pump not lasting as long as the ones from 20 years ago....even my local pool store says they don't last as long.

I mistakenly chose this Head and Shoulders conditioner instead of the shampoo. I didn't realize I had made a mistake until my husband asked me why the new shampoo was so viscous and wouldn't foam up! Surprisingly, the conditioner alone worked well for him though, helping with both dandruff and dry scalp. He has used dandruff shampoo for years, never a conditioner, for his flaky scalp. It may be that we accidentally stumbled upon something that works for him, used occasionally: H&S conditioner without shampoo. I have an acquaintance whose career is in glassblowing. The heat from the kiln has wrought havoc with her hair, which is now as dry as straw. She told me that she no longer even uses shampoo, but conditioner exclusively. Of course you would be buying conditioner to use with your shampoo, so my husband's experience with it may not seem applicable. But I do think it speaks well for this particular H&S conditioner that it works well even without shampoo for controlling dry scalp and dandruff.

This is a review for the 2wayz garden hose splitter. **August 2018: I've been using this for daily lawn sprinklering for 6 weeks now with no issues, I do turn the main tap off when I'm done**. Some of the reviews for this item seem out of date: the device now has an all metal body (stainless steel) and a metal (brass) attachment ring, weighing in at a chunky 10oz. It looks like the two hose attachments are sleeved into the body and in the current model there are pins (visible in the pictures and video) to prevent these tubes from blowing out, which is reported in one of the old Amazon user reviews. I bought this item because it had the long levers versus Amazon alternative: my wrists and thumbs are not in great shape and won't improve with time and this helps me get a good grasp. I had problems getting a wrench onto the knurled faucet attachment ring because the body, levers and faucet tap handle were all in the way so attachment is basically hand tight. I will always turn this off at the faucet tap and not simply rely on the valves in the splitter unless I get a watering timer where one has to let the controller have access to water. Please click yes if you found the review useful, Ta.

Like a few reviewers have said...they are ok. However, my issues are: - several of the colors are so similar that they really are fudging when they say there are 48 colors... That's the case with the maroon, the dark blue, the dark greens, the light greens, the pinks, and a couple of the browns. I honestly would say that I have repeated pens but the cap colors are different. ... - speaking of cap colors ..... the pen cap colors have very little to do with the actually ink color...see example in photos..the color I thought was florescent yellow based on the cap color was actually olive... cap colors that are pastel looking end up being very dark, not pastel at all. - There are no numbers on the colors so if I drop the container it will take a while to put them back in order to match my color chart, mainly because of the bad cap colors. My solution will definitely be to take a black sharpie and add my own numbering system! - bad bleed thru...I was going to color my chart directly into my journal but stopped after the first color and had to do it on a separate piece of paper and glue it into my journal. ... I bought them to use in my bullet journal and in my Bible...NOPE! Can't be used on either. - They are great if the area you are coloring is tiny or 1 stroke wide, otherwise you can see each individual line of color (ugh) and overlaps look bad. So, long story short, the colors they have are pretty so if all the other stuff isn't a problem for you ... these will work.

Heres some great research for you all... To start with I have used Maracyn Two for dropsy in the past and it works GREAT! within only a day I would see a difference and the fish would get 100% better BUT it always comes back later which is the main problem why people say its really hard to cure. From the university's and vet reports Maracyn Two can cause kidney failure. Well thats what we are trying to cure and thats why it comes back and it comes back worse and kills the fish. Now I read in forums Kanaplex is the best because its not as damaging to the kidneys (not as but still is). I read from university and vet reports that Kanamycin which is Kanaplex may cause renal damage "kidney failure" (oh great now what?) No wonder everyone is having relapse with dropsy, Kanaplex kills the bacteria then the fish gets better but then the kidneys start to fail because of the medicine and Dropsy comes back again. Note: I have been trying Kanaplex in the water and it has done nothing to help my 4" Black Moor Goldfish with mild dropsy. In fact she was getting worse. I did read the fish only absorbs 15% of Kanaplex in the water and is not as successful in the water for internal infections. It should be given by mouth if the fish is still eating for internal infections so the fish is absorbing the correct dose. After trying it in the food by the next day the scales have went down but I don't have faith she will be cured. I will keep giving her Kanaplex in her food for 10 days and update but After reading and researching I am afraid Kanaplex is just further damaging her kidneys :(... She will die if I don't try anyways so we will see. Quote from Dr. Govind Pandey: "kanamycin and amikacin are very effective against Gram-negative bacteria when given by injection. However, they cause kidney damage in fish when given by this route. Kanamycin and neomycin may be effective against external infections if used in bath treatments. Besides, kanamycin is also effective when mixed with feed to treat gastrointestinal bacterial infections. So going by the above research from Dr. Govind Pandey... Kanamycin can cause kidney damage and is good for gastrointestinal bacterial infections BUT if the fish already has kidney problems with dropsy FORGET IT! its only going to come back and end up killing the fish with kidney failure. So now what? No wonder everyone says it works but then Dropsy comes back again later and kills the fish. If the fish has only a gastrointestinal bacterial infection and NO kidney problems then this may work ok but not if the fish already has kidney problems (dropsy). so this is why I give Kanaplex 3 stars. Its good for somethings but not the things we are told and believe works. Its basically tricking you into thinking it works but then comes back with vengeance. so sad :(... Now I know why the experts say its better to euthanize the fish with clove oil because there really is no cure for Dropsy symptoms YET but is there? How about what they use to treat humans with bacterial, pathogens and kidney problems? or dogs and cats? Lets research that and go from there. Maybe we must think outside the box with feeding the fish a natural remedy that won't cause the kidneys further failure. Probiotics, enzymes and anti-parasite Herbs are a big break through for treating bacterial and pathogen parasitic Infections how about some of these may work for fish too? Quote from Drugs dot com: Kanamycin "This medicine may cause kidney problems, nerve problems, and hearing problems (like long-lasting hearing loss). This could even happen at normal doses. The risk of these problems may be higher if you already have kidney or hearing problems, if you get high doses of kanamycin, or if you get kanamycin for a long time. The risk may also be higher in older people, infants, or people who are dehydrated. Hearing loss can happen even after kanamycin is stopped. If you already have kidney problems or hearing problems, tell your doctor. Your doctor will watch you closely and may run hearing and kidney tests. Talk with your doctor. Do not use kanamycin if you are taking or have recently taken any drugs that can cause nerve, kidney, or hearing problems. This may be drugs like amphotericin B, bacitracin, cephaloridine, cisplatin, colistin, cyclosporine, ethacrynic acid, furosemide, paromomycin, polymyxin B, vancomycin, viomycin, or other drugs like this one. There are many other drugs that can do this. Ask your doctor or pharmacist if you are not sure. Very bad muscle problems and very bad breathing problems have happened with kanamycin. So there you go... make sure you research drugs before giving to fish and know exactly what you are treating for or you could be making the fish worse. The side effects that happen to humans happen to animals and yes fish too. We all have the same organs to live and labs test these meds for side effects on animals and fish before humans. You can be assured what side effects happen to humans also happens to animals and fish too. My common sense tells me its the very medicine that is bringing Dropsy back with kidney failure or the micro parasites have biofilm and nothing will kill them. so now what do we do? What medicine will kill the bacteria and micro pathogens but not further damage the kidneys? Baytril is what the vets use for fish with Dropsy and that will be my next research. Baytril does not seem to cause further kidney damage. Perhaps Seachem can start selling Baytril medication for fish. Baytril is already being sold over the counter for pet birds so why not make it for ornamental fish too. FDA does not want Baytril to be used in animals meant for human food because of human antibiotic resistance and thats why its controlled and has warnings not to use on farm animals for human food but it is/can be approved to be use over the counter for pet meds like it already is for pet birds. Baytril is popular for use with small animals and ornamental pet fish. Seachem get busy... get Baytril approved for over the counter ornamental fish medication.

The picture makes it seem like mostly 'bits' but it's really mostly hay. She is perfectly happy with it but I likely wouldn't buy it again - I'm also unsure about at least a couple ingredients that are unnecessary - carob beans?? And rabbits should not have anything from the onion family - that includes garlic and leeks! They are so small that I'm not too worried since I give this to her in small amounts and infrequently (she's spoiled enough already) but really that should NOT be in there. Calendula and carob beans and such may technically be edible, but I hope they are talking to a rabbit specialist vet or something before throwing this stuff in, and the leeks make me think this is not the case.. Overall, rabbit loves it but there are some little indications of perhaps a lack of research and since it's mostly hay, I likely won't be purchasing again.

I was disappointed to find that the 12mm thread to accommodate the ornamental ball mount was not clean, so that I couldn't screw the bolt through it, and I had to purchase a 12 mm x 1.75 mm pitch tap to clean it out. That worked and after a couple of passes of the tap II was able to attach the ball. Then I found that when I tightened down the single set screw, it pulled the opposing side of the assembly toward the pole causing the truck & ball to be skewed. The problem is due to the gap on that opposing inner surface, at the pulley opening, which would have been easily avoided if this unit had three setscrews, as all of the larger models do. The appearance of the resulting skewed assembly and the attached ornamental ball looked really tacky. I considered adding two more setscrews, and I may still attempt that, but in the meantime I inserted a couple of rubber shims on the opposing surfaces to prevent, or at least minimize the skewing. I don't know how well that will hold up over time. The manufacturer should take more care with the finish of the threaded opening, and the decision to use a single setscrew was a poor design choice. UPDATE: I drilled, tapped and added a couple of 1/4" x 20 setscrews at 120 degree positions, and was then able to properly attach I align the truck. I was worried that the cast aluminum would be brittle, and difficult to tap cleanly, but it went very well. It is now nicely in line with the pole. I used some blue LocTite on the setscrews to avoid them loosening over time.

This review is for the PS4 game of the year edition. Even though it has been a long time since I played the original Dragon Age, it has stayed in my mind as a fun and memorable one. Unfortunately, DA2 ended up as being a bit of a disappointment to me, losing some of the things that made the first DA memorable. So I was a bit reluctant to get Inquisition as I was not sure if I would get a return to form or more of the same linear limited open world of DA2, but decided to take a chance after the price dropped to under 20 bucks. Like the previous game, I was able to import the results from the previous games into Inquisition, which seems to affect the story a bit and character interactions, which is a nice touch to show the results of your previous games actions. DA2 was a bit more action-oriented hack and slash kind of combat RPG, whereas DA was more of a traditional RPG with more dialogue and choices with dialogue that affects the game. I personally preferred the gameplay of the first game than the hack and slash DA2. Inquisition seems to have balanced that a bit more, bringing back some of the best elements of the first DA, but the combat seems to still mostly a hack and slash repeating tapping of buttons with little strategy. Like DA2, I save often to avoid some combat frustration. There is a bit more weapon and armor variety than in DA2 and you can even create weapons and armor with even more variation. Like in DA2, the inventory and character menus are still a bit cumbersome to use as it is a bit of a burden to figure out which weapon/armor is best suited to the character with the same restrictions on certain weapons and armor that DA2 has. The skill tree is back, but like DA2, it is a bit of a chore to navigate. There are plenty of skills to choose from but I really would have liked if it was a bit more simplified that is easier to see on one screen versus the multiple you have to scroll through to find an ability you want. As with DA2, you have a group of four characters in your party which should be diverse in order to successfully complete the various quests. One of the best things of the first DA was the story, which I felt was lacking in DA2. Fortunately, the story is much improved over DA2 with good characters and character interactions. Considering this game is now 4 years old, the graphics are good and much better than the first two games, but still feel a bit dated compared to some of the newer games out now. The various characters have good detail with smooth animation. Like the previous games, there are camera glitches, especially during combat. Some of the same sound glitches that have occurred in DA2, where there is no sound or doesnt match the characters, occur on occasion. You would think that some of the bugs from the previous game would have been fixed for this one. One of my big gripes with DA2 was the limited game world map that used the same areas with very little variety throughout the game. Inquisition has a lot more depth with a variety of areas to explore. There are still some limitations with the game world but the tremendous number of places to explore and things to do makes for a great improvement over DA2. If anything, there may be too much to do and explore here. There are also some new elements added such as the war table, similar to some of the AC games. For me, I am not so sure I like that part of the game, but your actions open up new areas to explore, without having to navigate all over the map to find the areas. As it is with games that have a lot of quests (main/side), some are more interesting than others and get a bit repetitive after a while. There are plenty of interesting characters to interact with and the voice acting is good decent for the most part. The various party members have different skill sets and background stories that are affected by your game decisions, which provides some depth to the game. To me, the different menu interfaces seems a bit dated and could have been better done overall as it all seems a bit clunky in my opinion. The game balance is better with lots of places to explore and a good story that is affected by the multiple character interactions. Combat is a bit too simplified and there are some of the same glitches from the past games but overall is an improvement over DA2 and fun to play, but some quests are bland and monotonous. To me, this is a decent game overall, but not as good as the first DA game and I like the Elder Scroll series a bit better. 4 stars

Hummers love this one. There are eight ports and we routinely get a full house. The ports are well-spaced so they all drink cooperatively. They drain it fast! Also, orioles seem to be able to feed from it, too, another plus. The only negative is that you can't put hot sugar-water in it to sort of sterilize the glass like you can some of the other thicker glass ones. I like to pour the hot syrup in the other ones and let it cool before hanging. This one you have to let the syrup cool first and then load. Another slight negative is that occasionally bees get a bit crazy with this one and go for the little bit of nectar right around the junction of bottle and base, so I cut a thin ring of clear plastic to act as a collar and that excludes them. But for the other benefits, it's well worth it.

I am just a do it yourselfer so I was reluctant to install this as it seems like it would be like a jigsaw puzzle and would be complicated. Turns out it was not complicated at all. It took about two hours to remove the old cartridges and clean all what was left over of the DE in filter housing and install the new filter. There is one piece that is smaller than all the rest that is the key and the manifold inidcated where it goes. The rest are the same size. In my case there is a nut attached to a long rod that just spins, so I got some help. It is a lot easier with help. After installing it I ran the pump for a couple of hours and there was a huge difference in the pool water. The next day the pool was cleaner than ever before. So it works perfectly. The cleaning before replacing was really the time consuming part. Replacing it and reinstalling the housing must have taken about 20 minutes.

My lounge chair cover lasted about two and one-half years covering a stack of four chairs all year-round in a mid-Atlantic climate, all the while developing stress cracks in various spots in the outer layer. At first, I covered these cracks with Gorilla Tape, which worked fairly well as a temporary solution, but, this is not a long-term solution, obviously. The biggest flaw and my biggest gripe with respect to the Classic Accessories Ravenna cover series is that the covers are made out of a dark brownish-colored material that necessarily absorbs sunlight and thus offers very poor ultraviolet radiation resistance. Once situated outside and exposed to daily sunlight, these covers fade from dark brown to lighter shades of brown very quickly, which of course means that solar radiation is inflicting damage upon the covers and weakening their structure and their strength. The cover worked well, initially, and feature some good design traits, such as leg buckles and air vents, but, the inherent design flaw of the poor color choice makes this a flawed product, in my opinion. Now that I have to replace this cover, I am avoiding the Classic Accessories brand and am searching for covers that are white, or, a similar color, to provider better resistance to ultraviolet fading and damage.

As if she has never eaten food before. It's useless. There are drainage holes on every container(except for the bottom one). The holes are too small : water does not go through them. And no, it's not because ''seeds are stuck in them'' or because ''you have hard water in your area''. Even without any seeds, water does not flow. That means if you put seeds on every level, every single one of them will rot because they will be submerged in water. FIX: I had to use a drill to go through every hole with a bigger mesh. The holes had to be 3 times their actual size for water to drain. After this tweak, I can finally sprout seeds. My 1st batch were mustard seeds. It went pretty well. I'm now sprouting brocoli and radish seeds. Reading the many reviews it's quite obvious some customers get the product as desdribed and other receive a defective product with smaller holes(rendering the thing useless). I should not have to use a drill to fix a defective product I paid for. There are probably many factories and one of them uses a defective plastic mold. I'm rating it according to what I received. I would have given it zero stars if such an option was available.

This part should be listed as 300mm x 605mm, not 12" x 24". I used it to make 2 magnetic beds which hold high temp magnets for my Taz 4 3D printer. This part can handle the heat to 285F (141C). It machines well. I cut it down with a 5 degree reverse hook thin kerf blade on my sliding miter saw. I taped both sides to prevent chipping. Then I routed it with my CNC router. Came out great to make a removable magnetic bed.

This set doesn't include any security sensors. It's is a normal cordless phone set that also works with cordless VTech security sensors. (It strongly resembles my AT&T cordless system, which makes sense as the two companies have a licensing agreement.) Its strong points as a phone system include excellent range, more than needed battery life, good speaker volume adjustable during use, and good build quality. It has a lot of functions and features, most of which are simple to operate, though it takes some time with the manual to set it up initially. I'll give a more complete list of the features of the phone system below. Security system There are three kinds of sensors that work with this: a motion sensor; a sensor that triggers when a door, window, cabinet or drawer is opened; and a garage door sensor. (See links and prices below.) You can set the system to notify you at the base unit and handsets (up to four) when a sensor is triggered, or you can have the base unit call up to three other numbers, such as your cell phone, office, or neighbor and get a message about which sensor was triggered. If you use the latter "remote" method, you can listen to the sound inside your house via the microphone in the base unit, and even speak through the base unit speaker to someone in the house. You can set conditions for alerts, such what time of day the system is "armed," how long a sensor is triggered before an alert (for example, you can set it so a door must remain open for 15, 30 or 60 minutes before the system will notify you), or delay arming the system for a certain amount of time when you're getting ready to leave the house. Packages This is the package to get if you already have VTech sensors and want a(nother) base unit and handset, but maybe not the best deal if you know you'll want to buy sensors too. Below is what Amazon offers. (If you don't find the combination you want at the price you want, check at the VTech Phones website too: Shop Products (at bottom of page) > Wireless Monitoring System > subcategory With Cordless Phone. They offer combinations not sold elsewhere, with free shipping for orders over $50.) Base Unit and 1 Handset, 1 Garage Door Sensor, and 1 Open/Closed Sensor , currently $107 Base Unit with 2 Handsets, 1 Open/Closed Sensor , currently $130 Handset , currently $18 Open/Closed Sensor , currently $18 Motion Sensor , currently $25 Garage Door Sensor , currently $34 Phone system features -- Great range: goes out past 500 feet where there aren't barriers outside, no trouble with the thick stone walls inside my house -- Handset and base unit speaker volume is adjustable on the fly, from loud enough for quiet room to loud enough for very noisy room, handset speakerphone loud and adjustable too -- Caller ID/call waiting capable, ID announce, stores info for 50 calls -- 50 name and number phonebook directory -- Sensor log with last 50 alerts -- Up to 22 minutes of recording time -- Voicemail message can be up to 3 minutes -- Up to 99 messages saved -- Message retrieval from handset -- Remote access for checking messages, changing sensor alert mode -- Time and date attached to messages -- Voicemail waiting indicator -- Last 10 number dialed kept on redial -- Speed dial for 9 numbers -- Backlit handset keypad and display, large backlit base display (but no backlight for base keypad) -- Table- and wall-mountable -- Battery charging on base unit takes about 30 minutes to use handset, 12 hours for full charge -- ECO mode battery-conserving technology, battery power lasts longer than any call I've ever made -- Replace battery alert for sensors -- Quiet mode, select 1-12 hours, sensor alerts and paging to find handset remain on, answering system takes calls -- Mute microphone -- Call blocking -- Call screening -- Call intercept -- Conference between an outside line and up to 2 handsets -- Any key answering -- Intercom between handsets and base unit -- Expandable up to 5 cordless handsets (only 4 for some functions) or up to 400 sensors -- Voice guide to help set up answering system -- DECT 6.0 digital technology -- Full-duplex handset and base speakerphones (talk and receive at once) -- California Energy Commission (CEC) compliant, with battery charging test mode -- Choice of English, Spanish or French prompts If you have caller ID you can set the system to reset the time and date automatically with each incoming call. Otherwise you have to manually reset the correct time and date after each power outage, if you want to know when voicemail was received. Winter use Those of us who live where it gets cold in the winter should keep in mind that the operating temperature range of the sensors only goes down to 0 degrees F (-18 C). That's a limitation of the batteries. So any sensors you have outside, and some unheated garage door sensors, may not work during part of the winter. Repeated freezing and thawing may also damage batteries, so it might be best to remove outside ones during part of the winter. Warranty One-year repair-or-replace warranty against defects in materials and workmanship. If you register the system at the VTech website you get three additional months coverage. Made in China.

I purchased this in Feb 2018. I am reporting on it after only 4 months of daily use. I have really liked this collar a lot. Lightweight, easy on, easy off and does what you expect. When dog pulls, it cinches down so it doesn't slide off and doesn't choke your dog like a standard collar or noose style lead. I would recommend this if your dog is not a lunger or hard tugger, is rather passive or of an average active nature. I am giving it 3 stars instead of 2 stars because I believe it is great under the right circumstance. In fact under the right circumstance, perhaps would have rated this 4 stars. My circumstance is not right for this collar, it is too fragile for a powerful and motivated dog. Therefore, a lesson learned that I am sharing with you. I use this in concert with a standard collar which has the ID tags etc. I do not double up a safety connection to the standard collar. Last night my 45 lb 3 y/o Walker Coonhound was excited about a squirrel or racoon and pulled hard to get to the tree, a common occurrence. This time (after about 4 months of this type of activity) the metal O-ring used to connect the collar to the leash actually broke, and BANG!! She was off and running, howling and trying to climb a tree to get whatever was up there. Luckily I was able to reach her and get a solid grip on her other collar before she blasted off to find another critter. The last time she got away from me it took me over 6 hours to track her down. This would have been a bad situation. Naturally, this happens after the return period. Now, I cannot trust this collar... I won't replace it, I doubt I will try a DIY to rig this up for use nor can I spend a ton of time trying to get a new ring put on at a tack shop/etc, so it will likely end up in the trash, money wasted.

I purchased two of these Hori protectors from seller JADD ENTERTAINMENT with FBA (fulfilled by Amazon). This is the third out of four PS Vita Slim protector brands I have tried. This Hori failed me just like the brands Gam3Gear and Akwox. Only the brand Mr Shield did everything I wanted from any screen protector. Anyways, I read the comments and watched videos of how to apply this Hori screen protector so I came prepared. I used a strong electric air blower and a keyboard vacuum during the whole installation process. I wanted to make sure no dust was anywhere on the device screens. I even made sure to blow dry the underside that you are suppose to peel off, steps 2 and 3 backing sheets. So when it is slowly being peeled off it hopefully wouldn't drop any dust particles all over the screen when the protector starts to adhere onto the device. Steps 1, 2, 3 and 4 process was a pain for the first PS Vita device I installed this on. There was a lot of particles that got caught when the two backing got peeled off, sigh. I had to use scotch tape onto the protector to lift it and remove the particles stuck under the actual screen protector. It was not a fun process. On the second device was the same but this time there was a rainbow effect on the underside of the screen protector. I completely forgot to take a photo as the whole process stressed me out. No matter how much I pressed the protector, the rainbow effect just wouldn't go away. I ended up applying a different protector (Mr Shield) on the second device that worked so much better and took less than 3 minutes to apply. I will be removing this Hori protector off the first device as I don't feel it will protect my screen at all. Waste of $30 for 2 of these terrible Hori film "protectors". This protector is also not edge to edge size as I was hoping for. It is also one of the thinnest screen protector I have ever come across... so thin you wonder if it'll even protect anything. It's thinner than the protective backing that you peel off the protector during application. It also has an annoying straight line in the middle of the protector from the peel off backing. It comes in 2 separate backing film that you peel right then the left (steps 2 and 3). Step 1 is a tab you peel off the back and it's what you use to stick onto the screen while you align the protector. This step 1 was useless because as you start peeling off the backing, the whole protector starts to move around even when you are firmly pressing it down. The only positive things I can say about this protector is how invisible it looks once applied and how easy it was to get the bubbles out. The thinness of this item is what makes it look seamless and almost invisible. The question is, how much will it actually protect your screen? This is literally like the film you find on brand new electronic devices when you first buy them that protects it from fingerprints... even though this Hori still catches fingerprints like crazy. For the price and only getting one thin film type "protector" per kit, I'd look elsewhere if you are serious about keeping your screen from any serious scratches. ETA: I decided to play with my Vita before removing this Hori protector. I didn't like the feel of the protector at all, had a very semi-rough cheap film feeling to it. There was some missed touch screen responses that had me worried that it might of been my brand new Vita, but come to find out it wasn't soon as I did the Welcome Park app. The line that I mention in my original review was annoyingly visible during gameplays that had white/light colored backgrounds. It's hard to capture a close up picture of the imperfections unless you see them in person. Even after my hard work of almost 2 hours of applying this protector on two different Vitas, there was still so many imperfections underneath the protector. Removing the two film backing while it adheres to the screen is such a major flaw imo, so many dust will fall... just look closely and use a flashlight if you already own this protector. The screen looks better without this protector on. The feel, middle line and collected dust outweighs the esthetic of "invisible" look of this protector. Extremely disappointed with this product and have no idea why so many praised it.

It does work, but let me explain the good and bad. The Good: *It does auto scoop after the cat uses the box, if it hits a tough spot, it will stop, back up, and try again. *It can be plugged in OR use batteries. *Great for a SMALL cat or kitten! The Bad: *It is very noisy when it does scoop, it scared the crap outta my dog, and my cat for the first few days. *The poop dump box is really tiny, you basically have to change it every day and that will get costly! I took advice and use the "Outlining it with a bag" idea. It makes it last a little longer but the disposable plastic will start to crack and break as you try and pull it out and put it back for each cleaning. Overall: I think it is just as much work as a normal litter box, if not a bit more. You have to dump the poop box, scrape off anything that might be stuck to the scooper, clean the mess you will make trying to get that disposable poo box out of the clips to dump it. Top of the cat litter. Though my dad's cat does not make it easy as he has decided that he likes to dig all the way to the bottom of the cat box to pee, then covers it up. So the litterbox struggles with that as pee and litter stick to the bottom. Plus, the cat only pee's on ONE side, so that side of the disposable poo container is maxed out, come the second use. When he goes, I basically have to clean the box twice a day. The only reason I'm going to use it is because my dads cat will pee on anything if the litterbox has anything in it at all, when I work full time and can't clean the box every time the cat takes a pee, the automatic will at least scoop it to the side for me to clean when I get home, thus the cat seems to agree with this.

May 12 2018 update: picked up a few packs from my local asian market. The noodles are now shrink wrapped inside the package and the vegis are mixed into the soup base packet. This means if you hate cilantro you cannot avoid it with this brand now. I'm sooo disappointed. A great instant pho! If the noodles seem a bit too hard to handle, slippery, always escaping the utensil, try breaking them into very small pieces before cooking. They snap easily and once they are small enough to sit on a spoon you can enjoy the noodles without chasing them around. I like to add a splash of fish sauce and sriracha, and if I have time a little thin sliced beef and spring onions. The latest one I bought had no cilantro/culantro in the ingredients and for the most part I was thrilled, except I did get a random cilantroey ghost of flavor in a few bites. If you hate cilantro, good deal! If you love it, you can always add more! All in all a satisfying, quick, and flavorful meal. Edit: After having another packet, I figured out the cilantro I was tasting. Eryngium is in the ingredients, which with investigation is culantro which apparently tastes really similar to cilantro. If you don't want that flavor leave out the vegi pack and you'll still have a great instant pho!

I recently bought a triumph motorcycle jacket, and although I didn't expect the striping on the jacket to be pure white, I also was not expecting them to be a dark beige either based off all the marketing photos of the jacket. Things to mention...it took about 5 coats to get it to look natural. Otherwise, the paint lines were still very visible. Also, the texture of the paint in general seem to me like regular paint that has thinner added to it to help it conform to the texture of leather creases. That being said, this paint is expensive for what you are getting. If I had a big project such as a sofa or full sized jacket I needed to paint, I think I'd try and mix a batch myself. But for this small project and not wanting to experiment with my expensive jacket it worked fine. I also used 2 top coats of the matte sealer to protect the paint job. We'll see how it all holds up as I have not for a chance to ride since painting it.

My first Dechra product is Dermabenss. My dog, a cocker spaniel, I rescued 4 years has a very bad skin issue. It was horrible going through this problem. I feel so bad for my dog and at the same time too many time offs & not to mention financial expenditure is crazy. Too many medications, and tests didn't cure the underlying issue. It was just crazy repetitive cycle. A relief is just a month after regimen of meds, and we are back at the same point again. But thank God buying dog insurance early on really helped a lot. What I have learned in this journey & by doing a lot of research is, the moment a dog starts to itch, pimple on the back, elevated temperature, loss appetite are tell-tale signs of onslaught of skin issues. I made too many mistakes that everytime this happens we rush to visit vet. But what they do is give same meds, tests, etc, but what they don't tell you that there is management so that our beloved dogs don't have to take antibiotics all the time by just using good medicated shampoo. Recently, my dog started to show the symptoms I mentioned, to think it was just a month of finishing her MRSA meds. The same MRSA med that human take that cost $4 K, yes you read it right. But for dogs it cost $300.00 plus. It is a very potent med. I vowed never again my dog will endure this cycle. I immediately bought Dermabenss and after one wash I saw the difference. I was so elated that I bought 2 more Dechra products. I got Miconahex for her yeast in her paws, TRIZ 4 for pimple/pyoderma. I now give her every other day bath. I use 2 shampoos, miconahex for her paws and I alternate between Dermabenss and Triz4. I also clean her ears every bath, and I now clip her hair to maintain shorter coat. I leave these shampoos for 15 min and I do have a timer to be sure. It has been an exhausting 4 years finding the best approach. Id rather bath her frequently with these shampoos rather than put her small body to be ravaged by these meds for a relief of a month. Not worth it! Sorry for writing this long, but I just want to share our journey. Im happy that by doing research & reading articles about dog skin issues showed me that although theres no cure theres management. Im glad that I settled for Dechra products after reading a lot of reviews. It really does work I am so blown away.
