Seemingly well built product (time will tell, like 4-5 years from now), and the instructions aren't bad albeit meant for RV, shed roofs and such, as installation via the included bolts, nuts and such would not work well with a travel trailer roof (relatively thin EDPM or TPO on most, stretched over very thin plywood - nothing's going to hold a screw well) where one needs to locate the underlying support beams (usually not more than 2" wide each) and figure out if such are metal, usually aluminum, or wood - never mentioned in the Renogy stuff. The controller is probably the money conserving component for Renogy (a friend has a controller from another manufacturer and albeit expensive it seems rock solid and is waterproof), but the panel and wiring/connectors are fine as are the roof connectors if not involving a travel trailer. Speaking of wiring, the Renogy wire is 10 gauge (I measured), and that's fine if only one 100 watt panel is involved and the distance between your battery and the controller is real short - which it should be - if not consider 8 gauge. I have another wiring set from a different vendor (extensively listed on Amazon) that was billed as 10 gauge, and it isn't - maybe 12 gauge at best....consequences if one expects full amperage transfer from controller to battery. Read the negative reviews carefully when purchasing extra wiring or connectors, not just the positive stuff, as some folks actually have a micrometer handy. That is not a Renogy issue from my 1 experience so far.

I bought these primarily for smoking. When taking large cuts of meat off the smoker, the WORST thing I could do would be to drop one. Tongs, spatulas, etc, were just too risky. With these, I can just pick up a 15 pound brisket, and put it in a tray. If you hold something hot for too long, the heat will penetrate them. So...don't do that. I found that if I pull a grate out of my smoker, at 225F, it is just cooling to a comfortable temperature as I start to really feel the heat. That's about perfect. At 300F, I would probably need to put the grate down, or use another layer of insulation. Dexterity is not great, but that's too be expected, when you're wearing thick rubber gloves.

Our English Mastiff mix was badly undernourished when we rescued him; at around 13 weeks, he was under 15 pounds, his ribs showed very clearly, and his stomach was caved in - no puppy belly at all. We've had him a couple of weeks now, and he weighs 35 pounds. He's gaining about a pound a day, but he's not overweight at all; one can still feel ribs and he has a normal puppy belly. His coat is gorgeously healthy, he feels good and is active and alert, his ears are in great shape; he loves the food. We may switch him to salmon or other non-chicken, non-beef formula because I understand that chicken, beef, and grain allergies are common. I see no harm in avoiding the potential for skin, ear, or other issues by feeding salmon or lamb, etc. instead. Photo 1 was taken at the shelter. He looked much less nourished than the photo indicates when we rescued him. The video and photos 3 and 4 show him at 2+ weeks after rescue.

Bought this generator 7 years ago. Only reason I'm reviewing it NOW is I'm generator shopping again. I go to my farm in TN several times a year and while I usually run a Honda EU1000i for things like lighting, charging cell phones, or running laptops or a ham radio as well as a coffee maker, it can NOT handle: saws all, circular saw, or pretty much anything over 900 W. We've also used this at home in MD during ice storms and other things that have killed the power for several days on end. I also have a military diesel 5kW generator to run the whole house (less water heater and AC) and can run several portable AC units or space heaters all day at about 1/2gallon of diesel per hour. And quiet, very quiet. Interestingly, the B&S generator will, or at least has run a small window air conditioner, or coffee pot, all the electrical hand tools I'd need to use around the farm (no grid electricity yet and solar's not installed yet either) but for one main issue. It can, literally, take upwards of 5 minutes to start. 10-15 pulls on the starter. Or used to. I never crank it up without pulling the carb/air filter cover and blasting ether starting fluid into it. Then it only takes maybe 3 pulls to get it fired up. This has been a constant since new - more or less. Started with just a few pulls when absolutely new. I only use no-ethanol gas in my small-engine gadgets (I've replaced too many carburetors over the years for melted seals/gaskets and gum build up) like chainsaw, weed wacker, lawn mower, post hole digger, tiller and generators, run the things dry several times and drain carbs when I can (on units that can be drained). The B&S is the most cantankerous of all the units I own, which is interesting because there's a B&S engine on my log splitter that's been a bear to start after the first season's use and I've been using it annually since 2003. I rarely take it out of the box any more. Unfortunately I had it for too long to return for refund by the time I figured this all out. Gotta do more generator shopping now.

As a user of the most recent upgrade of Microsoft Office Im told (by a pop-up window) I must accept one of two choices before I am allowed to use the software I paid for. Either I must allow Microsoft to fully monitor and report back to Microsoft how I use apps, features, and devices on my computer, or I must agree to allow Microsoft only to basically monitor and report back on Office settings and device capabilities on my computer. There is no option that allows me to decline Microsoft surveillance. I can postpone granting Microsoft permission to track the use of my apps and devices by closing the pop-up window (the one that offers me no option but to agree) and upon doing so I am permitted to use my software until the next time I open Office, when again I am blocked from using Word or Excel until I give Microsoft permission to snoop. Or until I close the pop-up window, which again gives me annoyingly temporary access to my software. Endlessly, I suppose. I could comment on Microsoft upgrades generally, and how the company seems to have a malevolent genius for identifying those features of Word and Excel that I actual find quite useful and over-enhancing them until they are essentially unusable. I could comment on that but theres hardly space.

I purchased this box because of the offer to get a $9.99 credit and thought I'd take a chance. There wasn't anything in the box I was especially interested in beyond that. While none of the snack samples were bad, I don't think there are many that I'd consider buying regularly with maybe one or two exceptions: -Doritos Blaze: Much too spicy for my taste. The flavor was OK, but the feeling of pure heat on my tongue was extremely unpleasant, and I will not be trying them again. I understand that for a lot of people the extreme heat is the selling point, but it's just not for me. -Doritos Crunch Mix: This was a very pleasant surprise. I like the individual ingredients, and the distribution of the various types was also very good. I never felt like I was left with "too many chex" like you can sometimes find in these mixes. The "crunch nuts", while not the nomenclature I would have used, were delicious, with a thin coating of breading over a nut. Not something I thought I was missing in life, but it turns out I was. -Sahale Snacks Tangerine Vanilla Cashews and Macadamia Nuts: These were the star of the box for me. Not only is the unexpected combination of "creamsicle" and nuts absolutely delicious, the quality of the ingredients was excellent. The flavors tasted like the real thing, and not artificial like you'd get with some candies or flavored drinks. -Milano Cookies: There's not much that needs to be said about Milanos, a nearly perfect cookie. These are an absolute classic with the perfect cookie-to-chocolate ratio, and it turns out three is exactly as many as I need to have to feel satisfied. -Hippeas snack puffs - I'm not sure if the bag I received was under-filled, but there were exactly 4 puffs in the bag. I wasn't really able to get any real sense of if I enjoyed them or not. The vegan white cheddar flavoring was OK, I just can't really form an opinion. -Rxbar Kids Chocolate Chip: We gave this to our 21 month old daughter, and she really seemed to enjoy it. She was very excited about the "cholockate" chips, and ate almost the entire bar, which is a glowing recommendation from her. I tried the remaining bite and thought it tasted very good, with a pleasingly firm yet chewy/crumbly texture which was great for her. I am definitely a fan of the simplified ingredients and can see myself buying these for her in the future. -Barnana Banana Coconut Snacks: Unfortunately, I did not care for these and ended up not finishing the whole bag. The taste was unappealing; slightly banana-flavored, slightly coconut flavored, but at the same time tasting a little like an old apple smells. The texture was likewise unpleasant; very chewy to the point of sticking to my teeth. -Rxbar Chocolate Sea Salt: Overall I enjoyed this snack, but I think there was probably too much of the sea salt. The flavor was good enough with the nuttiness of the almonds and cashews, but I'm not really looking for a bunch of salt when eating a flavor primarily of dates and chocolate. Overall, I was pleased with these snacks. Some of them I know to steer clear of in the future (I really, really regret the Doritos Blaze), but for others I may have found a few new go-to snacks!

I recently purchased this starter kit and it's fantastic. If you already have blank cards, you can use the dies included to embellish your cards, otherwise you'd need to buy one of the card making dies. The included box die makes an adorable, albeit small, gift box that also can be left as is or embellished with what is included. If you are a crafter, you know how slippery the slope can be for a new crafting adventure, but I can say that after scouring the options available, this is a great starter kit for a reasonable price. Indeed you do need to purchase other dies and paper to really bring out the machine's potential but it has been worth the adventure so far to me. Note that the acrylic platforms will get very, very messed up in use and will sound like they're going to crack in half or the handle will be hard enough to turn you will think you're about to break something. They will not crack and so far nothing has broken even when the handle has been difficult to turn. The manufacturer says to rotate the platforms for even wear, but I've opted to use only one plate as the base and let the other take the brunt of the cutting and will replace them one at a time as needed. Also, I will try putting the die, paper, and then another sheet of perhaps tissue paper in the "sandwich" so that the plate will receive at least a bit less wear while still letting the machine cut through my paper. Photos one and two show what was made with separately purchased card dies. Photo two shows the gift box included in the set (I used the flowers dies from another set).

Our church recently remodeled, and replaced our acoustic drums with electric drums. The size of our sanctuary just couldn't handle acoustic very well (too small/loud). The problem was, once we hooked up the electric drums we had an issue where they would periodically "clip" (pop in the speakers) when hit harder. This didn't seem to be a consistent repeatable thing, it just kept happening consistently-random. To avoid this, we had to pull the drum kit volume down to 3 or less (Default is 13). Additionally, there was this TERRIBLE BUZZ in the speakers. NO matter what volume level you put the drum kit on, the buzz was still there (Same level). When we pulled the drum volume down on the mixing board, we could reduce the volume of the buzz, but not eliminate it altogether, and then, all the other instruments/vocals were overpowering the drums. So i did some research and concluded that we must be experiencing a "Ground Loop". After reading reviews on this product, i though i would give it a try. I installed this between the drum kit and the snake. When i powered on the sound system, i was shocked that there was NO SOUND. No buzz, no hum, no noise of any kind. it didn't even sound like i had turned anything on. Our sound system has NEVER been that quiet, even before the remodel. My first thought was "Man, this thing isn't working, now i have NO sound". As i walked toward the box to start trouble shooting, it occurred to me that i should at least TEST the drums first. Guess what, they WORKED. The box was just THAT GOOD. Praise God. Additionally, this also seemed to fix the Popping / Clipping volume issue we had previously as i can now put the drums back on the default volume level of 13 with no issues.

This pen is what started my craze for Fisher space pens. I had a bullet pen for a while, but hated the refill. I bought a Capomatic but it felt cheap and clumsy. I bought this and fell in love. It still feels like a cheap metal Chinese pen, but it's actually made in the USA. Sadly, I try to steer clear from things made in the USA these days because they're so cheaply made compared to American-made products from our grandparents' generation and before. But you typically buy a Fisher pen for the pressurized refill, which I must say they improved upon over the last 8 or so years since I got my bullet pen. Now they write nice and dark and don't require all that much pressure. They're also very smooth. The only downside is that you still have to write at a near vertical angle in order for the ball-tip to make proper contact with the paper. I think that Fisher just needs to improve the design of their tips - more the shape than the construction of it. The tip extends too far out past the pen, and the ball is covered by a high cup on all sides, so you have very little leeway in writing at any angle less than say, 80 or 70 degrees. Most pens let you write at angles closer to 30 degrees in my experience because they let the tip extend out just far enough, and because their ball tips are more exposed, leaving you with a greater writing surface than Fisher pens have.

4-Star Printer -- 2-Star Software. Excellent printer let down by difficult to use and buggy P-Touch Software. Printer installation was simple following the clear instructions provided. I connected the printer wirelessly and via USB cable. During installation you must go to Brother's website and download the drivers and printer installation software. This requires registering the printer. Once the drivers are installed you then go through the printer installation process. This went well and was completed in ten minutes. My printer turns on, connects wirelessly to my network and is recognized by my computer. Next, I installed the Brother P-Touch Software and this is where my problems began and continue. I can't get the software to print a simple mailing label wirelessly despite the printer indicating it is connected to my computer. Each time I try to print a label the printer either says "no paper is installed" or the incorrect roll is installed. Once I connect the printer with a USB cable labels print out. There is clearly a problem between the software and printer in the wireless mode. I find the P-Touch software very user unfriendly. I spent an hour trying to build a simple mailing label which would print out correctly. To print a simple horizontal mailing label I have to tell P-Touch Software to print in "vertical" orientation. I admit I haven't spent hours reading the Software Owners Guide. It shouldn't be this difficult. The P-Touch Software and printer seem to not cooperate. In wireless mode when a label built in P-Touch software is too wide for the paper roll in the printer, the printer simply goes into error mode flashing the red LED and has to be turned off and back on to recover. The printer itself is excellent and gets 4-stars but it is marred by substandard software for printing labels. Until Brother fixes the software interface to this printer I will continue to be frustrated..

PLEASE READ UPDATE BELOW I loved this pedal, for bass as well as guitar, until it died on me last night. Tested again today - lights up when switched but no effect at all. Contacted Digitech to see if they'll make it good, but very disappointed. Very strange, since Digitech pedals are usually quite reliable. UPDATE: I called Digitech / Harman and even though I was over a month out of the warranty period they granted me a courtesy RMA. I could have taken it to a local shop but chose to pay to ship it to them. @ 1/2 weeks later I received it back, or I received a brand new one - can't tell. GREAT customer service! Boy I missed this pedal. It really gets attention when using as a 12-string sim on guitar, but where it shines for me is on bass. I turn the Tone control up to 3:00 and the mix (Level) control down to 11:00 or less for a subtle octave up blend. Absolutely no detectable latency when used live. Tracks perfectly with zero artifacts or collisions between multiple notes - this may be the only octave pedal out there that works well for chords. I'm hooked, if aliens steal it I'll buy another. Minor con - the knob indicators are mostly invisible in any kind of stage lights, so I stick on a couple white pointers cut from adhesive labels.

Bought this to try out considering the FT-569 impressed me so much. This is for all intents and purposes, the exact same thing without the tripod section. You do however have a screw on section at the base to screw on tripod accessory though. The next thing you will realize is.. it extends QUITE far... firther than most average selfie sticks. The aluminum body is locked by pressure lockers and have held it firmly in place, which brings me to one of my gripes: quality control. I've noticed one of the lockers have become difficult to close in, and at times after a quite loud audible "SNAP" (which makes you believe you broke the thing), it easily closes down. This is again I believe a QC issue, so don;t let that sway you... it is indeed a great unit. The other gripe I have is the cellphone spring loaded adapter. Now to be fair, they included both this and the screw release type, however the spring type is of a generic make and they tend quite often to break right at the base attachment... and also the rubber pull tends to tear off... a different design would be a plus. But what makes that adapter unique, is the reflective "sightglass" attachment for people with only a rear facing camera, or who just wants a better quality shot the rear cam provides... you simply look into the reflective section and shoot. All in all other than the small issues, it is a really nice unit for small gadgets like your gopro or phone... even a small compact cam. I don't recommend this for DSLRs.

EDIT: I use because of my power smoking neighbors and I have THREE of these in my small apartment, as well as an air purifier. When something is ON GOING, you need more, you need to replace more often and I found these with the air purifier helps me immensely. It's the WORST about 430 AM when they both wake up and start puffing. The combo of these jars (one in kitchen, two in my bedroom) with all the contents exposed on the lid in front of the ac unit, PLUS a small air purifier closer to my bed, allows me to sleep past 430 am without waking up and/or feeling ill. When there is an ongoing smell, like smokers near you, you need to maximize your efforts and this really helps me. _______________________________________________________ FIRST, and most important, if you have STRONG odors, TIP the jar upside down on the lid so all the contents are exposed. MAKES A HUGE DIFFERENCE than just removing the lid. Also you will easily see when you need to replace. IT WILL SMELL FAIRLY STRONG for a day or two, but then the odor starts to dissipate. These puppies are strong! Once the smell of the bad air sponge lessens, they're STILL WORKING at absorbing and you don't have to replace til the contents are mostly gone. I have POWER smokers leasing the first floor of a large OLD (1800's) house and a man across from me who cooks....roadkill....that has really gone bad? I'm on the second floor and smoke rises, so that is my BIGGEST issue. There are LOTS of cracks and crevices in old houses and when you share one, whatever your neighbor is cooking or smoking, in essence, so are you. These air sponges (I put two in my 12 X 14 bedroom) HELP A LOT. I put one on one side of my room where my bed is and one near my computer. WITH ONGOING smells, smoking, cooking, you DO NEED more of these, and to replace more often, but these and sealing up heating vents AND an air purifier help a lot. Unless it's for a really small area, you WILL NEED more than ONE! And if it's consistent, you will need to replace. The worse and more ongoing the smell, the more you will need to replace. BUT they do work and I will keep buying them.

I've got a lot less weeds than my neighbors who don't use Halts or any pre weed for that matter, so it's obviously working. I almost missed it this year as Temps in my area are much warmer in FEB than normal. I got it down right before it rained and before the temps went up. Now I'll hit it again in late AUG and see what it does for fall weed control. Update: 15 Mar 18 If you don't put this product down before the ground temps (not air temps) reach 55 for some weeds it's not going to work. In my area mid FEB is usually good, but I may switch to early FEB in the future to ensure I get it down before ground temps reach 55. This product will do anything in the middle of summer. It works best at a very specific time of seed germination in conjunction with ground temps and that differs based on where you are in the country. Use this web page to find out what the ground temps in your area are. Just open and put your zip code in the search bar. That's it.

I mounted a monitor to this bad boy. Being a bedbound gamer, I got sick of having to deal with laptops only. It took a lot of help and wire management, but I got what is very close to a real desktop experience - in bed! Other reviewers have basically voiced my concerns, so I'll list them simply. - The casters (wheels) are HARD to push in, though they seem sturdy once in. We had to use a rubber mallet to get them in, and I was afraid they were going to break when hitting them in. - The hole-and-pin design for the main tabletop height adjustment would probably be safer and sturdier, HOWEVER, the way it is now is more exact and precise. If it ends up lasting the test of time, it will be better. - I wish the side table was wider!! Maybe just by two inches. My previous overbed table had a larger side table, and it tends to get stuff piled on it, so an extra 1-2 inches would be nice. - Due to the way the bottom frame is shaped, only right handed people (or if you have the side table on the right side) will benefit from the sturdy U shape. I have it so the side table is on the left, and the frame is more towards me - however, as can be seen, I mounted a monitor and it seems to still be sturdy. Overall, this table is worth the price. The main larger table is very spacious and will fit even the biggest laptops on the market today, so if you are asking if your laptop fits - YES. It fit my alienware 17" with plenty to spare. Nows just hoping all the stuff I have on it doesn't come crashing down on me. It seems pretty sturdy so my confidence is high. For the usual person, this table would be more then sturdy enough. I think I've proved it with my images. (Although I've had it for less than a week, keep in mind)

All of the translucent Solutech PLA filament colors that I have tried (Blue, Red, Clear) were brittle. They also contained an excessive number of air bubbles. On prints with lots of retractions in a small area (aka: prints with multiple small islands), the extruder drive gear frequently grinds through the filament. This leads to failed prints. This sucks. It is possible to down-grade retraction settings and prevent this. However, diminished retraction settings lead to stringing. Therefore, this filament leaves me stuck between a rock (stringing) and a hard place (grinding). It's not worth the trouble. That said, Solutech opaque colors seem to be OK. Colors are vibrant, but filament diameter variation is frequently greater than the stated +/- .03.

I want to thank all the reviewers who talked about the game and recommended it because for the longest time I thought this was a cheaply made first person parkour runner type game. I was way wrong in thinking that and if it wasent for you guys I would have never bought this. While looking for ps4 exclusives to play (since everything I have is on steam nowadays) I kept seeing this pop up on my amazon recommendations and decided to read why people were so hyped about it. I purchased the first game remaster on the recent psn sale for $10 (to my knowledge the physical copy isn't available in the US and is overpriced import..) and fell in love with it even for a vita game so buying this ps4 designed game was a no brainer. This exponentially raises the bar on everything that made the first game great. Sure online aspects have shut down but they don't deter from the gameplay and enjoying the world and interactions of everything. Kats random voice sounds are adorable in each conversation, its not too "family friendly" but not too over the top violence. Its like watching a wholesome anime with good action/plot moments. Half of the time I am flying around just collecting random crystals and actually go out of my way to do sidequests and challenges as they come up just to get the full experience. The graphics, lighting, models, are really well done and everything is extremely fluid even when shifting gravity around and whipping through the air. Heres to hoping ps5 comes out soon so they can make a third one since developers said they would only make it on ps5/better hardware console :(. Really something to look forward to and definitly a game people should experience. Current setup: Original ps4 connected to 4k 43" roku tv with hdr (yes the tv does clean up the 1080p signal to look better even tho its not ps4pro) Personally I found the best way to savor this is to play in sessions in the evening before bed. so you can just relax and indulge in the gameplay. Coming from finishing uncharted lost legacy, and horizon zero dawn, I thought I would never touch my ps4 again until a game of similar scale was made. Im glad I decided to try this series out.

We have Axon 7 phones at home. My son has retired his phone because of issues he has been having. I suggest you read the reviews in Reddit before you buy. I made the mistake of reading from websites praising the new phone, thinking they are the pros. Reddit complaints: - Nougat update is bad for battery - Battery degrades faster (yes, battery degrades on all phones, but not like this one. Not a year in, my screen time is only less than 4 hours). - Camera's autofocus is glitchy - Daydream will kill your phone (overheats) - it becomes extremely hot, reaches 42 degrees while using their charger - when on call, screen doesn't dim, and your cheeks or ears will be "pressing" on icons - wifi and cell service are intermittent (I often see my phone switching between wifi and data, when the router is in the same room as I am) - bloated software Yes, the other hardware are good. First impression videos and reviews will tell you that. But that's just it, those people have not experienced owning this phone. For a budget phone, it does seem like a good deal. But there are other budget phones that will not overheat.

My Sharpie laundry pen dried out because I didn't cap it tightly enough, I went to buy another and price was 5x what it used to be. Lo and behold, Sharpie discontinued the laundry pen. I phone called Sharpie, and they said Sharpie Stained is the replacement for the laundry pen. I gotta write my kids' names on clothes and stuff, so I got a set. Sharpie Stained has a different tip than a regular Sharpie and than a laundry pen. Sharpie Stained have a brush tip that is longer and pointier than the older laundry pen style. They also look odd and have a splatter design, not like the traditional Sharpie. Most of this is rebranding to be more hip/cool/non-office. It's fine to write with on fabric. I feel it's less suitable than laundry pen for writing on hard surfaces, and I don't like the change to the new tip shape, but I don't care enough to pay more for a "new old stock" laundry pen. Some projects I had going, like a board where I marked my kids' heights, now have very slightly different pen style. Holds up fine to washing. Regular Sharpie will fade with washing and will feather more when used on fabric. Sharpie Stained is for fabric.

Yes, yes, yes! My fiance was not pleased when he saw I had ordered a shock collar for our mouthy and unruly German Shepherd. We had tried another that only beeped and vibrated that made absolutely no difference so it was time to upgrade. Within a day, there was already a difference in her behavior. In two days, a mega difference. It's now been a few weeks and I very rarely actually have to zap her. She will still give a bark, but it's just a bark, maybe two and then silence. She's eased up on bullying our small dog and the jumping on people has also nearly disappeared. I keep the shock mode on 5 most days, sometimes 6-7 if she's being a real jerk (it goes to 100, I can't even fathom using that). I once accidentally had it at 10 (watch those buttons when it's in your pocket!) and she screamed a god awful scream so I learned my lesson there. Between 5-7 gets her attention and makes her shake her head and back away, anything under and she doesn't notice. I also shaved her throat (not to the skin) to be sure there'd be contact and I check the area regularly to be sure it's not hurting her. Play with it yourself before you strap it to your dog to be sure you know what you're dealing with. I even held it to my neck - almost felt like getting a tattoo. Now that she's significantly more well behaved, I can use the vibrate mode on high and that will get her attention, so YES, buy this one!

For years, I had only heard about the game, how this series was "so good", and so on. But it wasn't until recently that I got to check it out for myself. I was blown away. It looks amazing, and although I've never really enjoyed action/shooting games, this one stands out as a gem. Anyone can pick this up and play it. The story is deep and dramatic, and there's lots to do. Lots of exploration, and one can change the outcome of certain sections of the game. The music is good but not tuneful. It's more of an ambient sound than something you can hum or whistle. There are a few things I did not like, however. I spent a lot of my first playthrough wondering "Where the hell do I go???" It isn't always clear what you are supposed to do. Later, I learned to use the Journal feature to get the details. I'm also relieved that the Codex has information that is not essential reading in order to play the game. That could have been very badly handled, and it wasn't on this occasion. The story feels a little short. When you reach the near-end of the game, look at your missions list in the Journal. You might feel like you didn't really do much that was story-based, but there were a number of distractions and side trips. Also, the Mako is unbelievably bad. It handles like an effing shopping cart, and the worst part is that it's completely not-skippable. You HAVE to learn to drive this tank for many of the assignments and activities or you will not finish the game. Fortunately, it CAN be mastered, though it will take a while to figure out the mechanics of it when the game doesn't give you much opportunity to figure them out before it becomes necessary. I highly recommend this game to anyone looking for sci-fi and space exploration games. This is probably the best one I've ever played, and I will definitely be continuing the trilogy. PS - can I just say how beautiful male Shepard and Kaiden are?

Like it so far but read on. Over the past 10 years I have used many hose end timers. All but one have failed within three years if less. Often much less. The DIG 7001 lasted 10 years but it's newest model is now over $100. I have never tried a Gilmour so here goes. I will report back if or when it fails to let you know how it lasts. It works well. PLUS: Replaces 2 regular timers Not hard to set up Takes AA Batteries and I use rechargeables. Reasonably easy to read display. Timers work independently. Says that it has a memory so that reprogramming is not needed when batteries are changed. I will report back. Has a manual setting where each side can be bade to water for a set time manually. Not both at once. Defaults to 30 min which I like. Good price MINUSES: If it dies while I am away it will destroy both sections of my jungle garden rather than one. Like nearly all of these timers it shuts the water off very suddenly which can cause a "water hammer" effect in the pipes. While this is usually harmless it is often audible. I install a section of hose before the timer to soak up some of the shock. I just make a several feet of hose and then fasten the timer at eye level above the spigot. Makes it easier to read too.

Initially I had a lot trouble with the shipping. I contacted the Vivosun support group and they were extremely helpful and resolved the problem for me. I added the product to LED tube lights that I am using to grow plants. I epoxied four 1" strips of aluminum flashing with a 45 degree bend as outriggers on either side of the fixture and used double sided tape to attached 4" x 48" strips Vivosun reflector material to the outriggers. That recaptures the light that would normally go out sideways from the fixture (the built in reflector only covers half the tube) and makes for a brighter, wider illumination region under the lights making them much more capable. It's a terrific product for that purpose. I can highly recommend it.

This is my second bluetooth adapter (for auto) from SoundBot. The first one used the aux jack. I had issues with the jack itself so quality was temperamental. This one solves that problem and since it's USB powered I can use whatever charger I want. This flexibility means I don't have to sacrifice charging power for my handheld devices. I'm currently using a 5.4a adapter to charge my phone at 3a over USB C and power this FM transmitter via USB A. Another brand I tried temporarily wouldn't even deliver 1a even though it was rated for 2.4a. My one beef with this transmitter is it doesn't seem to have the broadcast power of similar units. I get more interference over the radio and have to change the station occasionally to accommodate. It uses the same magnetic mount as the last unit so I didn't have to install anything new. Overall, I'm happy with it. If there are few free frequencies in your area it may not be the best bet for you.

The Hewlett-Packard HP LaserJet Pro M402n measures 14.5 inches wide, 14 inches long, and 7 3/4 inches tall. The unit is heavy and, while I am not providing any units of weight, I know that it is too heavy for a child under six year old to lift and tote around, but that it is easily manageable by anybody over eight years old. This unit prints only in black and white. It is "wired," which means, it is not "wireless" and thus cannot be operated from your cell phone or from an iPad. The on-button is located on the right-hand corner, and when depressed, there is a kind of a low humming sound (or whirring sound) that lasts for 60 seconds. After that, the unit is totally quiet. I've had this unit for two weeks, and have already printed about 500 pages. When you run out of paper, the unit stops printing, of course. And so, when the unit runs out of paper, you just open the drawer and slide in another batch of paper, and then you push a button located on top (this is the "OK" button). After pushing the "OK" button, the rest of the document gets printed out. In other words, if the unit runs out of paper, it is not the case that the machine gets confused and need to be re-booted, or anything like that. GREAT FOR DETAILED DIAGRAMS AND FOR GRAY SCALE IMAGES. Mainly, I use this printer for articles in biochemistry, molecular biology, and medicine. The way this unit prints a gray scale is excellent. The way this unit prints small pictures of complicated organic molecules is excellent. Regarding small pictures of complicated molecules, one example is the molecules in FIGURE 1 of Seo et al (2005) Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 102:5926-5931. The figures were printed with precision and accuracy. Regarding gray scale, and this is just to provide one of many fine examples, this unit did a great job at printing the sequencing gels shown in FIGURE 2 from Patel and Loeb (2000) J. Biological Chemistry 275:40266-40272. The printing is relatively quick, and when I depress the print button, the unit provides the printed pages -- one! two! three! four! and up to fifty or so pages. Zoom, zoom, zoom, goes the printer. The ink does not smudge, even when I drag my thumb over a freshly printed page. The text is aligned with the edges of the paper, and there is not one hint of crookedness.

I've only used this spreader for Scotts Four-Step products, never for salt spreading, but for the Scotts products it seems to work quite well. the spray pattern is uniform at either 5 ft or 2.5 feet wide and even though the internal revolving agitator is only about an inch wide the flow is steady with only an occasional side to side "shake" to even out the level of product in the hopper. The width control essentially has two positions which it can be set to. "Full" produces a 45 deg left to 45 deg. right spread about 5 ft wide when holding the Wizz Spreader at waist level. The "Narrow" setting blocks the left side only and reduces the flow rate so the spray is constrained to 0 degrees (directly ahead to 45 deg. right). It's handy for narrow strips of grass. This device is definitely intended to be held in your right hand for best control and results. I use it to spray a 2.5 ft. to 5 ft. perimeter around our large lawn and to spread product on our 12 ft parkway between the sidewalk and the street. I then use a larger push-type "broadcast" spreader for the main part of the lawn. This has worked well and if you walk at the prescribed speed of 4 ft. per second the application amount seems to be about correct at the recommended "rate-dial" settings. The electrical drive on this device seems to me to be much more consistent and reliable (for application rate) than any hand-cranked spreader. With the Wizz, the only variable in application rate is how fast you walk. With a hand-cranked model, there are two variables, walking speed and cranking speed, plus the act of hand-cranking can cause spreader to shake and wobble which affects the width and uniformity of the spread pattern. It's easy to spray clean with a garden hose set to fine spray (allow it to fully dry in the sun before storing) and the (4) AA cells seem to provide enough power for a year using it 4 or 5 times on a 2500 sq. ft. perimeter area.

Eheim Classic canister filters or external filters with models: 2211 (150), 2213 (250), 2215 (350) and the 2217 (600). 2211 Flow rate (60gph) Dimensions [4.3x11.4] has an inlet size of 12/16 and outlet of 9/12mm. 2213 Flow rate (116gph) Dimensions [6.3x14] and 2215 Flow rate (164gph) Dimensions [7.3x14.6] have both the 12/16mm on the inlet/suction and the outlet/pressure side. 2217 Flow rate(264gph) Dimensions [8x16] however, has 16/22mm inlet and a 12/16mm outlet. For everyone else information, when properly installed, given the unit has not been damaged through shipping (no chip, no cracks), the unit will not leak!!! *Double check on the washers, one that is between the motor cover and the canister itself and the other one on the that is between the inlet threaded elbow and the bottom of the canister, make sure the washer is placed properly before screwing the inlet threaded elbow. I foster dogs and 2 of the dogs that I fed to, one a chihuahua, another a bigger pitmix dog, got a little diarrhea from consuming these chews. They both ate the same raw diet and when I fed them, half a ring for the chi, and a full ring for the bigger dog, it showed when they went to the bathroom the next day. In a Canadian broadcast news program, CBC Marketplace "Toxic dog treats" they investigated that some dog treats have varying levels of glycerin that make dogs sick to the point of death (forbal esters). Some dogs react more strongly than others, some barely have any affects. None of the pet food companies care if your dog may get sick from the treats nor they may do good quality control. Why do they put a limit on how many rings a dog can have? It's fishy, a dog should be able to consume most of the bag and not get sick. These treats are cheap, but would not recommend. If you want a chew for your dog, you can get bully sticks in small cuts and big cuts depending on what dog you have. Bully sticks are made from the parts of a bull, not a list of ingredients. Just provide some water too because it's very drying to a dog's mouth like kibble. For puppies, you can get soft toys to get them thru the baby to adult teething phase. Please don't get this chew.

TL;DR - keep the batteries warm; listen to your machine; use common sense and a little finesse when clearing and this is a great little snow blower, even for Chicago winters. This isn't a "verified purchase" review because I bought this direct from Greenworks as Amazon didn't have the "no battery" option in stock when I bought it around October of 2017. I live in the suburbs of Chicago, and we get proper winters here. For the past four years, I've gotten by with the Greenworks corded snowblower, but that was a real PITA on my large-ish plot. Keeping the cable out of the way and then winding it all up at the end was finally too much for me, so I bought the 40V cordless equivalent. I already have three 40V Greenworks batteries for the other tools I have, so 40v vs 80v was an easy choice for me. When I first used it, I had the same problem many here are reporting - it would cut out as soon as it came under any load. I had to restart it every three or four minutes. I was bitterly disappointed, and almost wrote the thing off as useless. Then I was reading around about Lithium-iron batteries and discovered that they're somewhat heat sensitive. I'd been keeping my batteries and charger in the unheated (though attached) garage at home, and the blower in a plastic storage trunk in the back yard. It's not unusual for the temperature to hit -20F outside, and although the garage is a bit warmer, it's still on the chilly side... So I moved my batteries and the charger into my heated basement, leaving the blower in its thin plastic trunk, and never had a problem again! As I type this, I've just cleared 7" of wet snow from my double-width, triple-length driveway and the entire sidewalk of a corner plot. I used all three of the batteries (wet, deep snow takes its toll) but got through it without drama in about 60 minutes. The key is not to just go charging into deep snow at full pelt. Take it easy, listen to the machine, and when you're hitting solid ice (like at the end of a driveway on a road that's been plowed), adjust the angle to take smaller bites rather than just trying to force it through. If you listen to the machine, you'll soon tune into the sounds it makes and be able to tell when you need to slow down, adjust the angle, or even just take half-size bites (i.e. 10" wide instead of the full 20"). I'm really pleased that I replaced my wired snow blower, and with nowhere to really store a gas blower - nor the desire to store gas anywhere - my only regret is that I paid attention to too many negative reviews and didn't buy this three years earlier... The handle folds, meaning that it first inside my little Suncast Storage Shed (essentially a small trunk) without any problem at all.

Sea of Thieves LOOKS fun. It takes all the pirate tropes we know and love, wraps them in a cartoonish look and then lets you build your own adventure. On paper, that sounds amazing... Then the reality of the game sets in. So.. the breakdown... First time loading in: First and foremost, the game doesn't explain a single thing to you. You load in (for a really long time... the initial load screen is REALLY long, and rather than providing meaningful tips, just advertises new content) and then... you figure it out. There is no direction to the game, which is both good and bad. There is no primary story to follow, which, depending on your outlook, is also both good and bad as you're left to your own devices. You really can do whatever you want, but there just isn't any compelling reason to do anything. The game points you nowhere, and this is a continued theme even after you've "figured out" the game. NPCs: The NPC interactions are the flattest possible experience, and are relegated to single sentence "speech bubbles" that take awhile to click through, all while the character says nothing. Instead, the NPCs just grunt and huff and generally just make "noises" to intone they could be saying something. Were voice actors really that big of a problem or expense? There are skeletons to fight on the various islands, but that too is a flat experience. Yes there are differing skeleton types with specific weaknesses, but again, its flat. They've added in skeletons carrying powder kegs that often serve as "gotcha" moments you can do nothing about. You just get to die and sit in the "time out" penalty box of a ghost ship. Admittedly, the ghost ship is cool, and you can see other dead pirates arrive there, but it leads you back into another nonsense load screen when you try to rejoin the land of the living. Shared World Experience: Its terrible. I am going to be honest. The addition of Alliances has eased this a bit, but not resolved it. This game caters to the troll crews who have nothing better to do than sink you at the dock just to ruin your day. As a single player, you're in the worst possible position, as you will often run into instances where players will board your ship, kill you, and remain there to keep killing you as you are auto loaded back to your ship after sitting in the penalty box. You can choose to "scuttle" your ship, and reload somewhere else, but this isn't an option that is explained, and is again penalizing the "innocent" player. In the middle of a mission where you've got a handful of chests on board with some skulls or some merchant items and suddenly see some sails on the horizon? Sail the other way. You must waste a ton of your time avoiding other ships because you get nothing for your time unless you turn something in. You get notifications that you've completed the voyages when you've dug or found up the last "thing" but it is all for naught unless you turn everything in. Even with the option for alliances, often times crews have no issues using them opportunistically and turning on you in a heartbeat. (there is literally no downside to an alliance, and nothing but net positives all around, but trolls are trolls) . Sadly, in the name of "pirating" the game caters to the troll players. In order to avoid having wasted hours trying to complete a voyage, you must now waste an hour sailing in a cat and mouse game against people who appear to have nothing but time on their hands to ruin your day. Progression: There are currently a handful of grind wheels where you repeat the same mindless tasks for each "guild type" to slowly make the wheel turn and see the number get bigger. Progression is super slow at the mid point and painfully slow beyond it. Additionally, lets say you go on a spree, complete a few voyages, and go to turn in your bounty (the only way to level up) and you're met at the dock by a troll crew... well... you're boned, hours wasted with no progression. They can sink your ship in seconds while you're turning in 1 chest. Yes, you may have 10 things on your ship... you must run them 1 at a time to the turn in points, making you very vulnerable. A single powder keg detonated in the lower deck while you're turning in will sink your ship, cause your loot to float, and then... if / when you die, you're moved to another island with no recourse. You've lost your chance at progression and have wasted hours of your time. Other players are literally a barrier to continuing, and worse, can impact your willingness to even play the game. Which brings us to sailing... Sailing: Its fun. Not going to lie. The learning curve for it is a little steep for your first voyage, again with nothing to explain anything to you. Positioning your sails for maneuverability is equally as important as positioning your sails to catch the wind. Knowing how to turn tightly and continue with speed will become important... but... often times the game's voyages have you trekking from one side of the map to the other, many times against the wind. As a result, you will spend a LOT of time just pointing off in a direction and waiting to arrive, hopefully without running into other players. Friends: This is the linchpin. The game becomes infinitely more positive with the addition of a single friend. More friends? Bigger ship, more fun. The isn't in the missions themselves, but in getting everyone together to accomplish tasks on the ship while you go about the boring missions. A friend and I recently took down a skeleton Galleon while in our sloop. It was fun and hectic. Sadly, the game isn't built for you to get anything from this. There was no time for us to get loot from sinking that ship as two more instantly spawned and we had run very low on cannonballs. The game offers the option of having an "open" crew, where it will assign you random players to fill out larger ships. Sadly, these crews can be just as much a hinderance as a benefit. Luck never seems to be on my side here. Rewards: The rewards in this game are crap. Did you just complete an amazing mission with some friends? Cool, someone could easily steal the loot and you've literally gained nothing for the time. Did you sink a skeleton ship? So what? People will say that playing the game is the reward, and to a point, I agree. But, getting people together to accomplish "nothing" isn't easy and I would say is counter productive. You're more likely to get people together to do "a mission" or "beat a boss" none of which actually exist in Sea of Thieves. End Game Activities: You need people. Period. These are nothing more than wars of attrition. Go in with enough people and materials and no skill is required. You basically keep repeating actions till eventually the thing dies or the ship sinks... then you repeat. Did you run out of cannonballs during the fight? Welp, you're done. Go home and start over. Oh, want to fill up on cannonballs? Prepare to sail around to islands scavenging. You can't just buy crates of them so you can jump to it. There is as much tedium in the set up as there is in the actual end game. Conclusion? I want to play. I do. I enjoy the sailing, and Alliances are easing the pain of troll crews of preteens. When friends are on, we enjoy sailing around and digging up the chests. We a friend and I sunk a skeleton crew after a long battle while on a sloop it felt like a huge accomplishment. The game doesn't recognize it and thats really the point. The created a sand box with clear edges. The toys are only fun if there are other people to play, and the game pretty much expects you to "roll your own" adventure. The problem with that is there isn't anything to build your adventure out of, and if you do have an accomplishment, it is fleeting and you're done. I've loaded up the game several times, grabbed a chest or two, got bored and just logged out without turning them in because really, friends are fun, the game is not. The game is just a place for friends to be, and doesn't deserve any more credit than an empty room does. There is a ton of places for improvement, and I look forward to giving it more stars should something change but for now... meh.

Security people will tell you not to write down passwords. Sorry! Who can remember dozens of passwords for multiple sites? I can't and I have a great memory. The answer for me was to get a Rolodex business card file. My file box holds pages with a plastic sleeve over the card. Instead of writing on each card and having to scratch out passwords every time I change one I use these to record my passwords. I write the name of the site, the user name and the password on these little Post-Its and slip them in under the plastic sheath. When I change a password, I simply change the Post-It and don't need to order many more cards for the file. They save money and time

Ever since I purchased a PS Vita PCH-2001 (USA/NA) version, I've been eyeing this aqua blue PCH-2000 (Japan) version like a hawk. The last two digits after each PS Vita numbers are region codes; example PCH-2001 is 01 USA code, PCH-2000 is 00 Japan code etc. I'm seeing some reviewers who have the PCH-2006 and little do these consumers know that the product they received from some of these sellers are the Asia 06 Hong Kong/Korea code version. I have no issues with any PS Vita versions, but I like to get what I actually paid for, and in this case I strictly wanted the Japanese aqua blue. You can also tell if it's the Japanese version during your first initial setup of the device, it will default to Tokyo. I took a chance and made a purchase from seller cus mart with FBA (fulfilled by Amazon) of same day delivery. Everything arrived on time and the product came in BRAND NEW SEALED boxed. The PS Vitas in Japan are still in production and they actually do not have clear seals. The seller I purchased from added them as extra security and I'm very grateful. All PS Vitas are region free so I'm able to enjoy all of my new physical and digital games on this device. This device only comes stocked with 1GB internal memory, so I went ahead and purchased a 64gb from the same seller and they did not disappoint. Extremely happy with my new PS Vita, it's an absolute beauty in person! It's hard to describe how stunning the color of this device is as I noticed in different lighting it will either look darker or lighter. The X and O button functions on the Japanese versions are reversed and can easily be changed in the accessibility menu under settings. I recommend just leaving the X and O on default as it's only reversed on the LiveArea screen. Once a USA/NA game starts it defaults to what western gamers are accustomed to, X=confirm, O=cancel/back. You can also login to any PSN region you have, but be aware only one account per PS Vita, blame Sony. If you have multiple PS Vita proprietary memory cards, you can set each memory card with a different PSN, again blame Sony. Other than that, this PCH-2000 version functions just like the USA/NA PCH-2001 version. For those curious, my model version came stocked with 3.65 firmware. If you are looking for firmware below this, it is best to ask the seller of your choice as these are still in production and very popular in Japan.

Aquaponics. A bunch of hippy-dippy bee-ess. Plants are suppose to live with the root system in the water, and feed off the waste produced by the fish. The fish could live off nibbling on plant matter, algae, and all the insects that are attracted by the plants and land on the water. I've seen this at The California State Fair. Systems could be anything from a goldfish with a few sprigs of basil, to giant tanks with sturgeon, pumps, and hydroponic planters & grow lights. For x-dollars, you can start with a 0.5 gallon tank, which is recommended for a beta fish, that has a little planter on top. Suppose to be just right for growing herb. Not cannabis. These are actually sold on Amazon. I figured I could do better. A half gallon tank is suitable for nothing....not even a fighter fish or a guppy. I picked up a little 10 gallon tank from Wally World, and a slotted basket from a 99 Cent store for less than a Jackson. Filled it with water. Added some vegetables. Great. Now all that I need is fish. Got the fish. Got a solar powered air pump which came with little stones. Got AQUANEAT 4pcs Bio Sponge Filter. In no time at all, less than a day, the water got cloudy. Isn't that the opposite of what is suppose to happen? Filters are suppose to clean the water. Not make your water dirty. This is all hippy re-use and recycle. I'm not a farmer. I have an apartment in a skyscraper in The Financial District. The experiment is to regrow produce from kitchen scraps. My bok choy, celery, scallions, basil, and mint all seem to be thriving. The cilantro didn't root. Carrot tops are sprouting carrot greens. Avocado seed have done nothing yet, but a few look like they are about to open at the top, so a root may come out of the bottom. Onion roots look good, but I haven't seen anything sprout from the top yet. The beauty of an aquaponics system is that I really don't have to worry about crowding the plants. They will do just fine right next to each other, with their roots running through the substrate and into the water. On the side, I've got a bunch of bok choy stem sitting in little containers with just regular tap water, and they seem to be doing just as well....sprouting and rooting. I've also got a little planter with a carrot top sprouting carrot greens, a celery, and an onion. Since it is a plastic planter on a patio, hundreds of feet in the air in a highrise building, I toss my banana peels eggshells, and coffee grounds into the potting soil in hopes that it will add nutrients to the soil. So it's hard to tell if a tank of fish water actually helps or not. Now fish mongers will all weigh in with opinions about ammonia, bacteria, nitrites, nitrates,biological load with number of fish in relation to gallons of water, and not to mention that the live plants also contribute to the overall water quality. Since I'm not really interested in raising or breeding fish, cloudy water does not bother me as much. But I have to wonder if the plants will do better with clean water. Just can't believe that the water was clean, until I added the filters. I didn't want to be a quitter. So I added AQUAPAPA Corner Filter Aquarium Fish Tank Internal Air Driven Filter with Media XY-2008. Did it help? No. Water looks just as cloudy to me. Maybe I'll just have to wait for the "magic" to happen. Somewhere along the way, the fish waste will be absorbed by the plants, bacteria will colonize and do whatever it is suppose to do, and the mechanical filtration by the sponges will collect solid material in the water.......then I will have to change the water, clean the filters, and see what happens next. But in the meanwhile, my vegetables are growing. Too bad that in a 10 gallon tank, sitting outside on the patio, I can't raise any fish that are edible. The dream would be to have a swimming pool full of trout, bluegill, catfish, crappie, and bass. So that I can just sit in my backyard and sportfish from a lawn chair. And all that fish water could raise enough crops to feed me all year long. That would really mess with my neighbors in The City.

This product is the most cost effective means to address chlorine/chloramine. A little goes a very long way; potent stuff. Not suitable for tanks less than 30 gallons as it may prove difficult to calculate and measure the correct dosage. Keep in mind that you shouldnt create a premix out of the powder as it lacks the preservatives and stabilizers found in Seachems Prime product. Per the instructions for dosing, when adding to replacement water you dose for just the replacement water. When adding directly to the tank/pond, it is recommended to add enough to treat the entire tank volume. Lastly, I hughly recommend purchasing New Star Foodservice 42924 Stainless Steel Measuring Spoons Set, Mini to get more accurate measurements as it is very hard to tell if using a small medicine cup or anything similar. Hope the info helps!

I purchased this Kuru Toga four months ago, after a lot of research on mechanical pencils. The pencil has had moderate use over the last four months. I used the lead that came in it, and then added two more sticks of the Pilot Neox lead of the same exact size (0.5). I prefer this lead over what comes with the pencil, as it is creamier than the Kuru lead. I have another one of these pencils, and have never had an issue with switching brands of lead in the pencil. Today, I started noticing that the lead starts retracting when I put any pressure on the tip. I thought perhaps it had something to do with a bad batch of lead, or the fact that the Pilot lead seems softer than the Kuru. Because of this, I then swapped the lead out for some Pentel lead. The same problem happened. Finally, I went back to the Kuru lead and again, the lead is retracting. That leads me to believe it is not a lead issue, but rather a pencil issue. Disappointed for the price point, and how little the pencil has been used. I will say, that it does keep a sharp point on the pencil, which is great because I am using to do math calculations that need be written very small and precise, so that helps a lot. The tip itself however, is not very comfortable.

Despite being treated with Advantix year-round (and getting the Lyme vaccine annually, though that's a different story), my poor Newfoundland still got Lyme disease at 5-years-old. I had only ever found about 3 ticks on him before, but he had previously had problems with fleas. In the two months following his Lyme diagnosis and before I switched away from Advantix, I found multiple very swollen ticks on him. I don't know if the product is getting weaker, if ticks are becoming immune to it, if prolonged use on a dog can make it less effective, or if I just happened to go into extra protective dog mom mode once my baby became sick. Either way, Advantix didn't work very well for my 160-lb Newfoundland (not even when he was just a puppy in a smaller weight category and got fleas for the first time). At one point, I actually called and spoke to Bayer. I specifically asked about using the product on substantially larger dogs. The representative, while very friendly and helpful, advised that the product can safely be used more often than once a month. I asked if using it more frequently is required, and she confirmed that it is NOT. There's obviously a reason that the company recommends dosing more frequently for heavier dogs, but it would be great if they could clearly tell pet owners, "Hey, expect poor results unless you want to pay extra money treating every week, and then still you should probably expect poor results." Better yet - put a weight limit on the box instead of calling it "55+" or just make a stronger dose for giant breed dog owners. My thinking, though, is that even poor protection is better than none at all. Maybe if Advantix were cheaper I'd give it 3 stars. At least my dog stayed Lyme-free for 5 years?

PROS: I was looking to switch from pine litter over to another product because I moved into a small apartment and was hoping it would help even further reduce the amount of litter that is tracked by my cat. And it does so very nicely. I have no issues whatsoever. These litter pellets are very helpful as they don't break down - they whisk the moisture away, they repel it. They don't stay wet for long either and don't smell. I also found out this way that my cat has a urinary problem (he is 15 years old) because the urine is collected on a white "diaper" aka wee wee pad below. I have no idea for how long he had this problem which was invisible while I was using the pine litter. I am glad for the Breeze litter system as it allows me to monitor my cat's condition. CONS - using Breeze/Amazon wee wee pads: I don't like the amount of garbage I create by using the pads that have a plastic backing and under different circumstances would have changed back to a pine product, which I find tracks the least (I used it for four cats in my house). I am changing the pad every day. I cannot imagine using them for longer than two days for my single cat. But since my cat's circumstance may not lend itself as a good comparison, please check other reviews for how long the Breeze/Amazon pads last.

This is the third portable unit I've purchased in the past 10 years and the first to have a zipped bottom, I really liked that. The previous two units I had used a velcro bottom that just never stayed in place after a month or so of use. The doors are also closed with a zipper like all the rest and thats generally the second thing that goes wrong with this type of kennel and the most important to me. They last about 1 to 2 years then break or get jammed so we turn the unit with one door to the wall and the other facing out. When one door goes bad you can just turn the pen and use the other door. We use the portable to keep our two small dogs penned up at night otherwise they create a little havoc snooping around. We also take it with us when ever we travel with them, it just makes life easier and they have something thats familiar to them. Both dogs use the pen and weigh about 20lbs. each they have more then enough room to sleep and stretch out in. The screens around the sides are nice so you can just glance at it to check if the dogs are in the pen and provides more ventilation so thats another plus on this unit over the past ones. Our last pen had a full screen top and this one is a third canvas and two thirds screen so that would be a problem when we need to turn it to use the other door. I kept the zipper top from the old unit its all screen and amazingly it zips right in to the new unit, so that won't be a problem for me at least.

I purchased these for my home in Autumn of 2017 because evidence of mice was getting worse, in the kitchen and basement. I plugged these in, and that day cleaned all evidence of mice that I could find - including the drawer at the bottom of the stove. Since then I have not seen ANY evidence of mouse intrusion in my home - not a single mouse dropping anywhere!!! I only use 4 - 2 on the first floor and 2 in the basement. Fantastic!!! To the people still experiencing problems, perhaps placement is the issue? I put one from the back wall of my house facing front (which is in the kitchen), and one on the front wall facing back, (which is the living room). Same configuration in the basement. That's all it took.

The large version was small for my 11 pound cat. Its narrow towards the back and doesnt have a lot of ventilation. Be clear - it is ventillated so the pet can breathe - but seems like it would be hot in warm weather. It is well constructed and easy to carry with the cat inside. It is soft but sturdy. My cat was able to turn around inside. The fabric seems to be water repellent. The bottom has feet so the soft carrier does not sit completely on the ground- just in the middle which keeps it from tipping over. It collapses to about an inch thick if a weight is sitting on it. It seems less threatening to my geriatric cat than the hard sided kennel. The shoulder strap is strong and sturdy. I did not hear any seams popping when carrying my cat. I wish I had bought it sooner. I have had my hard sided carriers for almost 30 years and am upgrading my crates because the hard sided crates are heavy and bulky. With this carrier I can either hand carry or sling it over a shoulder and leave. The carrier all black interior seems like a cave and my cat likes to hide. She also likes to be warm. She likes soft things. I hope this carrier works as well for you as it has for me.

Swingline is a leading maker of shredding machines. Generally speaking there are two main types of shredders - long strips and cross cut. Cross cut machines are a bit more expensive but they offer better security by cutting horizontally and vertically, making is much harder for identity thieves to reconstruct documents. The smaller the final pieces, the more secure. This Swingline cross cutting machine tears through up to 14 sheets at a time. That means less time spent clearing jams or sitting there feeding small quantities of paper through at a time. The collection bin is huge, which means less time spent emptying the machine. I found the included shredder bags to be a perfect fit for the bin. Since the bag too is shredable it makes recycling a breeze.

I highly recommend you NOT buy this product from Amazon. My cat loved the Fortiflora that I purchased from the vet. Then I bought it from Amazon and added it to the food. Yes, the negative reviews are correct. This is not a genuine OEM Husqvarna belt as listed, but the length of the belt is clearly stated, so why all the negative reviews that the belt was too short or too long. Did you receive a belt that was a different length or did you order without measuring your old belt?? I purchased this same belt (Made in India) from another vendor for a few bucks less for my Craftsman 917.288562 42" lawn tractor. The old drive belt is 1/4" thick x 91.75" long. This belt is 1/2" thick x 92.5" long. So far the mower runs ok. The increase in thickness makes up for the increase in length. The specs for a new Craftsman 130969 42" Tractor Primary Drive Belt, sold by Sears, are 0.5" thick x 92.5" length. I found on my mower that the easiest way to uninstall and install the belt is to start from the front. On a Craftsman there should be a sticker on the bottom left side that shows you how to route the belt ( see pic). Install it over the front steering plate, from the bottom slip it over the large front blade pulley but do not install it on the engine pulley. Pull the slack out towards the back and install it over the transmission cooling fan and onto the transaxle pulley. Pull the slack towards the front and install it on the engine pulley, then follow the routing guide and install it on the idler pulleys.
